paragraphs: Year 8 - Unit 1 Grammar Reference
Languages and Communication

This file is your complete grammar reference for Unit 1. It covers seven grammar topics in depth, with clear explanations, examples, practice exercises, and answer keys. Study each section carefully and complete the exercises. If you need help, ask your AI tutor.
Topic 1: Although / While / Whereas
What Are Contrast Conjunctions?

Although, while, and whereas are called contrast conjunctions. They connect two clauses (parts of a sentence) that express different or surprising ideas. They are essential tools for showing that two things can be true at the same time even when they seem to conflict.
How to Use Although

Although introduces a clause that contains a surprising or unexpected fact. The main clause contains the key information. You can place the although-clause at the beginning or the end of the sentence.
Structure: Although + subject + verb, main clause. OR Main clause + although + subject + verb.
Examples:
Although it was raining, the students played outside.
She passed the exam although she had not studied much.
Although the rumour was plausible, Hamza did not believe it.
The team won the match although they had fewer players.
Notice the comma when the although-clause comes first. When it comes at the end, no comma is needed.
How to Use While
While can show contrast between two things that are simultaneously true. Be careful -- "while" can also mean "during the time that," so context matters.
Structure: While + subject + verb, main clause.
Examples:
While Hamza preferred careful thinking, Leyla wanted to act immediately.
While some students enjoyed the debate, others found it challenging.
While reading is a quiet activity, debating is loud and energetic.
While the city was modern, many traditional customs remained.
How to Use Whereas
Whereas is more formal and draws a direct comparison between two different subjects or situations. It usually appears in the middle of the sentence.
Structure: Clause 1, whereas clause 2.
Examples:
Mogadishu is a coastal city, whereas Hargeisa is inland.
Hamza was cautious, whereas Leyla was bold.
Traditional news is often verified, whereas social media posts may not be checked.
Some students showed zeal, whereas others were indifferent.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Forgetting the comma: When although or while begins the sentence, always place a comma before the main clause. Wrong: Although it was late we stayed up. Correct: Although it was late, we stayed up.
Using although and but together: Do not combine them. Wrong: Although it was cold, but we went out. Correct: Although it was cold, we went out.
Confusing while (contrast) with while (time): "While I was eating" means during the time. "While I like chocolate" means in contrast. Think about whether the sentence shows a time or a difference.
Using whereas at the start of a sentence: Whereas usually works best in the middle. Unusual: Whereas I like maths, my sister likes art. Better: I like maths, whereas my sister likes art.
Memory Tip
💡 Quick Memory Trick
Although = a door to a surprise (something unexpected follows).
While = two paths running side by side (two different things at the same time).
Whereas = a spotlight on differences (directly comparing two things).
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Fill in the blank with although, while, or whereas.
__________ the exam was easy, many students still made mistakes.
__________ I enjoy football, my friend prefers basketball.
Cats are independent, __________ dogs need more attention.
__________ it was very noisy outside, she managed to concentrate.
He speaks three languages, __________ his brother speaks only one.
__________ both teams played well, only one could win.
Exercise B: Combine each pair of sentences using the word in brackets.
It was a holiday. The shops were closed. (although -- hint: something unexpected)
Amina likes Science. Farah prefers English. (whereas)
The weather was hot. The students wore jackets. (although)
Some people trust social media. Others prefer newspapers. (while)
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. Although  2. While  3. whereas  4. Although  5. whereas  6. Although/While

Exercise B: 1. Although it was a holiday, the shops were closed.  2. Amina likes Science, whereas Farah prefers English.  3. Although the weather was hot, the students wore jackets.  4. While some people trust social media, others prefer newspapers.
Topic 2: -ing Forms as Subjects and Objects
What Are Gerunds?
When you add -ing to a verb and use it as a noun, it is called a gerund. Gerunds can be the subject of a sentence (what the sentence is about) or the object (what receives the action).
As a Subject
When the -ing form is the subject, it comes at the beginning of the sentence and tells you what the sentence is about.
Examples:
Reading improves your vocabulary.
Swimming is a great form of exercise.
Citing evidence makes your argument stronger.
Thinking critically is an essential life skill.
As an Object
When the -ing form is the object, it comes after certain verbs (enjoy, like, love, hate, avoid, finish, practise, suggest, etc.).
Examples:
I enjoy reading before bed.
She loves dancing in the rain.
He avoids quibbling about small details.
They finished studying for the test.
Gerund vs. Present Continuous
Do not confuse gerunds with the present continuous tense. In the present continuous, the -ing form is part of a verb phrase with "am/is/are." In a gerund, the -ing form works as a noun.
Present continuous: She is reading a book. (describes an action happening now)
Gerund: Reading is her favourite hobby. (the activity itself is the subject)
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Decide whether the -ing word is a gerund (subject or object) or part of the present continuous.
Jogging every morning keeps me healthy.
They are preparing for the debate.
I dislike waiting in long queues.
Learning new words is exciting.
She is writing an essay.
He practises speaking English every day.
Exercise B: Complete each sentence with the -ing form of the verb in brackets.
__________ (cook) is a useful life skill.
I enjoy __________ (listen) to podcasts in English.
__________ (spread) rumours can destroy friendships.
She avoids __________ (argue) about politics.
__________ (assimilate) into a new culture takes time.
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. Gerund (subject)  2. Present continuous  3. Gerund (object)  4. Gerund (subject)  5. Present continuous  6. Gerund (object)
Exercise B: 1. Cooking  2. listening  3. Spreading  4. arguing  5. Assimilating
Topic 3: Present Continuous Tense
What Is the Present Continuous?
The present continuous tense describes actions that are happening right now or are currently in progress. It creates a feeling of immediacy and movement.
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing
Forming the Present Continuous
Spelling Rules for Adding -ing
Most verbs: add -ing directly. talk --> talking, read --> reading
Verbs ending in -e: drop the e, add -ing. make --> making, argue --> arguing
Short verbs ending in consonant-vowel-consonant (CVC): double the last consonant. run --> running, sit --> sitting
Verbs ending in -ie: change ie to y, add -ing. die --> dying, lie --> lying
Examples in Context
The students are whispering in the corridors.
Leyla is reading the flyer carefully.
People are shouting slogans at the protest.
Hamza is thinking about whether the rumour is true.
The forum members are learning to question information.
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Rewrite each sentence in the present continuous.
She writes in her journal. -->
They discuss the problem. -->
He plans the next meeting. -->
We learn new vocabulary. -->
The teacher explains the rule. -->
Exercise B: Correct the mistakes in these sentences.
She is argueing with her friend.
They studying for the exam.
He am reading a book.
We is discussing the topic.
I are writing an essay.
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. She is writing in her journal.  2. They are discussing the problem.  3. He is planning the next meeting.  4. We are learning new vocabulary.  5. The teacher is explaining the rule.
Exercise B: 1. She is arguing with her friend.  2. They are studying for the exam.  3. He is reading a book.  4. We are discussing the topic.  5. I am writing an essay.
Topic 4: Abstract Nouns
What Are Abstract Nouns?

Abstract nouns name things you cannot see, touch, or measure with your five senses. They represent ideas, qualities, emotions, and states of being. Examples: love, happiness, courage, freedom, and from this unit -- serenity, foresight, dissent, zeal, defiance, revelation, and longevity.
How Abstract Nouns Are Formed
Many abstract nouns come from adjectives or verbs:
Using Abstract Nouns in Sentences
Pair abstract nouns with appropriate verbs. You feel serenity, show foresight, express dissent, and demonstrate zeal. Avoid treating them as physical objects.
Good: She felt a deep serenity watching the sunset.
Awkward: She held the serenity in her hands. (Serenity is not something you can hold.)
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Identify the abstract noun in each sentence.
His foresight in saving money helped the family during the crisis.
The student showed great zeal when presenting her project.
There was growing dissent among the students about the new policy.
After the argument, a sense of serenity returned to the classroom.
The revelation shocked the entire school.
Exercise B: Fill in the blank with the correct abstract noun (serenity, foresight, defiance, zeal, revelation, dissent).
She approached her studies with great __________ and never missed a deadline.
The __________ that the flyer was a hoax surprised everyone.
His __________ in planning ahead saved the project from failure.
The community expressed their __________ against the unfair decision.
After a stressful week, she found __________ in reading by the ocean.
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. foresight  2. zeal  3. dissent  4. serenity  5. revelation
Exercise B: 1. zeal  2. revelation  3. foresight  4. defiance/dissent  5. serenity
Topic 5: Reported Speech
What Is Reported Speech?
Reported speech (indirect speech) lets you tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. You change the tense, pronouns, and time expressions to fit the reporting context.
Tense Changes in Reported Speech
Pronoun and Time Changes
When you convert to reported speech, adjust pronouns and time expressions:
I --> he/she, we --> they, my --> his/her, our --> their
today --> that day, tomorrow --> the next day, yesterday --> the day before
here --> there, this --> that, now --> then
Reporting Statements, Questions, and Commands
Statements: He said, "I am happy." --> He said that he was happy.
Questions: She asked, "Are you ready?" --> She asked if I was ready.
Questions with question words: He asked, "Where is the meeting?" --> He asked where the meeting was.
Commands: She said, "Close the door." --> She told me to close the door.
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Convert each sentence to reported speech.
Hamza said, "I do not believe the rumour."
Leyla said, "My cousin sent me a flyer."
Mr. Farah asked, "Who wrote the flyer?"
Nadifa said, "I will check the facts before I share anything."
Yusuf said, "I joined the protest out of boredom."
Halima asked, "Are you coming to the forum meeting?"
Exercise B: Convert each reported speech sentence back to direct speech.
She said that she was feeling proud of the forum.
He told me that he would attend the next meeting.
The teacher asked if we had finished reading the story.
Answer Key

Exercise A: 1. Hamza said that he did not believe the rumour.  2. Leyla said that her cousin had sent her a flyer.  3. Mr. Farah asked who had written the flyer.  4. Nadifa said that she would check the facts before she shared anything.  5. Yusuf said that he had joined the protest out of boredom.  6. Halima asked if I/we was/were coming to the forum meeting.
Exercise B: 1. She said, "I am feeling proud of the forum."  2. He said, "I will attend the next meeting."  3. The teacher asked, "Have you finished reading the story?"
Topic 6: Zero and First Conditionals
Zero Conditional: Facts and General Truths
The zero conditional is used for situations that are always true -- scientific facts, general rules, and universal truths.
Structure: If + present simple, present simple.
Examples:
If you heat ice, it melts.
If you mix red and blue, you get purple.
If people spread rumours, trust decreases.
If a student studies consistently, their grades improve.
First Conditional: Real Future Possibilities
The first conditional is used for situations that are possible or likely in the future.
Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb.
Examples:
If it rains tomorrow, we will stay inside.
If Hamza checks the source, he will discover the truth.
If you study hard, you will pass the exam.
If the forum meets next week, thirty students will attend.
Key Differences
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Identify each sentence as zero or first conditional.
If you do not water plants, they die.
If she finishes early, she will help us.
If you press this button, the machine starts.
If we fact-check the news, we will avoid spreading lies.
If it snows, the roads become slippery.
Exercise B: Complete each sentence with the correct verb form.
If you __________ (not check) the facts, you __________ (believe) lies. (zero)
If Leyla __________ (attend) the meeting, she __________ (share) her ideas. (first)
If students __________ (think) critically, they __________ (make) better decisions. (zero)
If the rain __________ (stop), we __________ (go) to the park. (first)
Answer Key
Exercise A: 1. Zero  2. First  3. Zero  4. First  5. Zero
Exercise B: 1. do not check / believe  2. attends / will share  3. think / make  4. stops / will go
Topic 7: Connectives and Multi-Word Verbs
Connectives
Connectives link ideas and show the relationship between them. Here are the five key connectives for this unit:
Multi-Word Verbs (Phrasal Verbs)
Multi-word verbs combine a verb with a preposition or adverb to create a new meaning:
Practice Exercises
Exercise A: Join the sentences using the connective in brackets.
She was tired. She stayed up to study. (although)
He checked the facts. He could give an accurate report. (so that)
The evidence was clear. The committee changed its decision. (therefore)
The protest seemed real. It was actually a hoax. (however)
Students joined the forum. They wanted to learn critical thinking. (because)
Exercise B: Complete each sentence with give in, carry out, or speak up.
If you disagree with something, you should __________.
The students will __________ a survey on misinformation.
After hours of discussion, the committee decided to __________ to the students' demands.
Please __________ so that everyone at the back can hear you.
The team was asked to __________ the plan exactly as discussed.
Answer Key

Exercise A: 1. Although she was tired, she stayed up to study.  2. He checked the facts so that he could give an accurate report.  3. The evidence was clear; therefore, the committee changed its decision.  4. The protest seemed real; however, it was actually a hoax.  5. Students joined the forum because they wanted to learn critical thinking.
Exercise B: 1. speak up  2. carry out  3. give in  4. speak up  5. carry out
🎯 Well Done!
You have completed the full Grammar Reference for Unit 1.
Go back and review any sections where you made mistakes.
Ask your AI tutor for extra practice on topics that feel challenging.
Use these grammar structures in your writing and speaking tasks!
tables: If you heat water, it boils.
If it rains, I will bring an umbrella.
I stayed home because I was ill.
She studied hard so that she could pass.
Although it rained, we went outside.
I was tired; however, I finished my work.
He lied; therefore, he lost our trust.
He finally gave in and apologised.
Do not be afraid to speak up.

paragraphs: Year 8 - Unit 1: Languages and Communication
Lesson Hub - Whispers Beyond the Gate
Overview

Welcome to Unit 1 of your Year 8 English course at Ehel Academy. In this unit you will explore the powerful theme of languages and communication -- how people share ideas, how rumours spread, and how the truth can be lost or found depending on how carefully we listen and speak. You will read a compelling story set in Mogadishu, learn important grammar structures, build a rich vocabulary, and practise all four language skills: reading, listening, speaking, and writing.

This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through the material at your own speed, with the support of your AI tutor available around the clock and your teacher sessions twice a week. Every section includes activities, tips, and prompts to help you deepen your understanding. By the end of this unit you will be a more confident, thoughtful communicator -- someone who questions what they hear, chooses words with care, and speaks with purpose.

The story at the heart of this unit, "Whispers Beyond the Gate," follows a group of students in Mogadishu who discover how quickly misinformation can spread through a community and what happens when people act on rumour rather than fact. As you study this story, you will encounter the vocabulary words from your word list used in real contexts, and you will see the grammar patterns you are learning woven naturally into the narrative.

Throughout this lesson hub you will find "Ask Your AI Tutor" prompts. These are suggestions for questions you can ask your AI tutor at any time. Think of the tutor as a patient, knowledgeable study partner who is always ready to explain, give examples, or quiz you. Do not be shy about using it -- the more you interact, the faster you will learn.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you should be able to:
Use the contrast conjunctions although, while, and whereas accurately in spoken and written English.
Recognise and use -ing forms (gerunds) as subjects and objects in sentences.
Form and use the present continuous tense to describe actions happening now or in progress.
Identify and use abstract nouns such as serenity, foresight, dissent, and zeal in appropriate contexts.
Convert direct speech into reported speech, adjusting tense, pronouns, and time expressions correctly.
Construct zero and first conditional sentences to express facts and future possibilities.
Use connectives (because, so that, although, however, therefore) to link ideas logically.
Understand and use multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) such as give in, carry out, and speak up.
Read a full-length story critically, identifying themes, character development, and moral lessons.
Expand your vocabulary by learning and practising 33 new words from the Unit 1 word list.
Write a structured paragraph or short essay that uses contrast conjunctions and abstract nouns.
Speak about social issues with clarity, supporting your opinions with evidence.
Apply critical thinking to evaluate information and distinguish fact from rumour.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Which of these learning objectives do you find most interesting or challenging?
Ask your AI tutor: Can you explain one of the objectives in simpler words?
Try: What does it mean to use -ing forms as subjects?
Vocabulary Preview

Before you dive into the reading and grammar, take some time to preview the key vocabulary for this unit. These are words you will encounter repeatedly in the story, grammar exercises, and writing tasks. Familiarising yourself with them now will make everything that follows easier to understand.
Core Vocabulary Groups
Your Unit 1 vocabulary list contains 33 words organised into groups by part of speech. Here is a quick overview of the groups and some highlighted words from each:
Nouns: barricade, zeal, definition, foresight, defiance, dissent, serenity, revelation, longevity, squalor, embargo, quibble

These nouns include several abstract nouns -- words for ideas, feelings, and qualities you cannot physically touch. Words like zeal (strong enthusiasm), serenity (calmness and peace), and foresight (the ability to plan for the future) are especially important in this unit because they appear in both the story and the grammar lessons on abstract nouns.
Verbs: allot, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite

These verbs describe mental and social actions. For example, to assimilate means to absorb and understand new information, and to cite means to refer to a source of evidence. You will use these verbs in your writing and speaking tasks.
Adjectives: concise, temperate, plausible, rueful, pensive, impending, shrewd, gullible, spontaneous, conscientious, bizarre, pervasive, conventional

These adjectives will help you describe people, situations, and feelings with precision. Notice how some are positive (conscientious, shrewd), some are negative (gullible, bizarre), and some are neutral (conventional, temperate). Understanding these shades of meaning will make your descriptions richer and more accurate.
How to Study Vocabulary Effectively
Read the definition in your Vocabulary file and make sure you understand it in your own words.
Read the example sentence to see how the word is used naturally.
Write your own sentence using the word. Try to connect it to your own life or the story.
Say the word aloud several times to get comfortable with the pronunciation.
Test yourself by covering the definition and trying to recall it from memory.
Use the word in conversation with classmates, family, or your AI tutor during the week.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you give me three more example sentences using the word foresight?
What is the difference between shrewd and conscientious?
Can you quiz me on this week's vocabulary?
Grammar Introduction

This unit covers seven grammar topics. The primary focus is on contrast conjunctions -- although, while, and whereas -- which help you show differences and unexpected connections between ideas. You will also study five additional grammar areas that work together with these conjunctions to make your English more expressive and precise.
1. Although / While / Whereas

These three words are called contrast conjunctions because they connect two ideas that are different from each other or that seem surprising when placed together. Learning to use them well is one of the most important skills in this unit.

Although introduces a surprising or unexpected fact. It signals that what follows might seem to contradict the main idea, but both are true. For example: Although the morning was calm, something felt strange. Here, you would expect a calm morning to feel normal, but the writer tells you it did not.

While can show contrast between two things that happen at the same time or that are true at the same time. For example: While Hamza preferred careful thinking, Leyla was ready to act immediately. Both things are true at once, and the writer wants you to notice the difference.

Whereas is more formal and is used specifically to highlight a direct comparison between two different things. For example: Cats are independent, whereas dogs are loyal. The structure draws a clear line between the two subjects.

A key point to remember: when although or while comes at the beginning of a sentence, you need a comma before the main clause. When whereas joins two clauses, it usually appears in the middle of the sentence with a comma before it.
2. -ing Forms as Subjects and Objects

You already know the -ing form from the present continuous tense (I am running). But -ing forms can also work as nouns -- they can be the subject or the object of a sentence. When used this way, they are called gerunds.
As a subject: Reading is a wonderful habit. Here, "reading" is the thing you are talking about. It acts like a noun.
As an object: I enjoy swimming. Here, "swimming" is the thing you enjoy. It comes after the verb and acts as the object.
This pattern is very common in English and appears throughout the unit story. Recognising gerunds will help you understand complex sentences and use them in your own writing.
3. Present Continuous Tense
The present continuous tense describes actions that are happening right now or are in progress. It is formed with the verb to be (am, is, are) plus the -ing form of the main verb.
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing
Examples: She is studying for her exam. They are discussing the news. I am writing an essay.

In the story, you will see present continuous used to bring scenes to life: students are whispering in the corridors, people are shouting at the protest, and the forum members are learning to think critically. Notice how this tense creates a feeling of immediacy -- you can almost see the action unfolding.
4. Abstract Nouns

Abstract nouns name things you cannot see, touch, or measure -- ideas, qualities, emotions, and states. Words like serenity (peacefulness), zeal (enthusiasm), foresight (planning ahead), and dissent (disagreement) are all abstract nouns.

Understanding abstract nouns matters because they allow you to express deep and complex ideas. Instead of saying "he planned ahead," you can say "he showed great foresight." Instead of saying "she was very enthusiastic," you can write "her zeal was inspiring." This makes your writing more sophisticated and precise.

Many abstract nouns are formed from adjectives or verbs. For instance: calm becomes serenity, defy becomes defiance, and reveal becomes revelation. Learning to spot these patterns will help you build your vocabulary quickly.
5. Reported Speech

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) lets you tell someone what another person said without using their exact words. You change the tense, pronouns, and time expressions to fit the new sentence.
Direct speech: Leyla said, "I am going to the protest."
Reported speech: Leyla said that she was going to the protest.

Notice three changes: the pronoun "I" became "she"; the present continuous "am going" shifted back to past continuous "was going"; and the quotation marks disappeared. These shifts follow clear rules that you will practise in your Grammar file.
Reported speech is very common in storytelling, journalism, and everyday conversation. Whenever you retell something someone said, you are using reported speech.
6. Zero and First Conditionals

Conditionals express if-then relationships. The zero conditional is for things that are always true (facts and general rules). The first conditional is for things that are possible or likely in the future.
Zero conditional: If + present simple, present simple. Example: If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils. This is a scientific fact -- it is always true.
First conditional: If + present simple, will + base verb. Example: If we check the facts first, we will make better decisions. This is a real possibility in the future.

In the story, characters use conditional logic when they reason about consequences: "If we join a lie, we are not helping -- we are hurting." Recognising conditionals in text will sharpen your critical reading skills.
7. Connectives and Multi-Word Verbs

Connectives are words that join ideas and show how they relate: because (reason), so that (purpose), although (contrast), however (unexpected change), and therefore (conclusion). Using connectives makes your writing flow smoothly and logically.

Multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) combine a verb with a preposition or adverb to create a new meaning: give in (stop resisting), carry out (complete a task), and speak up (express your opinion). These are very common in English and appear often in the story.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between although and whereas with three examples?
Give me a mini-quiz on reported speech.
What is the difference between a zero conditional and a first conditional?
Reading and Listening
The centrepiece of this unit is the story "Whispers Beyond the Gate." You will find the full text in your separate Story file. Below is guidance on how to approach your reading for maximum learning.
Before You Read
Before opening the story, take a few minutes to activate your thinking. Answer these questions in your notebook or discuss them with your AI tutor:
What does the phrase "whispers beyond the gate" make you think of? What kind of story might have this title?
Have you ever heard a rumour at school that turned out to be false? What happened?
Why is it important to check facts before sharing information with others?
What do you think the word embargo means? Have you heard it used in the news?
While You Read
As you read the story, keep a notebook nearby and do the following:
Underline or highlight every vocabulary word from the Unit 1 list that you spot in the text.
Circle any sentences that use although, while, or whereas.
Put a star next to moments where a character shows foresight, zeal, or defiance.
Write a brief note at the end of each section summarising what happened in your own words.
Notice the grammar patterns: look for present continuous, reported speech, conditionals, and gerunds.
After You Read
Once you have finished the story, answer the following comprehension questions in your notebook. Write in complete sentences.
What is the main conflict in the story? Describe it in two or three sentences.
How does the rumour about the embargo begin, and why do so many students believe it?
What role does social media play in spreading the misinformation? Give specific examples from the text.
How does Hamza's attitude change from the beginning of the story to the end?
What is the Mogadishu Student Forum, and why is it created?
The story says, "Truth needs no volume -- just courage, care, and questioning minds." What does this mean to you?
Find two examples of abstract nouns in the story and explain why the author chose them.
Identify one sentence that uses a first conditional and explain its meaning.
How does the story's ending show that real change has happened at the school?
Listening Practice
To develop your listening skills alongside your reading, try these activities:
Read aloud: Choose two or three paragraphs from the story and read them out loud. Focus on clear pronunciation and natural pausing at commas and full stops.
Record yourself: Use a phone or computer to record yourself reading a passage. Listen back and note any words you stumbled on. Practise those words until you are comfortable.
Dictation with your AI tutor: Ask your AI tutor to dictate sentences from the story. Write them down and then check your spelling and punctuation.
Pair reading: If you have a study partner, take turns reading sections of the story to each other. The listener should summarise what they heard.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you dictate five sentences from the story for me to write down?
I want to practise pronunciation -- can you give me the hardest words from the story to say aloud?
Can you ask me comprehension questions about the story?
Speaking

Strong communication is not just about writing well -- it is about speaking with confidence, clarity, and respect for your listener. This section gives you structured speaking activities to practise the language from this unit.
Activity 1: Contrast Debate
Choose one of the following topics and prepare a two-minute speech in which you use at least two contrast conjunctions (although, while, whereas):
Social media is helpful for learning, although it can also spread false information.
Traditional news sources are reliable, whereas online posts are often unverified.
Spontaneous protests can raise awareness, while planned actions are usually more effective.

Structure your speech like this: Opening statement (state your position), Two supporting points (use contrast conjunctions to acknowledge the other side), Conclusion (restate your view with a strong closing sentence). Practise with your AI tutor or a classmate.
Activity 2: Retelling in Reported Speech

Choose a scene from the story and retell it using reported speech. For example, instead of quoting Leyla directly, say: "Leyla told Hamza that there was going to be a protest. She explained that her cousin had sent a flyer."
Practise retelling at least three scenes. Focus on changing the tense and pronouns correctly. This is an excellent way to internalise reported speech before you use it in writing.
Activity 3: Vocabulary Challenge

Take five vocabulary words from the unit list and use each one in a spoken sentence that relates to your own life, your school, or your community. Record yourself or practise with a partner. After each sentence, explain what the word means in your own words.
For example: "My grandmother showed great foresight when she saved money for our education, even before I was born. Foresight means the ability to think about and plan for the future."
Activity 4: Discussion Circle

If you are working with classmates or during your teacher session, discuss the following questions. Remember to use full sentences, support your ideas with reasons, and listen respectfully when others speak:
Is it ever right to join a protest if you are not completely sure about the facts? Why or why not?
How can young people tell the difference between real news and misinformation?
The story shows that silence can sometimes be a strength. Do you agree? Give an example.
What responsibilities do social media users have when they share information online?
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that social media does more harm than good.
Listen to my retelling and tell me if I used reported speech correctly.
Give me feedback on my pronunciation of these five vocabulary words.
Writing

Writing is where you bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, and ideas -- into a finished piece. This section guides you through structured writing tasks that build from simple sentences to a full paragraph or short essay.
Warm-Up: Sentence Building
Start by writing individual sentences using the grammar patterns from this unit. Complete at least ten of the following:
Write a sentence using although to contrast two ideas about school life.
Write a sentence using while to compare two people's opinions.
Write a sentence using whereas to show a difference between two places.
Write a sentence with a gerund as the subject (e.g., Reading improves your vocabulary).
Write a sentence with a gerund as the object (e.g., I enjoy studying languages).
Write a sentence in the present continuous describing something happening at your school right now.
Write a sentence that includes the abstract noun serenity or foresight.
Convert this direct speech to reported speech: Hassan said, "I will check the source before I share it."
Write a zero conditional sentence about a fact related to language or communication.
Write a first conditional sentence about something that might happen if people spread rumours.
Write a sentence using the connective however to show a change in thought.
Write a sentence using the multi-word verb speak up.
Main Writing Task: Contrast Paragraph
Write a paragraph of 120-150 words on the following topic:

Compare how people communicated in the past with how they communicate today. Use at least two contrast conjunctions (although, while, whereas) and at least three vocabulary words from the Unit 1 list.
Follow this structure:
Topic sentence: State the main idea of your paragraph (e.g., Communication has changed dramatically over the past century).

Supporting sentences: Give specific examples of how communication was different in the past (letters, face-to-face, radio) compared to today (social media, instant messaging, video calls). Use although, while, or whereas to show the contrasts.

Vocabulary integration: Work in at least three unit vocabulary words naturally. For example, you might describe how people today are sometimes gullible about online information, whereas in the past they relied on conventional sources with greater foresight.
Concluding sentence: End with a thought about what this change means for the future.
Extended Writing Task: Opinion Essay
When you are ready for a bigger challenge, write a short essay of 200-250 words on this topic:
"Should students be taught how to identify misinformation as part of their school curriculum? Give reasons for your answer and include examples."
Use the following essay structure:
Introduction (2-3 sentences): Introduce the topic and state your opinion.
Body Paragraph 1: Give your first reason with an example. Use a contrast conjunction.
Body Paragraph 2: Give your second reason with an example. Use reported speech to reference something someone said.
Conclusion (2-3 sentences): Summarise your view and end with a strong closing statement.
Remember to use connectives to link your ideas, include abstract nouns where appropriate, and proofread for grammatical accuracy. Ask your AI tutor to review your draft and give feedback.
Writing Checklist
Before you submit or share any piece of writing, check it against this list:
Have I used at least two contrast conjunctions correctly?
Have I included vocabulary words from the unit list?
Is my grammar accurate (verb tenses, subject-verb agreement)?
Have I used commas correctly with although and while clauses?
Is my writing organised with a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Have I proofread for spelling mistakes?
Does my writing address the topic directly and stay focused?

Activities and Exercises

This section provides a wide range of practice activities covering all the skills and grammar points in this unit. Work through them at your own pace. You do not need to complete every single activity, but the more you practise, the stronger your English will become.
Exercise 1: Fill in the Contrast Conjunction
Choose although, while, or whereas to complete each sentence.
__________ Amina prefers reading fiction, her brother Hassan enjoys non-fiction books.
__________ the weather was very hot, the students continued their outdoor activity.
Mogadishu is a coastal city, __________ Hargeisa is located inland.
__________ some people believe everything they read online, others are more sceptical.
She worked very hard on the project, __________ the final result was disappointing.
Traditional markets are lively and social, __________ online shopping is convenient but impersonal.
__________ the exam was difficult, most students managed to pass.
Yusuf likes to plan everything in advance, __________ Farah is more spontaneous.
Answers: 1. While  2. Although  3. whereas  4. While  5. although  6. whereas  7. Although  8. whereas
Exercise 2: Gerund Identification
In each sentence below, identify the -ing form and say whether it is acting as a subject, an object, or part of the present continuous tense.
Swimming is Halima's favourite sport.
The students are discussing the news article.
I enjoy learning new vocabulary every week.
Thinking critically is an important life skill.
They are preparing for the student forum.
Citing evidence makes your argument stronger.
Answers: 1. Swimming -- subject  2. discussing -- present continuous  3. learning -- object  4. Thinking -- subject  5. preparing -- present continuous  6. Citing -- subject
Exercise 3: Present Continuous Conversion
Rewrite each sentence using the present continuous tense.
The teacher explains the grammar rule. --> The teacher __________.
We study for the exam. --> We __________.
Hassan reads the flyer carefully. --> Hassan __________.
The students debate the issue at the forum. --> The students __________.
Amina writes in her journal. --> Amina __________.
Answers: 1. is explaining the grammar rule  2. are studying for the exam  3. is reading the flyer carefully  4. are debating the issue at the forum  5. is writing in her journal
Exercise 4: Abstract Noun Matching
Match each abstract noun with its correct definition.
Answers: 1-c, 2-a, 3-d, 4-e, 5-b, 6-f
Exercise 5: Reported Speech Transformation
Convert each direct speech sentence into reported speech.
Farah said, "I am proud of the student forum."
Halima said, "We will check the facts before we share anything."
The teacher asked, "Have you finished your homework?"
Hassan said, "I do not believe the rumour."
Amina said, "I was reading the article when the bell rang."

Answers: 1. Farah said that he was proud of the student forum.  2. Halima said that they would check the facts before they shared anything.  3. The teacher asked if we/they had finished our/their homework.  4. Hassan said that he did not believe the rumour.  5. Amina said that she had been reading the article when the bell rang.
Exercise 6: Conditional Sentences
Decide whether each sentence is a zero conditional or a first conditional, then fill in the correct verb form.
If you __________ (mix) truth with rumour, people __________ (become) confused. [Type: ____]
If Hamza __________ (check) the source, he __________ (find) out the truth. [Type: ____]
If water __________ (reach) 100 degrees, it __________ (boil). [Type: ____]
If we __________ (not think) critically, we __________ (fall) for misinformation. [Type: ____]
Answers: 1. mix / become -- zero conditional  2. checks / will find -- first conditional  3. reaches / boils -- zero conditional  4. do not think / will fall -- first conditional
Exercise 7: Connectives in Action
Join each pair of sentences using the connective in brackets.
She studied very hard. She passed the exam. (therefore)
He wanted to go to the protest. He was not sure it was real. (however)
They created the forum. Students could discuss issues openly. (so that)
The rumour was plausible. It turned out to be false. (although)
Many people shared the flyer. They did not check the facts. (because)

Answers: 1. She studied very hard; therefore, she passed the exam.  2. He wanted to go to the protest; however, he was not sure it was real.  3. They created the forum so that students could discuss issues openly.  4. Although the rumour was plausible, it turned out to be false.  5. Many people shared the flyer because they did not check the facts.
Exercise 8: Multi-Word Verb Completion
Complete each sentence with the correct multi-word verb: give in, carry out, speak up.
If you have an opinion about the issue, do not be afraid to __________.
The team decided to __________ the investigation before making any accusations.
After a long argument, she finally decided to __________ and accept the compromise.
Answers: 1. speak up  2. carry out  3. give in

Vocabulary Reinforcement

By this point in the unit you have encountered the vocabulary words in the story, in grammar examples, and in exercises. Now it is time to deepen your knowledge and make these words part of your active vocabulary -- words you can use confidently in your own speech and writing, not just recognise when you see them.
Reinforcement Activity 1: Word Families
Many vocabulary words belong to families of related words. Knowing the family helps you use the right form in different situations. Study these word families:
Write one sentence for each word family using two different forms of the word. For example: "Her defiance surprised everyone; she had never been defiant before."
Reinforcement Activity 2: Context Clues
Read each sentence and use the context to guess the meaning of the bolded word. Then check your guess against the vocabulary list.
The embargo on trade meant that no goods could enter or leave the port for weeks.
Despite his shrewd thinking, he was tricked by the clever disguise.
The smell of fresh bread was pervasive throughout the entire neighbourhood.
She wrote a concise report -- only two pages, but it covered everything clearly.
After the mistake, he wore a rueful expression, wishing he could undo what he had done.
Reinforcement Activity 3: Vocabulary in Use

Write a short paragraph (5-7 sentences) about a time when communication went wrong -- at school, at home, or in your community. Use at least five vocabulary words from the unit list. Underline each vocabulary word you use.
Reinforcement Activity 4: Crossword Clues
Test your knowledge by matching these clues to the correct vocabulary word:
A strong feeling of enthusiasm (4 letters): __________
Very dirty conditions caused by poverty (7 letters): __________
Easily tricked or deceived (8 letters): __________
Happening suddenly without planning (11 letters): __________
The ability to plan for the future (9 letters): __________
To mention as a source of evidence (4 letters): __________
A law stopping trade with a country (7 letters): __________
Following accepted rules or traditions (12 letters): __________
Answers: 1. zeal  2. squalor  3. gullible  4. spontaneous  5. foresight  6. cite  7. embargo  8. conventional

Self-Study Tips
Since you are learning at your own pace, it is important to develop strong study habits. Here are practical tips to help you get the most out of this unit:
Time Management
Set a regular study schedule. Aim for 30-45 minutes of English study per day, five days a week. Consistency is more important than length.
Break the unit into chunks. Do not try to finish everything in one sitting. Spread the work over the week: vocabulary on Day 1, grammar on Day 2, reading on Day 3, and so on.
Use a timer. Set a timer for 25 minutes and focus entirely on one task. Take a 5-minute break, then do another 25-minute session. This is called the Pomodoro technique.
Plan ahead for teacher sessions. Before each teacher session, write down questions or areas where you are stuck. This way, you can use the session time wisely.
Active Learning Strategies
Do not just read -- interact. Highlight, underline, take notes, and summarise in your own words. The more you engage with the material, the more you will remember.
Teach someone else. If you can explain a grammar rule to a friend or sibling, you truly understand it. Teaching is one of the best ways to learn.
Use your AI tutor daily. Ask it to quiz you, explain things differently, give extra examples, or check your work. It is available 24/7 and never gets tired of your questions.
Keep a vocabulary journal. Write each new word, its definition, and a personal sentence. Review your journal at the end of each week.
Read in English every day. Even ten minutes of reading -- a news article, a short story, or a page from a book -- will improve your vocabulary and grammar over time.
Dealing with Difficulties
If you do not understand something, do not skip it. Ask your AI tutor to explain it in a different way, or bring the question to your teacher session.
Mistakes are part of learning. Every error you make and correct is one step closer to mastery. Do not be discouraged.
If you feel overwhelmed, go back to basics. Re-read the vocabulary list, review the grammar introduction, and try the easier exercises first before moving on.
Connect English to your daily life. Think in English, label objects around your house, or describe what you see on your way to school using new vocabulary.

Self-Assessment

Before moving on to the next unit, honestly assess your progress. Rate yourself on each skill using this scale: 1 = I need much more practice, 2 = I understand but still make mistakes, 3 = I am confident and can use this well.

If most of your ratings are 3: Excellent work. You are ready to move on to Unit 2 with confidence. Consider helping a classmate who is still working through the material -- teaching others is a powerful way to reinforce your own learning.

If you have some ratings of 2: You have a good foundation but need a little more practice. Focus on the areas where you rated yourself a 2 and spend extra time on those exercises before the next unit. Ask your AI tutor for additional practice in those specific areas.

If you have ratings of 1: Do not worry -- this means you know exactly where to focus your effort. Go back to the relevant sections of the Grammar and Vocabulary files, redo the exercises, and ask your AI tutor for step-by-step help. You can also bring these areas to your teacher session for additional support.

Preparing for Next Unit
Well done on completing Unit 1! Before you move on, here are a few things to do to prepare for what comes next:
Review your vocabulary journal. Make sure you can define and use all 33 words from this unit. These words may appear again in future units.
Reread your best piece of writing. Whether it is your contrast paragraph or your opinion essay, read it one more time and feel proud of your progress.
Reflect on the theme. Unit 1 explored how communication can build or destroy trust. Think about how this theme connects to your own life and community.
Look ahead. Unit 2 will build on the grammar and vocabulary foundations you have established here. Keep practising, keep asking questions, and keep writing every day.
Ask your AI tutor for a bridge activity -- a short task that connects what you learned in Unit 1 to what you will learn in Unit 2.

Remember: learning a language is a journey, not a race. Every word you learn, every sentence you write, and every conversation you have in English brings you closer to fluency. You are doing important work, and your effort matters. Keep going!

paragraphs: Year 8 - Unit 1 Story
Whispers Beyond the Gate
📖 About This Story
Genre: Teen school mystery / social drama
Theme: Misinformation, student voice, truth vs. rumour
Setting: Present-day Mogadishu, Somalia (urban modern high school)
Moral: Truth needs no volume -- just courage, care, and questioning minds.
Look for vocabulary words from your Unit 1 list as you read. They appear naturally throughout the story.
Part 1: A Strange Morning

Hamza walked to Nuur Academy just after sunrise. Mogadishu shimmered in the early light -- the distant Indian Ocean sparkled beyond the rooftops, and tuk-tuks hummed past him as the smell of spiced shaah drifted from a corner cafe. It was a temperate morning, mild and pleasant, but something in the air felt strange, like a word caught between the teeth and never quite spoken.
At the school gate, his classmate Leyla waved him over. Her usually bright expression was clouded. She dropped her voice to a whisper.
"Have you heard about the embargo?" she asked, eyes wide. "The port is being shut down. No more rice, no fuel, no sugar. People are saying it is going to get very bad very quickly."
Hamza blinked. "What? There is nothing about that on the news."
"My cousin Khalid told me," Leyla explained. "He says he knows someone in customs. He sent a flyer. Look." She pressed a crumpled paper into his hand.
Hamza read the bold red letters aloud: "STAND UP FOR SOMALIA. 3:30 PM. Bakaaraha Road. Bring signs. Block the trucks. Show your defiance."
There was no organiser's name. No logo. No source. Just urgent words in angry ink. His gut twisted with doubt.
"And we believe this?" he asked.
Leyla bit her lip. "Khalid sounded serious. And a protest is happening whether we go or not."
Part 2: The Buzz Builds

By mid-morning, the corridors of Nuur Academy were buzzing with whispered conversations. Some students scrolled through TikTok, watching shaky clips that showed young people tipping crates across a road. The caption read: "We are blocking imports until the leaders hear us. Mogadishu, stand up!"
The rumour was becoming pervasive -- spreading through every classroom, every group chat, every whispered exchange under the stairwell. It seemed as though the entire school believed it.
Hamza and Leyla sat under the stairwell during break, watching the chaos grow on their screens.
"Is it real?" she asked. "Some of it looks staged."
"It could be from anywhere," Hamza said carefully. "We do not even know when the video was made. The story seems plausible, but plausible is not the same as true."
"Still," Leyla murmured, "if we do nothing, are we just sheep?"
Hamza hesitated. "Acting without facts is worse. If we join a lie, we are not helping -- we are hurting."
While Hamza was cautious and thoughtful, Leyla felt the pull of action. This difference between them would matter as the day unfolded.
Part 3: Classroom Warnings
In their history class, Mr. Farah -- a tall, quietly commanding man who had been teaching at Nuur Academy for fifteen years -- introduced the vocabulary word foresight.

"Foresight is your ability to see what could happen before it does," he explained, writing the word on the board in careful letters. "It is not about being scared. It is about being wise. A person with foresight asks questions before acting."
A student named Nadifa raised her hand. "Sir, is not protest a way to stop something bad before it gets worse?"
"Yes," said Mr. Farah, "but protest only works when the reason is real and the facts are solid. A conscientious person checks first and acts second."

He turned back to the board. "Let us list examples of productive resistance -- movements that made a real difference -- and then failed ones. Think critically. Who had facts? Who just followed? I will allot ten minutes for group discussion."
Hamza wrote in his notebook: "If we do not think for ourselves, someone else will think for us." He underlined it twice.
Part 4: The Protest Scene

At 3:30 PM, Hamza and Leyla walked to Bakaaraha Road. They told themselves they were only going to observe, not to participate. But the scene that greeted them was larger and louder than either of them had expected.

Crowds had gathered -- mostly teenagers, some filming on their phones, a few adults watching warily from behind market stalls. Several boys had built a barricade out of fruit crates and old tyres, blocking the road entirely.
"Do not let the trucks pass!" shouted one boy, his voice raw with zeal. "We will not give in!"

Another student with a portable speaker was yelling slogans: "They want us silent! We refuse to be silent!" A few people cheered. Others looked unsure, glancing around as if waiting for someone to tell them what to do.
Hamza scanned the crowd with shrewd eyes. Something felt off.
"Look," he pointed. "That boy -- the one with the speaker. He is filming himself, not the protest. He is laughing."
The boy took a selfie in front of the barricade. Another student tagged a post online: "Fake riot for the feed. #content"
"This is a joke," Hamza said, jaw tight with frustration. "These people do not care about truth. They want views and followers."
Leyla shook her head. "We need to leave. Now."
Although the protest was loud and energetic, it was built on nothing real -- and they both knew it.
Part 5: Unmasking the Lie
Back at school the next day, the atmosphere had shifted. Where there had been excitement and zeal, there was now embarrassment and silence. A local news outlet had published the truth:
"There is no official embargo. A viral student protest has been traced to a hoax flyer. Students were misled."
Mr. Farah stood before the class, disappointment etched into his face. The room was so quiet you could hear the ceiling fan turning.
"Who wrote the flyer?" he asked. No one answered.
He sighed. "Let me ask another question: what happens when we follow anger instead of truth?"
One boy muttered, "We look stupid."
Hamza raised his hand slowly. "We lose trust. People stop believing us -- even when we are telling the truth."
"Exactly," Mr. Farah said. "This is not about who started it. It is about what we choose to do next. The revelation that it was a hoax is painful, but it is also an opportunity."
Part 6: The Confession
That evening, Leyla received a voice message from her cousin Khalid -- the same person who had told her about the protest in the first place.

His voice was shaky: "Leyla... I made the flyer. I just wanted to boost my follower count. I thought it would go viral and then everyone would forget. I did not know it would get this serious. I am sorry."
Leyla sat frozen, staring at her phone. Then she whispered, "He started all of it."
Hamza clenched his fist. "People could have been hurt. There were younger students at that barricade. What if things had turned violent?"
"He was gullible enough to think there would be no consequences," Leyla said, her voice edged with dissent from Khalid's carelessness. "And we were almost gullible enough to follow him blindly."
"We need to fix this," Hamza said. "Somehow."
Leyla looked at him, pensive and thoughtful. "I think I know how."
Part 7: Building Something Better

The next morning, Hamza and Leyla pitched an idea to Mr. Farah: The Mogadishu Student Forum -- a weekly meeting where students could discuss current events, fact-check news, and debate real issues in a respectful environment.
"We want to allot time each week for open dialogue," Hamza explained. "A space where people can talk instead of shout, ask questions instead of follow blindly."
Mr. Farah studied them for a long moment. Then he nodded. "You are trying to be part of the solution instead of the problem. I will support this."
At the first meeting, forty students showed up. Some were quiet and nervous. Some were still angry about what had happened. But all of them were curious.
"Whereas last week we followed a lie," Hamza began, standing in front of the group, "this week, we ask questions. That is how real change begins -- not with shouting, but with thinking."
Leyla stepped forward. "We need to stop being so gullible. If you cannot cite your facts, do not post them. Do not act on them. Do not share them."
A Form 3 student named Yusuf added quietly, "Some of us joined the protest out of boredom. We did not think. I feel rueful about that -- genuinely sorry and a little ashamed."
Another student, Halima, said: "The pressure to act is real. But silence is not always cowardice. Sometimes it is serenity -- choosing to wait, to learn, to understand before you move."
Part 8: From Mistake to Movement

In the weeks that followed, the forum became a safe space for honest discussion. Students learned to assimilate information carefully rather than react to it emotionally. They practised weighing evidence, questioning sources, and respecting viewpoints different from their own.
One week, they debated censorship. Another week, they discussed corruption. Then they tackled online disinformation -- the very thing that had started all of this.
Hamza moderated a fierce debate about whether protests should be spontaneous or carefully planned.
"Spontaneous actions can change the world," argued Nadifa. "History is full of moments where people rose up without warning."
"That is true," Leyla replied, "but conscientious actions -- ones where people think carefully and prepare -- leave fewer scars. They are slower, yes, but they tend to last. They have longevity."
A younger student read aloud from a note she had written: "If we do not think for ourselves, someone else will think for us."
The next day, that line appeared on the wall outside the school -- in permanent marker. No one knew who had written it, but no one erased it either.
Part 9: One Final Test
Two months later, a new flyer appeared at the school gates. This one read: "Rise Up. Speak Loud. Ban school fees for all!"
Some students felt the familiar rush of excitement. Others felt a twinge of recognition -- they had seen this before.
Hamza picked up the paper and examined it calmly. He did not rush. He did not react. He smiled.
"All right," he said. "Let us check the source. Let us ask: Who wrote this? Who benefits? What is the data? Is this plausible, or is it another bizarre attempt to manipulate us?"
Instead of rushing into the streets, thirty students joined that week's forum to debate the issue first. They used conventional methods -- research, discussion, evidence -- to investigate the claim.
Although the flyer was designed to provoke an immediate reaction, the students responded with foresight and patience. They did not need to dispel rumours with force; they dispelled them with facts.
That was real change. Not loud, not viral -- but lasting.
Part 10: A Quiet Ending

On the last day of term, Hamza and Leyla sat on the school rooftop, eating sambusa and watching the city stretch below. Mogadishu's skyline glowed in the late afternoon light. Somewhere in the distance, the call to Asr drifted over the buildings.
"I am proud of us," Leyla said. "We were almost part of a lie. Now we help people question what they hear and think before they act."
Hamza nodded. "It is easy to shout. It is harder to think. But thinking is what makes the difference."
Leyla smiled. "And thinking lasts longer than shouting."

They sat in comfortable silence, feeling something that did not need a hashtag or a viral video to be real. It was quiet. It was strong. It was a kind of serenity that comes from knowing you chose truth when it would have been easier to follow the crowd.
And in that quiet, they knew: this was just the beginning.
✅ Vocabulary Coverage
This story uses all 33 vocabulary words from Unit 1.
Nouns: barricade, zeal, definition, foresight, defiance, dissent, serenity, revelation, longevity, squalor, embargo, quibble
Verbs: allot, accustom, assimilate, concede, dispel, cite
Adjectives: concise, temperate, plausible, rueful, pensive, impending, shrewd, gullible, spontaneous, conscientious, bizarre, pervasive, conventional
Grammar patterns used: although/while/whereas, present continuous, -ing forms, abstract nouns, reported speech, conditionals, connectives, multi-word verbs
