Please write a __________ answer -- no more than two sentences.
The teacher decided to __________ extra time for students who needed help.
His explanation sounded __________, but I was not completely convinced.
The __________ boy believed everything he was told without questioning it.
If you want to prove your point in the essay, you should __________ reliable sources.
The forum helped to __________ the fear that students could not make a difference.
Answers: 1. temperate  2. defiance  3. accustom  4. revelation  5. concise  6. allot  7. plausible  8. gullible  9. cite  10. dispel
Exercise 3: Word in Context
Read each short paragraph and identify the vocabulary word that best fills the gap.

Paragraph A: After the exam results were announced, Amina sat alone in the library, staring out of the window. She was not crying or angry -- she was simply __________, thinking deeply about what she could have done differently.
Answer: pensive

Paragraph B: The students decided to create a weekly discussion group. Whereas before they had simply reacted to news, now they wanted to __________ information carefully, weighing the evidence before forming opinions.
Answer: assimilate
Paragraph C: A strange smell filled the classroom -- something between burnt coffee and old socks. Everyone agreed it was __________; nobody could figure out where it was coming from.
Answer: bizarre
Exercise 4: Sentence Writing
Write your own sentence for each of the following words. Make sure your sentence shows the meaning of the word clearly.
conscientious
barricade
zeal
dissent
impending
serenity
conventional
longevity
quibble
assimilate
(Check your sentences with your AI tutor or teacher for accuracy.)
Exercise 5: Odd One Out
In each group, one word does not belong. Circle the odd one out and explain why.
serenity, foresight, barricade, defiance (Hint: three are abstract nouns; one is concrete.)
shrewd, gullible, conscientious, embargo (Hint: three are adjectives; one is a noun.)
allot, assimilate, concise, dispel (Hint: three are verbs; one is an adjective.)
plausible, bizarre, conventional, cite (Hint: three are adjectives; one is a verb.)
Answers: 1. barricade (concrete noun, not abstract)  2. embargo (noun, not adjective)  3. concise (adjective, not verb)  4. cite (verb, not adjective)
Exercise 6: Synonyms and Antonyms
For each word, write one synonym (word with a similar meaning) and one antonym (word with an opposite meaning). Use a dictionary or ask your AI tutor if you need help.
Suggested Answers: concise: brief / lengthy | gullible: naive / sceptical | spontaneous: unplanned / deliberate | serenity: peace / chaos | shrewd: clever / foolish | defiance: resistance / obedience
📚 Keep Practising!
Review this word list every few days until you can define each word from memory.
Write new sentences using these words in your vocabulary journal.
Ask your AI tutor to quiz you or create flashcards.
Try to use at least three new vocabulary words in your daily conversations this week.
tables: Using few words to say something clearly.
The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.
A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing.
The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.
A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness.
She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.
The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts.
Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.
A statement that explains the meaning of a word.
Look up the definition of any word you do not know.
To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose.
The teacher will allot fifteen minutes for group discussion.
To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience.
It took me a while to accustom myself to waking up early.
A small argument about something unimportant.
They had a quibble about which film to watch but quickly agreed.
To learn and understand new information and make it part of your knowledge.
When you move to a new place, it takes time to assimilate into the culture.
Having mild or moderate weather; not too hot or too cold.
The temperate climate in spring is perfect for outdoor activities.
Seeming reasonable or likely to be true.
Her excuse for being late was plausible, so the teacher accepted it.
The act of refusing to obey someone or something.
The students showed defiance by refusing to accept the unfair rule.
There was dissent among the students about the new school uniform.
Very dirty and unpleasant conditions, usually from poverty.
The charity worked to improve conditions for families living in squalor.
To admit something is true or that you have lost.
After losing, the captain had to concede that the other team played better.
Feeling sad or sorry about something you did.
He gave a rueful smile after forgetting his friend's birthday.
Thinking deeply, often in a sad or serious way.
She sat by the window with a pensive look, lost in thought.
The ability to think about and plan for the future.
Having foresight helps you make better decisions in life.
The dark clouds warned of an impending storm.
Having good judgement; able to make smart decisions.
The shrewd student always found the most efficient way to study.
Easily tricked because you believe what people say.
She was so gullible that she believed the prank was real.
We had a spontaneous celebration when we heard the good news.
A state of being calm and peaceful.
After a busy day, she found serenity sitting quietly in the garden.
Careful and thorough; paying attention to doing things well.
A conscientious student always completes homework on time.
The film had a bizarre ending that nobody expected.
Spreading widely throughout an area or group.
The smell of spiced tea was pervasive in the morning market.
A law or order that stops trade or limits what can be sold.
The government placed an embargo on certain imported goods.
To make something go away, especially a feeling or belief.
The teacher tried to dispel the students' fears about the exam.
To mention something as a source of evidence.
In your essay, you should cite the books you used for research.
A surprising or important fact that is made known.
The revelation that the flyer was a hoax shocked the school.
Following accepted rules or traditions; normal.
In a conventional classroom, students sit at desks and listen.
a. thinking deeply in a sad or serious way
b. a law that stops trade
c. to admit something is true after resisting
d. having good judgement and making smart decisions
h. how long something lives or lasts

paragraphs: Year 8 English — Unit 2 Grammar Reference
Partitive Expressions, Conditionals, Relative Clauses, Comparatives, Passive Voice
Section 1: Partitive Expressions
What Are Partitive Expressions?

Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. They make your sentences clearer and more specific. For example, instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These small phrases make a big difference in how natural and precise your English sounds.

Partitive expressions are especially important when you are talking about uncountable nouns — things you cannot count individually, like water, information, excitement, or sugar. Since you cannot say "two waters" or "three informations," you use partitive expressions to indicate quantity: a glass of water, a piece of information.
How to Form Partitive Expressions
Follow these steps to create partitive expressions:
Identify whether the noun is countable or uncountable. Countable nouns have singular and plural forms (apple/apples). Uncountable nouns do not (water, information, sugar).
Choose the right partitive word. The table below shows common partitive expressions and the types of nouns they go with.
Combine the partitive word with the noun. For example: a piece of + cake = a piece of cake.
Use the expression in a sentence. For example: "Can I have a piece of cake, please?"
Common Partitive Expressions
Partitive Expressions for Countable vs. Uncountable Nouns
For countable nouns, you can also use:
a few + plural noun: "I have a few friends in this class."
a couple of + plural noun: "She bought a couple of books."
several + plural noun: "There are several reasons for this decision."
For uncountable nouns, use:
a little + uncountable noun: "Add a little sugar to the tea."
a bit of + uncountable noun: "I need a bit of advice."
a great deal of + uncountable noun: "There is a great deal of information to process."
Common Mistakes with Partitive Expressions
Mistake 1: Using the wrong partitive expression for the noun type.
Incorrect: "a piece of water" — you cannot cut water into pieces!
Correct: "a glass of water" or "a drop of water"
Mistake 2: Forgetting to include the partitive word altogether.
Incorrect: "I want cake." (too general)
Correct: "I want a piece of cake." (specific and natural)
Mistake 3: Using "a lot of" when a more specific expression would be better.
Vague: "I felt a lot of excitement."
Better: "I felt a surge of excitement."
Memory Tip
💡 Quick Tip
Think of partitive expressions as sharing words.
They help you tell others exactly what part you want from a whole.
If you can count it, use a few or a couple of.
If you cannot count it, use a bit of or a little.
Practice Exercises — Partitive Expressions
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct partitive expression.
Can I have __________ (a piece of / a few) cake?
Pour me __________ (a cup of / a couple of) tea, please.
I need __________ (a little / a bunch) help with my homework.
She wants __________ (a slice of / a drop of) pizza.
There is __________ (a lot of / a few) excitement in the room!
Only __________ (a handful of / a glass of) students passed the difficult exam.
After the announcement, there was __________ (a surge of / a slice of) indignation among the community.
He ate __________ (a bit of / a multitude of) bread for breakfast.
Answers:
a piece of
a cup of
a little
a slice of
a lot of
a handful of
a surge of
a bit of
Exercise 2: Write Your Own Sentences
Write a sentence using each of the following partitive expressions. Make your sentences interesting and relevant to your own life.
a surge of __________
a multitude of __________
a handful of __________
a piece of __________
a drop of __________
Section 2: Zero and First Conditionals
What Are Conditionals?

Conditionals are sentences that describe what happens (or what will happen) if a certain condition is met. English has several types of conditionals, but in this unit you will focus on two: the zero conditional and the first conditional.
Zero Conditional — Facts and General Truths
The zero conditional describes things that are always true. It is used for scientific facts, general truths, and reliable cause-and-effect relationships.
Structure: If + present simple, present simple
Examples:
If you heat ice, it melts.
If you mix red and blue, you get purple.
If it rains, the grass gets wet.
If plants do not get water, they die.
You can also use when instead of if in zero conditional sentences, because the result always happens: "When you heat ice, it melts."
First Conditional — Real Future Possibilities
The first conditional describes things that are possible or likely to happen in the future. It is used when you believe there is a real chance that the condition will be fulfilled.
Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb
Examples:
If I study hard, I will pass the test.
If it rains tomorrow, I will bring an umbrella.
If we intervene now, we will avert disaster.
If you feign calm, they will push harder.
Variations: You can use might or can instead of will when the result is less certain:
If I finish my homework, I might play outside.
If you ask politely, she can help you.
Common Mistakes with Conditionals
Mistake 1: Using the wrong tense in the if-clause.
Incorrect: "If you will study hard, you will pass."
Correct: "If you study hard, you will pass." (Present simple in the if-clause, not will.)
Mistake 2: Confusing zero and first conditionals.
Zero: "If you heat water, it boils." (Always true — a fact.)
First: "If you heat the water, I will make tea." (A specific future possibility.)
Practice Exercises — Conditionals
Exercise 1: Identify the Type
Read each sentence and write whether it is a zero conditional or a first conditional.
If you drop a ball, it falls. __________
If it snows tomorrow, we will build a snowman. __________
If you press the button, the door opens. __________
If Faisa investigates the story, she will uncover the truth. __________
If water reaches 100 degrees, it boils. __________
Answers: 1. Zero  2. First  3. Zero  4. First  5. Zero
Exercise 2: Complete the Sentences
Fill in the blanks with the correct verb forms.
If you __________ (study) every day, you __________ (improve) your English. (First)
If water __________ (freeze), it __________ (turn) to ice. (Zero)
If we __________ (leave) now, we __________ (arrive) on time. (First)
If you __________ (not water) plants, they __________ (die). (Zero)
If she __________ (practise) speaking, she __________ (become) more confident. (First)
Answers:
study, will improve
freezes, turns
leave, will arrive
do not water, die
practises, will become
Section 3: Relative Clauses
What Are Relative Clauses?

Relative clauses are parts of a sentence that give you extra information about a noun. They begin with relative pronouns: who (for people), that (for things or people), and which (for things). Relative clauses make your sentences more detailed and interesting.
How to Form Relative Clauses
Identify the noun you want to give more information about.
Choose the correct relative pronoun: who (people), that (things/people), which (things).
Add the extra information after the relative pronoun.
Check punctuation: defining clauses have no commas; non-defining clauses use commas.
Defining vs. Non-Defining Relative Clauses
Defining clauses give essential information. Without them, the sentence does not make complete sense. They do not use commas:
"The student who won the debate is in my class." (Which student? The one who won.)
"The document that contained the permits was anonymous." (Which document? The one with permits.)
Non-defining clauses give extra, non-essential information. The sentence still makes sense without them. They use commas:
"Faisa, who runs the radio station, is very brave." (We already know which Faisa.)
"The radio station, which was started as a school project, became very popular." (Extra detail.)
Common Mistakes with Relative Clauses
Mistake 1: Using which for people.
Incorrect: "The teacher which helped us is very kind."
Correct: "The teacher who helped us is very kind."
Mistake 2: Forgetting commas in non-defining clauses.
Incorrect: "My brother who enjoys maths is studying for his test." (This implies you have multiple brothers.)
Correct: "My brother, who enjoys maths, is studying for his test." (Extra info about your only brother.)
Practice Exercises — Relative Clauses
Exercise 1: Choose the Correct Pronoun
Fill in each blank with who, that, or which.
The girl __________ won the race is my friend.
I have a book __________ is very interesting.
The teacher __________ helps us is very kind.
The flowers, __________ are blooming, smell wonderful.
The argument __________ he presented was flawed.
The plant, __________ tendrils flourished in the light, grew taller every day.
Answers: 1. who  2. that  3. who  4. which  5. that  6. whose
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
Combine each pair of sentences into one sentence using a relative clause.
Ahmed edits audio. Ahmed is Faisa's partner at the radio station.
The message was anonymous. The message started the investigation.
Mr. Kadir gave evasive answers. Mr. Kadir works for the deputy mayor.
The gardens are threatened. The gardens are in the northern part of the city.
Answers:
Ahmed, who is Faisa's partner at the radio station, edits audio.
The message that started the investigation was anonymous.
Mr. Kadir, who works for the deputy mayor, gave evasive answers.
The gardens that are in the northern part of the city are threatened.
Section 4: Questions Beginning with Prepositions
What Are Prepositional Questions?
In formal English, you can place a preposition at the beginning of a question rather than at the end. This makes the question sound more polished and academic.
Informal: Which perspective was the story told from?
Formal: From which perspective was the story told?
How to Form Prepositional Questions
Identify the preposition in the informal question (from, with, to, about, for, etc.).
Move the preposition to the beginning of the question.
Follow it with the question word (which, whom, what).
Complete the sentence with the correct word order.
Examples
Practice Exercise — Rewrite Formally
Rewrite each question with the preposition at the beginning.
Which teacher do you like learning from?
Which country did this package come from?
Which friend are you going to the library with?
Which issue is the article about?
Which person did you address the letter to?
Answers:
From which teacher do you like learning?
From which country did this package come?
With which friend are you going to the library?
About which issue is the article?
To which person did you address the letter?
Section 5: Comparatives and Superlatives
What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
Comparatives are used to compare two things. Superlatives are used to describe the highest or lowest degree among three or more things.
How to Form Comparatives
The rule depends on the length of the adjective:
How to Form Superlatives
Irregular Comparatives and Superlatives
Using Comparatives and Superlatives from the Story
In "Signal in the Static," Faisa uses comparatives to describe conflicting versions of events:
"One version is louder, another quieter."
"One is cleaner, the other more erratic."
"Truth is not always the most luminous."
Practice Exercises — Comparatives and Superlatives
Exercise 1: Write the Correct Form
My brother is __________ (funny) than my sister.
Today is the __________ (cold) day of the year.
This book is __________ (interesting) than that one.
She is the __________ (good) student in the class.
The second film was __________ (bad) than the first.
Of all the planets, Jupiter is the __________ (large).
Answers: 1. funnier  2. coldest  3. more interesting  4. best  5. worse  6. largest
Section 6: Passive Voice
What Is the Passive Voice?
The passive voice shifts the focus of a sentence from who did the action to what was done. It is useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or when you want to emphasise the action itself.
Active: The teacher read the book.
Passive: The book was read by the teacher.
How to Form the Passive Voice
Find the object of the active sentence. (What receives the action?)
Make the object the new subject of the passive sentence.
Use the correct form of "to be" (was/were for past tense, is/are for present tense).
Add the past participle of the main verb.
Optionally add "by" + the doer if it is important to know who did it.
Passive Voice Tense Table
Passive Voice in the Story
Faisa deliberately uses passive voice in her newspaper article to create a powerful effect:
"The gardens were promised."
"The questions were raised."
"The files were deleted."
"The permits were signed."
Notice how the passive voice creates mystery — we do not know who promised, raised, deleted, or signed. This is a strategic writing choice that you can use in your own work.
Common Mistakes with Passive Voice
Mistake 1: Using the wrong form of "to be."
Incorrect: "The book is read yesterday." (Past action needs past tense.)
Correct: "The book was read yesterday."
Mistake 2: Forgetting to use the past participle.
Incorrect: "The letter was wrote by my friend."
Correct: "The letter was written by my friend."
Practice Exercises — Passive Voice
Exercise 1: Rewrite in Passive Voice
The chef baked the cake.
Someone stole my bicycle.
The students completed the project.
The wind destroyed the old building.
Faisa wrote the article.
Answers:
The cake was baked by the chef.
My bicycle was stolen.
The project was completed by the students.
The old building was destroyed by the wind.
The article was written by Faisa.
Exercise 2: Active or Passive?
Identify whether each sentence is active or passive.
The message was deciphered by the students.
Ahmed edited the audio files.
The land permits were signed by the mayor.
Faisa broadcast the truth to the city.
The investigation was launched after the broadcast.
Answers: 1. Passive  2. Active  3. Passive  4. Active  5. Passive
📖 Grammar Summary
Partitive Expressions: a piece of, a surge of, a multitude of, etc.
Zero Conditional: If + present simple, present simple (facts)
First Conditional: If + present simple, will + base verb (real possibilities)
Relative Clauses: who (people), that/which (things), with or without commas
Comparatives: -er or more + adjective (two things)
Superlatives: -est or most + adjective (three or more things)
Passive Voice: subject + was/were + past participle
tables: Can I have a piece of cake?
She drank a glass of orange juice.
Would you like a cup of tea?
He ate a slice of pizza for lunch.
I need a bit of help with this.
There was not a drop of water left.
There are a lot of students in the class.
She felt a surge of excitement.
A multitude of people gathered outside.
Only a handful of students arrived early.
He bought a bunch of flowers.
Which store did you buy that from?
From which store did you buy that?
Who did you give the book to?
To whom did you give the book?
What topic is your presentation about?
About what topic is your presentation?
Who are you going to the party with?
With whom are you going to the party?
careful -> more careful, beautiful -> more beautiful
careful -> most careful, beautiful -> most beautiful
The report is written every week.
The project will be completed tomorrow.

paragraphs: Year 8 English — Unit 2
Design, Packaging, and Sustainability
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 2 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the fascinating world of design, packaging, and sustainability through the lens of a powerful story called "Signal in the Static." This story follows two courageous Somali students, Faisa and Ahmed, who run a youth radio station in Hargeisa and use their platform to investigate a secret land deal that threatens community gardens. Their journey will show you how design thinking, clear communication, and a commitment to truth can make a real difference in your community.

Throughout this unit, you will strengthen your English skills across all four domains: reading, writing, speaking, and listening. You will learn new vocabulary that helps you express complex ideas about investigation, emotion, and public policy. You will study partitive expressions as your primary grammar focus, along with zero and first conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives and superlatives, and passive voice. Each of these grammar tools will help you communicate with greater precision and confidence.

The theme of design, packaging, and sustainability connects to the story in a meaningful way. Just as designers must think carefully about how they present information and products to the public, Faisa and Ahmed must design their radio broadcasts to be clear, honest, and impactful. Just as sustainable packaging avoids waste and protects the environment, sustainable journalism avoids sensationalism and protects the truth. You will see how the principles of good design apply not only to physical products but also to the way we communicate ideas and hold institutions accountable.

This unit is designed for self-paced learning, which means you can work through the material at your own speed. You have access to a 24/7 AI tutor who can help you whenever you are stuck, and you will also meet with your teacher twice a week for guided sessions. Take your time with each section, revisit anything that feels difficult, and remember that learning is a journey, not a race.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: What is the connection between design thinking and journalism?
Or: Can you explain what sustainable communication means in simple terms?
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and analyse a narrative text, identifying themes of truth, bias, manipulation, and civic responsibility.
Use partitive expressions correctly and confidently in both speaking and writing (e.g., a piece of evidence, a surge of indignation, a multitude of voices).
Form and use zero and first conditionals to express general truths and real future possibilities.
Construct relative clauses with who, that, and which to add detail and clarity to your sentences.
Compare and contrast ideas using comparative and superlative forms of adjectives.
Recognise and produce passive voice sentences, understanding when and why writers choose passive over active constructions.
Ask formal questions beginning with prepositions (e.g., From which perspective was the story told?).
Define, spell, and use all 32 vocabulary words from this unit in context.
Design and deliver a short spoken presentation on a topic related to youth involvement in public policy.
Write a persuasive paragraph or short essay using the grammar and vocabulary from this unit.
Evaluate sources and identify bias in media narratives.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment checklists and study journals.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Which of these objectives should I focus on first?
Or: Can you give me a quick quiz on any of these skills?
Vocabulary Preview

Before you dive into the story and grammar lessons, take some time to preview the key vocabulary for this unit. These words appear throughout "Signal in the Static" and will also be used in your grammar exercises, writing tasks, and speaking activities. Familiarising yourself with these words now will make the rest of the unit much easier to navigate.
Nouns You Will Learn
The nouns in this unit help you talk about stories, ideas, conflicts, and the natural world.
narrative — a story or description of events that tells what happened
synopsis — a short summary of a story or text that gives the main points
argument — a disagreement or discussion where people express different opinions
analysis — the process of studying something carefully to understand it better
multitude — a large number of people or things
restitution — the act of giving back something that was lost or taken away
hindrance — something that makes it difficult for you to do something
tendril — a thin, curly part of a plant that helps it climb or hold onto something
Verbs You Will Learn
The verbs in this unit describe strong actions and emotions. Many of them express feelings, strategies, and consequences.
exult — to feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory
loathe — to feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone
usurp — to take control of something without the right to do so
feign — to pretend to have something or to be something that you are not
intervene — to come between people or groups to help settle a problem
obstruct — to block something so that it cannot move or be seen
avert — to prevent something bad from happening
inundate — to flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something
flourish — to grow or develop in a healthy or strong way
decipher — to figure out or understand something difficult to read or understand
rebuke — to speak to someone in a way that shows you are angry because they did something wrong
quell — to stop something, especially a feeling or a situation, from becoming worse
Adjectives You Will Learn
The adjectives in this unit describe people, situations, and qualities. They will help you write more vividly and speak more precisely.
adept — very skilled or good at something
impassive — not showing any emotion or feeling; calm and controlled
sociable — someone who likes to talk and spend time with other people
implicit — suggested or understood without being directly stated
exhilarating — very exciting and happy
pertinent — related to the topic or important for understanding something
erratic — not regular or consistent; unpredictable
luminous — giving off light; bright or shining
oblique — not straight or direct; slanted or angled
monotonous — boring because it is always the same and never changes
💡 Study Tip
Write each new word on a flashcard with the definition on one side and an example sentence on the other.
Try to use at least three new vocabulary words in your conversations each day.
Group the words by part of speech (nouns, verbs, adjectives) to help you remember them.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you test me on five vocabulary words from this unit?
Or: Can you use the word oblique in three different sentences so I can understand it better?
Grammar Introduction

This unit covers several important grammar topics that will help you express yourself more clearly and precisely. Your primary grammar focus is on partitive expressions, but you will also practise zero and first conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives and superlatives, passive voice, and questions beginning with prepositions. Let us begin with an overview of each topic.
Partitive Expressions — Your Primary Focus

Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. Instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These expressions make your language more specific and more natural.
Here is how partitive expressions work:
Identify what you are talking about. Is it countable (apples, books, students) or uncountable (water, information, excitement)?
Choose the right partitive word. Common ones include: a piece of, a cup of, a slice of, a bit of, a lot of, a surge of, a multitude of, a handful of, a drop of.
Combine them. Put the partitive word before the noun: a piece of cake, a glass of water, a surge of indignation, a multitude of calls.
Use them in sentences. For example: "There was a surge of excitement when Faisa went on air." Or: "A multitude of listeners called the station."

Why do partitive expressions matter for this unit? In the story "Signal in the Static," Faisa and Ahmed deal with large quantities of information, strong emotions, and complex situations. Partitive expressions help the author describe these things precisely. When you read phrases like "a surge of indignation" or "a multitude of calls," you are seeing partitive expressions in action. Learning to use them will make your own writing more vivid and more mature.
Zero and First Conditionals

Conditionals help you talk about cause and effect. The zero conditional describes things that are always true: "If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils." The first conditional describes real possibilities in the future: "If we intervene now, we will avert disaster." Both types follow a clear pattern:
Zero conditional: If + present simple, present simple. Example: If you mix red and blue, you get purple.
First conditional: If + present simple, will + base verb. Example: If I study hard, I will pass the test.

In the story, Faisa and Ahmed use conditionals when making decisions: "If we stop now, they win." "If we keep going, we could get in trouble." These conditional structures help them weigh their options and express what might happen next.
Relative Clauses

Relative clauses give you extra information about a noun. They begin with words like who (for people), that (for things or people), and which (for things, especially in non-essential clauses). For example:
"The woman who whispered to them was a market vendor."
"The story that went missing involved government land deals."
"The radio station, which was run by students, became a trusted voice in Hargeisa."

There are two types of relative clauses. Defining clauses give essential information and do not use commas: "The dog that barks loudly is mine." Non-defining clauses give extra, non-essential information and use commas: "My dog, which is very friendly, loves to play."
Comparatives and Superlatives

When you need to compare things, you use comparatives (for two things) and superlatives (for three or more). Short adjectives add -er or -est (tall, taller, tallest). Long adjectives use more or most (beautiful, more beautiful, most beautiful). Some adjectives are irregular: good becomes better and best; bad becomes worse and worst.

In the story, Faisa uses comparatives to describe conflicting versions of events: "One version is louder, another quieter. One is cleaner, the other more erratic." These comparisons help the audience understand the complexity of the situation.
Passive Voice

The passive voice shifts the focus from who did something to what was done. It follows the pattern: subject + was/were + past participle. For example: "The files were deleted." "The permits were signed." "The gardens were promised." The passive voice is especially useful when you do not know who performed the action, or when the action itself is more important than the actor.

Faisa uses passive voice strategically in her school newspaper article: "The gardens were promised. The questions were raised. The files were deleted. And the truth? Still unclear." Notice how the passive voice creates a sense of mystery and urgency. You will learn to use this technique in your own writing.
Questions Beginning with Prepositions
Formal English sometimes places prepositions at the beginning of questions rather than at the end. Compare these two forms:
Informal: "Which perspective was the story told from?"
Formal: "From which perspective was the story told?"

In the story, Faisa creates a segment called "From which perspective is the garden story being told?" — a powerful example of this formal question structure. Learning to ask questions this way will help you sound more academic and precise in your writing and presentations.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you explain partitive expressions using examples from everyday life?
Or: What is the difference between a defining and a non-defining relative clause?
Or: Can you give me five examples of passive voice sentences?
Reading and Listening

The centrepiece of this unit is the story "Signal in the Static," which you will find in your separate Story document. This section will guide you through how to read the story effectively and how to develop your listening skills alongside your reading.
Before You Read
Before you open the story, take a few minutes to think about the following questions. Write your answers in your notebook or discuss them with your AI tutor.
Have you ever heard a rumour and wondered whether it was true? How did you try to find out?
What role do you think young people should play in their community? Should they be allowed to ask difficult questions of adults in authority?
What do you know about community radio stations? Have you ever listened to one?
What does the title "Signal in the Static" suggest to you? What might the signal represent, and what might the static represent?
While You Read — Active Reading Strategies
As you read "Signal in the Static," use these strategies to deepen your understanding:
Underline or highlight vocabulary words from this unit when you find them in the story. Notice how they are used in context.
Pause at the end of each section and summarise what happened in one or two sentences. Write these summaries in your notebook.
Look for grammar in action. When you spot a partitive expression, a conditional sentence, a relative clause, or a passive voice construction, make a note of it.
Ask yourself questions as you read. Why did Faisa make that choice? What would you have done differently? What clues suggest that something is wrong?
Pay attention to perspective and bias. Who is telling each part of the story? Whose voice is missing? Why might the mayor's office present a different version of events?
After You Read — Comprehension Questions
After finishing the story, answer these questions in complete sentences. Try to use vocabulary words and grammar structures from this unit in your answers.
What is the main conflict in the story? Who are the key characters involved?
Why does Faisa decide to investigate the anonymous message rather than dismiss it? What does this tell you about her character?
How does Mr. Kadir respond to Faisa's questions during the interview? What literary device or grammar construction does the author use to describe his reactions?
Explain the debate that takes place at school. What are the two sides of the argument? Which side do you agree with and why?
What happens when Ahmed's computer files are wiped? How does this event change the direction of the story?
How do Faisa and Ahmed use their radio broadcast to share information without accusing anyone? Why is this approach important?
What evidence finally proves that the land deal was real? How does Faisa respond to this discovery?
What is the outcome of the investigation? How does the city respond?
Find three examples of partitive expressions in the story and explain what each one means.
Find two examples of passive voice in the story. Rewrite each sentence in active voice and explain how the meaning changes.
Listening Activities
Good listeners are active listeners. Use these activities to practise your listening skills:

Read aloud. Choose a section of the story and read it aloud to yourself or a family member. Focus on pronunciation, intonation, and expression. Pay special attention to the dialogue — try to bring each character to life through your voice.
Dictation practice. Ask your AI tutor to read a sentence from the story while you write it down. Then check your spelling and punctuation. This is excellent practice for both listening and writing.
Audio summary. After reading the story, record yourself giving a two-minute summary of the plot. Listen back and check whether your summary is clear, complete, and uses correct grammar.

Listening for bias. Imagine you are listening to the radio broadcast that Faisa and Ahmed create. What would you listen for to determine whether their report is fair and balanced? Write down three things you would check.

Try asking: Can you read a paragraph from the story aloud so I can practise listening?
Or: Can you quiz me on what happens in the story section by section?
Or: Can you help me practise dictation using sentences from the story?
Speaking

Speaking confidently in English takes practice, and this unit gives you many opportunities to develop your speaking skills. The activities below range from simple discussion prompts to more structured presentation tasks. Work through them at your own pace, and remember that making mistakes is a natural and valuable part of learning.
Discussion Prompts
Choose at least three of the following prompts and discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or a family member. Try to speak in complete sentences and use vocabulary from this unit.
Describe a time when you discovered that something you believed was not entirely true. How did you feel? What did you do?
In the story, Faisa says, "Youth are not a hindrance. We are a mirror." What does she mean by this? Do you agree?
If you ran a school radio station, what topics would you cover? How would you make sure your reporting was fair?
The story explores the conflict between speaking up and staying safe. When is it appropriate to challenge authority, and when might it be wiser to stay quiet?
How does the design of a message affect how people receive it? Think about advertisements, news reports, or social media posts you have seen.
Explain one grammar concept from this unit to a partner as if they have never heard of it before. Use examples to make your explanation clear.
Presentation Task: Youth and Public Policy
Prepare a short spoken presentation (two to three minutes) on the topic: "Should young people have a voice in public policy decisions?" Follow these steps:
Choose your position. Do you agree that young people should be involved, or do you think there are limits to youth involvement?
Plan your argument. Write down three main reasons that support your position. For each reason, include at least one example or piece of evidence.
Use grammar from this unit. Include at least one partitive expression, one conditional sentence, one relative clause, and one comparative or superlative.
Practise aloud. Rehearse your presentation until you can deliver it smoothly without reading from your notes. Focus on speaking clearly, making eye contact, and using appropriate gestures.
Deliver your presentation. Present to your teacher, your class, your family, or your AI tutor. Ask for feedback on your content, grammar, and delivery.
Pronunciation Focus
Several vocabulary words in this unit contain sounds that can be tricky for learners. Practise these words carefully:
exult (ig-ZULT) — the x makes a gz sound
oblique (oh-BLEEK) — stress is on the second syllable
pertinent (PUR-tih-nent) — three syllables, stress on the first
impassive (im-PASS-iv) — stress on the second syllable
monotonous (moh-NOT-oh-nus) — four syllables, stress on the second
restitution (res-tih-TOO-shun) — stress on the third syllable
exhilarating (ig-ZIL-uh-ray-ting) — five syllables, stress on the second

Writing

Writing is one of the most powerful ways to demonstrate your understanding of grammar, vocabulary, and ideas. In this section, you will complete several writing tasks that build on the themes and skills from this unit. Each task is designed to help you practise a specific aspect of your English.
Writing Task 1: Persuasive Paragraph

Write a persuasive paragraph (100 to 150 words) arguing either for or against the following statement: "Young people should be allowed to investigate and report on public policy issues." Your paragraph must include:
A clear topic sentence that states your position
At least two partitive expressions (e.g., a piece of evidence, a multitude of reasons)
At least one conditional sentence (zero or first conditional)
At least one relative clause
A strong concluding sentence that reinforces your argument
Writing Task 2: News Report in Passive Voice

Imagine you are a journalist reporting on the events in "Signal in the Static." Write a short news report (80 to 120 words) using the passive voice for at least five sentences. Your report should cover the key events: the anonymous message, the investigation, the deleted files, the broadcast, and the outcome. Here is an example to get you started:
"An anonymous message was discovered at Horizon Youth Radio. An investigation was launched by two students, Faisa and Ahmed..."
Writing Task 3: Comparative Essay

Write a short essay (150 to 200 words) comparing two characters from the story. You might compare Faisa and Ahmed, Faisa and Mahir, or Faisa and Mr. Kadir. Use comparatives and superlatives throughout your essay. Consider these questions:
Who is more courageous, and why?
Who is more cautious, and why?
Who has the strongest argument in the debate scene?
Who is the most important character in the story, and what makes them so?
Writing Task 4: Reflective Journal Entry

Write a reflective journal entry (100 to 150 words) about a time when you had to make a difficult decision. Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit and at least two different grammar structures (e.g., a partitive expression and a relative clause). Think about:
What was the situation?
What were your options?
How did you feel?
What did you decide, and what happened as a result?
What did you learn from the experience?
Writing Checklist
Before you submit any writing task, use this checklist to review your work:
Does your writing have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Have you used the required grammar structures (partitive expressions, conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives, passive voice)?
Have you included the required number of vocabulary words?
Is your spelling correct? Pay special attention to the vocabulary words.
Is your punctuation correct? Check commas in relative clauses and conditional sentences.
Does your writing make sense? Read it aloud to check for clarity.
Have you proofread for any repeated words or awkward sentences?

Or: Can you suggest a better topic sentence for my essay?
Activities and Exercises

This section contains a variety of activities that will help you practise and reinforce the grammar, vocabulary, and comprehension skills from this unit. Work through each activity carefully, and check your answers against the answer keys provided at the end of this section.
Activity 1: Partitive Expressions — Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct partitive expression from the box below.
Options: a piece of | a surge of | a multitude of | a bit of | a glass of | a handful of | a drop of | a lot of
Faisa felt __________ indignation when she learned about the secret deal.
Can I have __________ water, please? I am very thirsty.
After the broadcast, __________ listeners called the station to share their stories.
Ahmed needed __________ help fixing the computer after the files were deleted.
The detective found __________ evidence at the scene of the crime.
There was only __________ milk left in the fridge, so we could not make tea.
She picked up __________ almonds from the bowl and ate them quickly.
The students had __________ questions after the presentation, so the teacher stayed late.
Activity 2: Zero and First Conditionals — Match and Complete
Part A: Match the beginnings with the correct endings to form conditional sentences.
If you heat ice, __________. (a) I will bring an umbrella.
If it rains tomorrow, __________. (b) it melts.
If Faisa investigates the story, __________. (c) you get green.
If you mix blue and yellow, __________. (d) she will uncover the truth.
Part B: Complete each sentence with the correct verb form.
If you __________ (study) every day, you __________ (improve) your English quickly. (First conditional)
If water __________ (reach) 100 degrees, it __________ (boil). (Zero conditional)
If we __________ (intervene) now, we __________ (avert) the problem. (First conditional)
If you __________ (not eat) breakfast, you __________ (feel) tired by midmorning. (Zero conditional)
Activity 3: Relative Clauses — Combine the Sentences
Combine each pair of sentences into one sentence using a relative clause with who, that, or which.
Faisa runs a radio station. Faisa is a student at the school.
The document contained land permits. The document appeared in Ahmed's locker.
Mr. Kadir wore an impassive expression. Mr. Kadir is the deputy mayor's aide.
The broadcast received many calls. The broadcast discussed the garden story.
The vine grew towards the light. The vine had delicate tendrils.
Activity 4: Comparatives and Superlatives — Write the Correct Form
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
Faisa is __________ (brave) than Ahmed when it comes to asking difficult questions.
This was the __________ (exciting) broadcast the station had ever produced.
Mr. Kadir's answers were __________ (vague) than the students expected.
Of all the characters, Faisa is the __________ (determined) to find the truth.
The second version of the story was __________ (erratic) than the first.
Ahmed is the __________ (adept) person in the school at editing audio files.
Activity 5: Passive Voice — Rewrite the Sentences
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice. Remember the pattern: subject + was/were + past participle.
Someone deleted the files from the computer.
The mayor's office signed the land permits.
Faisa wrote an article for the school paper.
The students produced a powerful broadcast.
An anonymous source delivered the evidence to Ahmed.
Activity 6: Questions with Prepositions — Rewrite Formally
Rewrite each informal question in the formal style, with the preposition at the beginning.
Which perspective was the story told from?
Which student did you work with on the project?
Which school does she go to?
Which topic are you writing about?
Activity 7: Vocabulary in Context — Choose the Right Word
Choose the correct vocabulary word from the box to complete each sentence.
Options: exult | feign | obstruct | decipher | flourish | rebuke | avert | quell
The teacher had to __________ the students for talking during the exam.
After months of hard work, the business began to __________ and make a profit.
He tried to __________ interest in the conversation, but everyone could tell he was bored.
The heavy traffic seemed to __________ our route, and we arrived late.
She managed to __________ the crisis by acting quickly and calmly.
After winning the competition, the entire team began to __________ with joy.
It took hours to __________ the coded message, but eventually they understood it.

Self-Study Tips

Studying on your own can be challenging, but it is also incredibly rewarding. Here are some strategies that will help you get the most out of this unit and build strong study habits that will serve you well throughout your education.
Tip 1: Set Clear Goals for Each Study Session

Before you start studying, decide what you want to accomplish. For example, you might say, "Today I will learn five new vocabulary words and complete Activity 2." Having a clear goal helps you stay focused and gives you a sense of accomplishment when you finish. Write your goal at the top of a page in your notebook before you begin.
Tip 2: Use the Spaced Repetition Method
Research shows that you remember information better when you review it at increasing intervals. Instead of studying all 32 vocabulary words in one sitting, try this approach:
Day 1: Learn 8 new words. Write each one three times. Use each one in a sentence.
Day 2: Review yesterday's 8 words. Learn 8 more. Write and use them in sentences.
Day 3: Review all 16 words from Days 1 and 2. Learn 8 more.
Day 4: Review all 24 words. Learn the final 8.
Day 5: Review all 32 words. Test yourself by covering the definitions and trying to recall them.
Day 7: Review again. By now, most words should feel familiar.
Day 14: Final review. The words should be in your long-term memory.
Tip 3: Read Widely and Often

The more you read in English, the faster your vocabulary and grammar will improve. Try to read for at least 15 minutes every day. You can read anything that interests you — news articles, short stories, comic books, or even social media posts in English. As you read, look for examples of the grammar structures you are learning. When you find a partitive expression or a passive voice sentence in the wild, write it down in your notebook. This will help you see how real writers use these structures.
Tip 4: Practise Speaking Every Day

If you do not have someone to practise speaking with, use your AI tutor. Talk about your day, describe what you see around you, or discuss the story from this unit. The more you speak, the more natural English will feel. Do not worry about making mistakes — they are proof that you are learning. Try to use at least two or three vocabulary words from this unit in every conversation you have in English.
Tip 5: Keep a Learning Journal
At the end of each study session, write a short reflection in your journal. Answer these questions:
What did I learn today?
What was easy? What was difficult?
What do I want to review tomorrow?
How am I feeling about my progress?
Your learning journal is private — it is just for you. Writing about your learning helps you process what you have studied and identifies areas where you need more practice.
Tip 6: Connect New Knowledge to What You Already Know

When you learn a new word or grammar rule, try to connect it to something you already understand. For example, if you are learning the word "restitution" (giving back something that was lost or taken), you might think about a time when someone returned something to you, or you might connect it to the Arabic concept of reparation or the Somali value of restoring justice within a community. These personal connections make new knowledge stickier and easier to recall.

Signal in the Static
Year 8 — Unit 2 Story
About This Story
Genre: Action drama / political mystery
Setting: Present-day Hargeisa, Somaliland
Characters: Faisa Yusuf (student journalist), Ahmed (her co-host and editor), Mr. Kadir (deputy mayor's aide), Mahir (fellow student)
Themes: Truth, manipulation, power, civic responsibility, and the courage to ask questions
Moral: Do not follow every voice that shouts — decipher the truth for yourself.
💡 Vocabulary Watch
As you read, look for the vocabulary words from Unit 2.
They appear naturally throughout the story.
When you find one, pause and check whether you can define it from context.
Chapter 1: The Broadcast Begins
Faisa adjusted her headphones and leaned into the mic.
"This is Horizon Youth Radio — news and truth from Hargeisa's students, for the city's future. I'm Faisa Yusuf. Let's begin."
Behind the glass, Ahmed gave her a thumbs-up. The red "LIVE" light glowed.

They had been running the school radio station for almost a year now. What began as a project in journalism class had grown into a respected youth platform. They had covered water shortages, exam protests, and even a segment about restitution for families displaced by flooding.
Now, something new was unfolding — and it was bigger than anything they had faced.
Chapter 2: The Oblique Message
It began with a message slipped under the radio room door. No name. No signature.
"They're lying. What you're hearing about the city gardens? All fake. There's more beneath the soil than plants."
Ahmed thought it was a joke. "Someone trying to be dramatic."
Faisa, however, did not dismiss it. The handwriting was neat. The language was not emotional. In fact, it was calculated. Oblique, even. Suggestive, but not direct.
"I want to know more," she said.

Later that week, a new narrative emerged online: photos of government trucks near the northern edge of the public gardens. Some claimed the land was being sold to foreign developers. Others said it was just "renovation."
The official synopsis from the mayor's office?
"New irrigation pipelines. Community gardens to flourish."
But no plans had been released. No engineer names. No contractors. Just noise.
Chapter 3: Uncovering the Static
Faisa and Ahmed began to investigate. They interviewed gardeners, market sellers, even a retired city planner. Most had not heard anything official.
One vendor near the gardens whispered, "They say a man from Djibouti came with contracts. Big money. But the families living nearby were told nothing."
Faisa felt a surge of indignation. "Why wouldn't they announce it?"
"Because," the woman said, "some would fight back."
Chapter 4: The Interview
At school, Faisa managed to arrange an interview with the deputy mayor's aide, a man named Mr. Kadir. He was tall, calm, and wore an impassive expression throughout the discussion.
"So the city plans are public, yes?" Faisa asked.
He smiled faintly. "Of course."
"Can we see them?"
"They're being updated. The full report will be available next month."
Ahmed glanced at Faisa. A delay. Convenient.
"Some say nearby families are being pressured to move. Quietly."
"That is incorrect," Kadir said smoothly. "Those are baseless rumours."
"But who started the rumours?" Faisa pressed.
Kadir's expression did not change. "That's not pertinent."
Chapter 5: Student Debate
Back at school, the Horizon Debate Club hosted a special event:
"Should Youth Intervene in Public Policy?"
Faisa presented her argument: "If we don't ask questions, adults will write the future without us. We must intervene, investigate, and demand clarity."
A student named Mahir disagreed. "We aren't experts. If we get involved in everything, we'll only create confusion. Youth action can be a hindrance, not help."
Ahmed spoke next. "But what if we're the only ones who can spot what adults miss?"
Faisa concluded with power: "Sometimes we must quell our fear to challenge authority. Especially when silence is the goal."
Chapter 6: Signal Interference
That night, Ahmed was editing the next episode when his computer glitched. The files on the gardens had vanished. Just static remained.
"Someone accessed the system," he told Faisa, shocked. "They wiped it."
She stared at the blank timeline on the screen. "That's not a mistake. That's a warning."
Then came the call.
A man's voice. Masked. Modulated.
"Stop pushing. You're kids. Don't make this worse. Not everything is your business."
Faisa's throat tightened. "What if we keep going?"
"Then you'll forfeit more than files."
The line went dead.
Chapter 7: The Decision
Ahmed was shaken. "This is serious. If we keep digging, we could get in trouble."
"But if we stop, we let them usurp the truth."
Faisa looked around their tiny studio. She loved this room — the old microphones, the battered table, even the wall where vines climbed from a pot and sent out delicate tendrils toward the light.
The moment was quiet. Monotonous, almost. But beneath it pulsed a choice.
"I say we go live tomorrow. Not with conclusions. Just with questions."
Ahmed hesitated. Then nodded. "Alright."
Chapter 8: Broadcast
The next day, they went on air.
"Today's episode is different," Faisa began. "We won't tell you what's true. We'll tell you what we found — and let you decide."
They played interviews, quoted vendors, and described the missing documents.
They even aired a segment titled "From which perspective is the garden story being told?" — a lesson in bias and source-checking.
"We're not accusing anyone," Ahmed said. "But we are asking why so many answers have gone dark."
As they wrapped up, the studio line lit up. Call after call.
"I'm a teacher," one voice said. "I've heard the same rumours. Thank you for speaking up."
Another said, "I work at city hall. You're not wrong."
Faisa exulted — silently. No cheering. Just a strong breath. A moment of pride.
Chapter 9: Retaliation
Days later, the radio station's power cut during their morning slot. The school's admin claimed it was a generator issue.
Students began receiving fake emails saying the station was shutting down.
A few teachers suggested Faisa and Ahmed had gone too far.
"You're students," one said. "Stay in your lane."
But they did not stop.
Faisa wrote a piece titled "Who Decides What's Pertinent?" for the school paper. It included screenshots, quotes, and passive voice to mask sources.
"The gardens were promised. The questions were raised. The files were deleted. And the truth? Still unclear."
Chapter 10: The Turning Point
A week later, a surprise.
A new document appeared in Ahmed's locker. Anonymous again.
But this time, it was real: a scanned analysis of land permits, with signatures from the mayor's office and a private firm.
They had proof.
The garden land had been leased — quietly. No community vote. No consultation.
Faisa stared at the papers, heart pounding.
"We need to decipher this," she said. "Every line."
They did. Then they aired a new show. No shouting. Just evidence.
They used comparatives to describe conflicting stories:
"One version is louder, another quieter. One is cleaner, the other more erratic."
"But truth," Faisa ended, "is not always the most luminous. Sometimes, it hides in whispers — until someone asks the right question."
Chapter 11: A Victory, and the Price
The city responded.
An official investigation began. The deputy mayor resigned. Some families received formal apologies and early compensation.
They were not heroes. But they were heard.
At the school's year-end ceremony, Faisa was invited to read a message.
She simply said: "Youth are not a hindrance. We are a mirror. We reflect what adults refuse to see."
The crowd stood. Applauded. Not all, but enough.
She and Ahmed did not feel triumphant. They felt steady.
The kind of joy you do not shout about.
The kind you earn.
💡 After Reading
Look back through the story and find all the vocabulary words that were highlighted in bold.
Can you identify examples of partitive expressions, conditionals, relative clauses, and passive voice?
Discuss with your AI tutor: What is the moral of this story, and how does it connect to your own life?

tables: To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory.
After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.
To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone.
I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.
To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.
To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.
To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad.
The teacher had to intervene when the students started arguing.
To block something so that it cannot move or be seen.
The fallen tree obstructed the road, making it hard for cars to pass.
To prevent something bad from happening.
She turned her head to avert her eyes from the bright light.
To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something.
After the heavy rain, the streets were inundated with water.
To grow or develop in a healthy or strong way.
The plants in the garden flourish when they get enough sunlight and water.
To figure out or understand something that is difficult to read or understand.
I had to decipher the secret message written in code.
To speak to someone in a way that shows you are angry because they did something wrong.
The teacher had to rebuke the student for not doing his homework.
To stop something, especially a feeling or situation, from becoming worse.
The teacher tried to quell the students' excitement before the school trip.
A story or description of events that tells what happened.
The narrative of the book was exciting and kept me interested until the end.
A short summary of a story or text that gives the main points.
The teacher asked us to write a synopsis of the book we read in class.
A disagreement or discussion where people express different opinions.
During the debate, the students had a strong argument about which book was better.
The process of studying something carefully to understand it better.
The analysis of the book helped us understand the main themes.
A large number of people or things.
There was a multitude of stars in the night sky.
The act of giving back something that was lost or taken away.
After the mistake, the company made restitution by refunding the money.
Something that makes it difficult for you to do something.
The rain was a hindrance to our outdoor game.
A thin, curly part of a plant that helps it climb or hold onto something.
The vine wrapped its tendril around the fence to climb higher.
Very skilled or good at something.
She is adept at playing the piano and can play many songs.
Not showing any emotion or feeling; calm and controlled.
Even when he heard the bad news, he remained impassive.
Someone who likes to talk and spend time with other people.
Maria is very sociable; she enjoys meeting new friends at school.
Something that is suggested or understood without being directly stated.
Her smile had an implicit meaning that she was happy.
Riding the roller coaster was an exhilarating experience.
Related to the topic or important for understanding something.
The teacher asked us to share pertinent information about our project.
The weather has been erratic, changing from sunny to rainy quickly.
Giving off light; bright or shining.
The stars in the night sky are luminous and beautiful.
Not straight or direct; slanted or angled.
The teacher drew an oblique line on the board.
Boring because it is always the same and never changes.
The speaker's voice was so monotonous that students struggled to stay awake.
c. To feel very happy after a success
e. To grow in a healthy way

paragraphs: Grammar  --  Unit 3: Community and Environment
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Four grammar lessons with exercises

This file covers four important grammar topics for Unit 3. Work through one lesson at a time. Read the explanation, study the examples, and then complete the exercises. Check your answers using the answer keys at the end of each section. Say the example sentences out loud -- grammar sticks better when you hear it!
Lesson 1: Clauses Ending with Prepositions
What it means and why it matters

A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. A preposition is a small word like in, on, at, from, about, to, or with that shows a relationship between words. In English, it is perfectly natural and correct to end a clause with a preposition. Understanding this pattern will help you speak and write more fluently.
For example, instead of saying the very formal "From which city did you come?" we naturally say "Which city did you come from?" The preposition "from" sits comfortably at the end.
How to form it (step by step)
Identify the preposition in your sentence. Common prepositions include: in, on, at, from, about, to, with, for, of.
Form your clause with a subject and verb: "we speak", "I came", "you are thinking".
Place the preposition at the end of the clause: "These are the issues we speak about."
Check it sounds natural. If it flows in conversation, it is correct.
Examples
"Here's the city I came from."
"These are the issues we speak about."
"That's the friend I was talking to."
"This is the restaurant we went to last night."
"I don't know what you are thinking about."
"She's someone you can depend on."
"Is this the place you were looking for?"
"This is what we look away from." (from the story)
Formal vs. informal
In very formal writing, some people prefer to move the preposition before the relative pronoun. Both forms are correct, but the informal version sounds more natural in everyday English.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Trying to force the preposition to the front, making the sentence sound awkward.
Wrong: "About what are we speaking?"
Better: "What are we speaking about?"
Mistake 2: Dropping the preposition entirely.
Wrong: "This is the friend I was talking." (missing "to")
Correct: "This is the friend I was talking to."
Memory tip
Think of the common phrase "Where are you at?" It ends with a preposition, and everyone understands it perfectly. If it sounds natural at the end, leave it there.
Exercise 1A: Fill in the missing preposition
Complete each sentence by adding the correct preposition at the end.
That's the movie I was waiting ______.
This is the place we always go ______ for ice cream.
Here's the book I learned a lot ______.
She's the friend I like to hang out ______.
What are you talking ______?
Who did you give the book ______?
That's the problem we need to think ______.
This is the school I graduated ______.
Exercise 1B: Rewrite in natural English
These sentences use the formal structure. Rewrite them so the preposition comes at the end of the clause.
"This is the book about which I was talking."
"To whom did you send the letter?"
"For what are you looking?"
"From which country does she come?"
Answer Key -- Exercise 1A
for
to
from
with
about
to
about
from
Answer Key -- Exercise 1B
"This is the book I was talking about."
"Who did you send the letter to?"
"What are you looking for?"
"Which country does she come from?"
Lesson 2: The Second Conditional
What it means and why it matters

The second conditional is used to talk about imaginary or unlikely situations in the present or future. It helps you express dreams, wishes, and hypothetical scenarios. When you say "If I were a bird, I would fly," you are not actually a bird -- you are imagining what you would do in that unreal situation.
How to form it
The structure is:
If + subject + past simple verb, subject + would + base form of the verb.
Notice that we use the past tense in the "if" clause, but we are talking about the present or future, not the past. This is what makes the second conditional special.

Important: For the verb "be", always use were for all subjects (I, you, he, she, it, we, they). This is one of the few places in English where "were" is used instead of "was", even with singular subjects.
Examples
"If I were rich, I would buy a big house."
"If we were more aware, we would protect more." (from the story)
"If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger." (from the story)
"If the park were safer, more people would come."
"If she could fly, she would travel the world."
"If every town had a Zawadi, what would the coastlines of Africa look like?" (from the story)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using "was" instead of "were".
Wrong: "If I was rich, I would buy a car."
Correct: "If I were rich, I would buy a car."
Mistake 2: Forgetting "would" in the result clause.
Wrong: "If I were happy, I smile."
Correct: "If I were happy, I would smile."
Mistake 3: Using the second conditional for real or likely situations. Use the first conditional instead.
Wrong: "If it rains tomorrow, I would stay home." (Rain tomorrow is possible.)
Correct: "If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home." (first conditional)
Correct: "If it rained every day, I would move to a desert." (second conditional -- unlikely)
Memory tip
Think of "If I were..." as opening a door to a world of imagination. When you step through that door, you enter a place where anything is possible -- but only in your mind.
Exercise 2A: Complete the sentences
Fill in the blanks using the correct form of the second conditional.
If I ______ (be) a superhero, I ______ (help) everyone.
If our school ______ (have) a swimming pool, we ______ (swim) every day.
If my friend ______ (be) here, we ______ (play) together.
If the ocean ______ (be) cleaner, more fish ______ (live) in it.
If you ______ (have) a million dollars, what ______ you (do)?
If Zawadi ______ (not start) Coastal Rise, the bay ______ still (be) polluted.
Exercise 2B: Write your own
Write three second conditional sentences about things you imagine or wish for. Use the structure: If + past simple, would + base verb.
If I were ______, I would ______.
If my community had ______, we would ______.
If I could ______, I would ______.
Answer Key -- Exercise 2A
were; would help
had; would swim
were; would play
were; would live
had; would ... do
had not started; would still be
Lesson 3: Verbs and Adjectives Followed by Prepositions
What it means and why it matters

In English, many verbs and adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. Using the wrong preposition can confuse your listener or change the meaning. There is no simple rule for which preposition goes with which word -- you must learn them as fixed pairs. The good news is that the more you read and listen, the more natural these combinations will feel.
Common verb + preposition combinations
Common adjective + preposition combinations
Using -ing after prepositions
When a verb comes after a preposition, it always takes the -ing form (the gerund). This is an important rule that applies every time.
"I am interested in volunteering." (not: interested in volunteer)
"She is tired of waiting." (not: tired of wait)
"He apologised for being late." (not: apologised for be late)
"They are good at solving problems." (not: good at solve problems)
"Thank you for helping us." (not: Thank you for help us)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong preposition.
Wrong: "I am interested on learning."
Correct: "I am interested in learning."
Mistake 2: Forgetting the preposition entirely.
Wrong: "I am known being articulate."
Correct: "I am known for being articulate."
Mistake 3: Using the base verb instead of -ing after a preposition.
Wrong: "She is good at solve problems."
Correct: "She is good at solving problems."
Memory tip
Make flashcards with the verb or adjective on one side and the correct preposition on the other. Test yourself daily. You can also ask your AI tutor to quiz you on these pairs.

Answer Key -- Exercise 3A
in
for
with
of
of
on
to
for
of
for
Answer Key -- Exercise 3B

waiting
arriving

helping

Lesson 4: The Present Continuous Passive
What it means and why it matters

The present continuous passive is used when you want to talk about an action that is happening right now, but the focus is on what is receiving the action, not on who is doing it. This structure is very useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from context.
Compare:
Active: The volunteers are cleaning the beach. (focus on who is doing it)
Passive: The beach is being cleaned. (focus on the beach itself)
How to form it
The structure is:
Subject + am/is/are + being + past participle
Use is with singular subjects (he, she, it, the mural). Use are with plural subjects (they, the murals, new bins).
Examples
"New bins are being affixed along the beach paths." (from the story)
"Murals are being painted by the schoolchildren."
"A community event is being commemorated."
"The books are being read by the students."
"The park is being cleaned by volunteers."
"Letters are being delivered right now."
Adding the doer with "by"
You can add who is doing the action using by, but only if this information is important or interesting.
"New murals are being painted by the schoolchildren."
"The shoreline is being restored by the Coastal Rise team."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong form of "to be".
Wrong: "New mural is being painted." ("mural" is singular, but the original says "murals")
Correct: "New murals are being painted."
Mistake 2: Forgetting "being".
Wrong: "New murals are painted." (This is simple present passive, not continuous.)
Correct: "New murals are being painted." (This shows the action is happening now.)
Mistake 3: Using the wrong form of the main verb.
Wrong: "The park is being cleaning."
Correct: "The park is being cleaned." (Use the past participle, not -ing.)
Memory tip
Think of "being" as a bridge between "is/are" and the past participle. It tells you the action is happening right now and the focus is on what is being acted upon.
Exercise 4A: Rewrite in the present continuous passive
Change each active sentence to the present continuous passive.
The volunteers are cleaning the park.
The artists are painting new murals on the walls.
The students are reading the books in the library.
The chef is decorating the cake for the party.
Workers are delivering letters to every house.
The community is organising a new recycling programme.
Exercise 4B: Fill in the blanks
Complete each sentence using the present continuous passive form of the verb in brackets.
The letters ____________ (deliver) right now.
A new song ____________ (write) by the school choir.
The house ____________ (paint) blue.
New trees ____________ (plant) along the road.
A documentary ____________ (film) about our community.
Clean-up events ____________ (organise) by Coastal Rise.
Answer Key -- Exercise 4A
The park is being cleaned (by the volunteers).
New murals are being painted on the walls (by the artists).
The books are being read in the library (by the students).
The cake is being decorated for the party (by the chef).
Letters are being delivered to every house (by workers).
A new recycling programme is being organised (by the community).
Answer Key -- Exercise 4B
are being delivered
is being written
is being painted
are being planted
is being filmed
are being organised
Bonus: Formal Language Structures
"It is imperative that..."
This formal phrase means "it is very important that" and is used when you want to stress urgency or necessity. In the story, Zawadi uses it in her speech:
"It is imperative that we not let Kilimo Bay become only a memory."
Other examples:
"It is imperative that we protect our environment."
"It is imperative that every student reads this notice."
"It is imperative that we act quickly."
"I'd like to commemorate..."
This polite, formal phrase is used to honour or remember something important. In the story, the mayor says:
"I'd like to commemorate this community."
Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on verb + preposition combinations?
Can you give me more examples of the second conditional?
Can you help me practise changing active sentences to the present continuous passive?
What is the difference between the first conditional and the second conditional?
