paragraphs: Unit 3: Community and Environment
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-paced lesson
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has all the content you need to study for Unit 3, organised into clear sections.
You also have three companion files: the Story ("Rising Tide"), the Grammar Reference, and the Vocabulary Word Book.
Study a little every day. Read actively, write in your notebook, and speak English out loud. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your questions to your teacher sessions twice a week.
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 3: Community and Environment. In this unit, you will explore how young people can make a difference in their communities by protecting the environment. You will read a story called "Rising Tide" about a girl named Zawadi who leads a youth movement to clean up a polluted bay in a coastal Kenyan city. Through this story and the activities in this lesson, you will build your vocabulary, sharpen your grammar skills, and develop your ability to speak and write persuasively about issues that matter to you.

The theme of this unit -- community and environment -- is directly relevant to your life. Whether you live near the coast, in a city, or in a rural area, environmental issues affect everyone. Learning to express your ideas about these issues clearly and persuasively is a valuable skill that will serve you well beyond this English course.

You will learn how to use clauses that end with prepositions to sound more natural in English, how to use the second conditional to talk about imaginary situations and possibilities, how to use the present continuous passive to describe actions in progress, and how to combine verbs and adjectives with the correct prepositions. These grammar skills will make your English more fluent and sophisticated.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a complex narrative about community activism and environmental protection.
Use 33 new vocabulary words accurately in speaking and writing.
Construct clauses that end with prepositions naturally and correctly.
Form and use second conditional sentences to express imaginary situations and wishes.
Identify and create present continuous passive sentences to describe ongoing actions.
Combine verbs and adjectives with their correct prepositions, including using the -ing form after prepositions.
Write a persuasive speech or formal letter about an environmental issue in your community.
Speak confidently about community problems and propose solutions using the grammar structures from this unit.
Understand different perspectives on environmental issues and express your own viewpoint.
Use formal language structures such as "It is imperative that..." in appropriate contexts.
Vocabulary Preview

Before you begin reading and working through the activities, take a look at the key vocabulary for this unit. You will encounter these words throughout the story, grammar exercises, and activities. Open your Vocabulary companion file for full definitions, examples, and practice exercises. Here is a quick preview of the words grouped by type.
Verbs

stifle -- to stop something from happening or developing | wane -- to become less over time | commemorate -- to remember and honour | articulate -- to express clearly | hasten -- to make faster | advocate -- to publicly support (also a noun) | affix -- to attach | facilitate -- to make easier | illuminate -- to light up or make clear | brandish -- to wave or hold excitedly
Nouns

potential -- ability to develop | exasperation -- strong frustration | misgiving -- doubt or worry | perspective -- a way of thinking | debris -- broken pieces or waste | banter -- playful conversation
Adjectives

gaunt -- very thin and weak | coherent -- clear and logical | omniscient -- all-knowing | dire -- very serious | sporadic -- irregular, not steady | amiable -- friendly | dejected -- sad and disappointed | telltale -- revealing | emaciated -- extremely thin from lack of food | tedious -- boring and long | succinct -- brief and clear | rational -- logical, sensible | durable -- long-lasting | exuberant -- full of energy and joy | imperative -- extremely important (also a noun)
💡 Study Tip
Do not try to memorise all 33 words at once. Learn 5-6 new words each day.
For each word, read the definition, say it out loud, and write your own sentence.
Keep a vocabulary notebook. Write each new word, its definition, and your sentence.
Review yesterday's words before learning today's new ones.
Grammar Introduction

This unit focuses on four grammar topics that will help you express yourself more naturally and precisely in English. Open your Grammar companion file for detailed explanations, step-by-step instructions, and practice exercises with answer keys. Here is an overview of what you will learn.
1. Clauses ending with prepositions
In natural English, it is perfectly correct to end a clause with a preposition. This sounds more natural than moving the preposition to the front of the sentence. For example:
Natural: "That's the city I came from."
Formal: "That is the city from which I came."
Natural: "This is what we look away from."
In the story, Zawadi uses this structure when she says: "That's the city I came from." It sounds natural and confident.
2. The second conditional

The second conditional lets you talk about imaginary or unlikely situations. It uses the structure: If + past simple, would + base verb. You will use this to discuss what could happen if circumstances were different.
"If we were more aware, we would protect more."
"If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger."
"If every town had a Zawadi, what would the coastlines look like?"
3. Verbs and adjectives + prepositions

Many English verbs and adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. Using the wrong one can confuse your listener. You will learn common combinations and practise using the -ing form after prepositions.
"I am interested in volunteering." (not: interested on)
"She is known for being articulate." (not: known of)
"He is frustrated with the slow progress." (not: frustrated about)
4. The present continuous passive
This structure focuses on what is happening to the subject right now, rather than who is doing it. The form is: subject + am/is/are + being + past participle.
"New bins are being affixed along the beach paths."
"Murals are being painted by schoolchildren."
"A documentary is being filmed about our community."

Part 1: Reading and Listening
Reading: "Rising Tide"

Open your Story companion file and read "Rising Tide" carefully. This is the central text of Unit 3. It tells the story of Zawadi, a young woman in a Kenyan coastal city who leads a youth movement called Coastal Rise to clean up the polluted Kilimo Bay. The story is rich with vocabulary words and grammar structures from this unit.
Before you begin reading, set a purpose. Think about these questions:
What environmental problem does Zawadi face?
How does she decide to tackle it?
What role do art and communication play in solving the problem?
How does Zawadi grow as a person throughout the story?

As you read, keep a notebook beside you. When you encounter a vocabulary word from this unit, write it down along with the sentence it appears in. This will help you understand how the words are used in context.
Reading strategy: Identifying grammar in context
One of the most effective ways to learn grammar is to see it used in real texts. As you read "Rising Tide", look for examples of the four grammar structures:
Clauses ending with prepositions: Look for sentences where a preposition appears at the end, such as "That's the city I came from."
Second conditional: Look for "if" sentences that describe imaginary situations, such as "If we were more aware, we would protect more."
Present continuous passive: Look for sentences using "is being" or "are being" + past participle, such as "New bins are being affixed along the beach paths."
Verb/adjective + preposition: Look for combinations like "interested in", "tired of", "known for", and "proud of".
Write down at least two examples of each grammar structure that you find in the story. You will use these examples in the activities below.
Listening activity

Ask your AI tutor to read sections of the story aloud to you. Listen carefully to the pronunciation of the vocabulary words and the rhythm of the grammar structures. Then try reading the same sections aloud yourself.
Focus on these passages for your listening practice:
Zawadi's speech at the gallery (starting with "It is imperative...").
The conversation between Zawadi and the girl at the school visit ("If the ocean is sick...").
Zawadi's speech at the discussion panel ("My name is Zawadi...").
After listening, answer these questions:
Which vocabulary words did you hear? List at least eight.
Did you notice any second conditional sentences? Write them down.
Which sentences ended with a preposition?
Post-reading comprehension
Answer these questions in full sentences in your notebook.
What is Kilimo Bay, and why is it important to Zawadi?
What is Coastal Rise, and what is its mission?
Why does Jamal suggest creating a photo gallery? What effect does it have?
In her gallery speech, Zawadi says it is "imperative" that they act. What does she mean, and why does she use formal language?
How does the community change after the gallery event? Give at least three specific examples.
What does the mural say, and what does it mean?
At the panel discussion, Zawadi says, "We weren't experts. Just youth with a view. A perspective. That was enough to start." What message does this send to other young people?
The story ends with the question: "If every town had a Zawadi, what would the coastlines of Africa look like?" How would you answer this question?

Part 2: Speaking
Speaking Activity 1: Discussing the story
Practise discussing the story with your AI tutor or a study partner. Use these prompts to guide your conversation. Try to speak for at least one minute on each topic.
Describe the problem in Kilimo Bay. What caused it, and how did it affect the community?
Explain how Zawadi solved the problem. What steps did she take?
Which character in the story do you admire most, and why?
Do you agree that art can help solve community problems? Give reasons for your answer.
As you speak, try to use vocabulary words from this unit. For example, instead of saying "the beach was dirty," you could say "the beach was covered in debris and looked gaunt from neglect."
Speaking Activity 2: The second conditional debate
Use the second conditional to discuss imaginary scenarios with your AI tutor. Take turns asking and answering these questions.
If you were the mayor of your town, what would you change about the environment?
If your school had an environmental club, what projects would you organise?
If every family reduced their plastic waste by half, what would happen to the local environment?
If you could travel anywhere in the world to see a natural wonder, where would you go and why?
If clean water were available to everyone, how would life change?
Speaking Activity 3: Mini-speech
Prepare a one-minute speech on one of these topics. Use at least three vocabulary words and at least one grammar structure from this unit.
"Why our community needs a clean-up project"
"If I were in charge of protecting the environment..."
"How young people can make a difference"
Structure your speech like this:
Opening: State your main idea clearly. You could begin with "It is imperative that..."
Body: Give two or three supporting points with examples.
Closing: End with a powerful question or a call to action.
Practise your speech several times. Record yourself if possible. Listen back and check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use vocabulary and grammar from this unit?
Speaking Activity 4: Role-play
With your AI tutor acting as one character, role-play one of these scenarios:
You are Zawadi giving a talk at a new school. The AI tutor plays a student asking questions about Coastal Rise.
You are a journalist interviewing Zawadi after the panel discussion. Ask about her journey and what she learned.
You are presenting a proposal to the mayor for a new environmental project. The AI tutor plays the mayor.

Part 3: Writing
Writing Task 1: Descriptive paragraph

Write a descriptive paragraph (6--8 sentences) about an environmental problem in your community or a place you know. It could be litter on the streets, pollution in a river, deforestation, or any other issue.
Your paragraph should include:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit (underline them).
At least one clause ending with a preposition (e.g. "This is the river we used to swim in.").
A description of what the place looks like now and what it used to look like.

Example opening: "The river near our neighbourhood is not the river my grandmother talks about. There is debris along the banks, and the water carries a telltale smell of pollution. This is the river we once swam in and fished from."
Writing Task 2: Formal letter or persuasive speech
Choose one of the following tasks:
Option A: Formal letter. Write a formal letter to your local council or school principal, proposing an environmental project for your community. Your letter should:
Use the formal letter structure (your address, date, recipient's address, greeting, body, closing, signature).
Include at least one sentence using "It is imperative that..."
Include at least two second conditional sentences (e.g. "If the council provided bins, we would reduce litter.").
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
Be clear, coherent, and succinct.
Option B: Persuasive speech. Write a persuasive speech (150--200 words) that could be delivered at a school assembly about protecting the environment. Your speech should:
Open with a strong statement (e.g. "It is imperative that we act now.").
Use at least two second conditional sentences to describe what could change.
Include at least one present continuous passive sentence (e.g. "Trees are being cut down every day.").
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
End with a call to action.
Writing Task 3: Diary entry

Imagine you are Zawadi on the evening after the gallery event. Write a diary entry (100--150 words) describing how you feel, what happened, and what you hope will happen next. Use first person ("I") and try to include:
At least three vocabulary words (e.g. exasperation, potential, advocate).
At least one second conditional sentence (e.g. "If more people had come, we would have raised even more awareness.").
Reflection on how the evening changed your perspective.
Writing Task 4: Creative extension

Write a short sequel to "Rising Tide" (150--200 words). Imagine what happens to Zawadi and Coastal Rise one year later. Does the movement grow? Does Zawadi face new challenges? How does the bay look now?
Your sequel should use:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit.
At least two different grammar structures from this unit.
A satisfying conclusion that shows growth or change.

Part 4: Activities and Exercises
Activity 1: Vocabulary bingo

Create a bingo card with 16 squares (4 x 4). Write a different vocabulary word from this unit in each square. Ask your AI tutor to read definitions aloud. When you hear the definition of a word on your card, cross it out. The first person to cross out a full row or column wins.
Activity 2: Grammar transformation
Transform these active sentences into the present continuous passive.
The council is building a new recycling centre. --> ______
Students are planting trees in the school garden. --> ______
The mayor is opening a new community centre. --> ______
Workers are repairing the road. --> ______
Volunteers are distributing food to families. --> ______
Now transform these formal sentences into natural English by moving the preposition to the end.
"This is the community in which we live." --> ______
"About what are you concerned?" --> ______
"For whom did you make this poster?" --> ______
Activity 3: Second conditional chain
This is a fun speaking and writing game. Start with a second conditional sentence, then use the result to start the next sentence.
Example:
"If I had more time, I would volunteer at the beach."
"If I volunteered at the beach, I would meet other young activists."
"If I met other young activists, we would start a community project."
Continue the chain for at least five sentences. Try to make your chain tell a story.
Activity 4: Preposition challenge
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition. Then identify whether the word before the preposition is a verb or an adjective.
She is proud ______ her community's progress. (verb / adjective?)
I am looking ______ volunteers to join the project. (verb / adjective?)
They are responsible ______ keeping the park clean. (verb / adjective?)
We need to think ______ the consequences. (verb / adjective?)
He is interested ______ studying marine biology. (verb / adjective?)
She apologised ______ arriving late. (verb / adjective?)
The students are tired ______ writing the same exercises. (verb / adjective?)
Please listen ______ the announcement carefully. (verb / adjective?)
Activity 5: Story-based grammar hunt
Go back to the story "Rising Tide" and complete this challenge:
Find three clauses that end with a preposition. Write them down.
Find four second conditional sentences. Write them down and explain what imaginary situation each one describes.
Find two present continuous passive sentences. Rewrite each one in the active voice.
Find five verb/adjective + preposition combinations. Use each one in a new sentence of your own.
Activity 6: Community poster project
Design a poster for an environmental campaign in your community. Your poster should include:
A bold, succinct slogan (like the ones in the story: "No More Plastic!" or "Our Bay, Our Future").
A short paragraph (3--4 sentences) explaining the campaign. Use at least three vocabulary words.
A call to action using formal language (e.g. "It is imperative that we act now.").
Answer Key -- Activity 2
Present continuous passive transformations:
A new recycling centre is being built (by the council).
Trees are being planted in the school garden (by students).
A new community centre is being opened (by the mayor).
The road is being repaired (by workers).
Food is being distributed to families (by volunteers).
Natural English rewrites:
"This is the community we live in."
"What are you concerned about?"
"Who did you make this poster for?"

Self-Study Tips
Studying English on your own is a skill in itself. Here are some strategies that will help you get the most out of this unit and every unit that follows.
Tip 1: Active reading

Do not just read passively. When you read the story or any text, have a pen and notebook ready. Underline vocabulary words, circle grammar structures, and write questions in the margin. After reading, summarise what you read in two or three sentences. This forces your brain to process the information actively.
Tip 2: Speak every day

Even if you are studying alone, speak English out loud every day. Read passages from the story aloud. Practise your speeches. Talk to your AI tutor in English. The more you use your voice, the more natural English will feel. Try to speak for at least ten minutes every day.
Tip 3: Write regularly

Writing is one of the best ways to consolidate grammar and vocabulary. Try to write something in English every day, even if it is just a few sentences. You could keep a daily journal where you describe what happened that day, using vocabulary words from your current unit.
Tip 4: Use spaced repetition

Do not try to learn everything in one session. Spread your learning over several days. Review words you learned yesterday before learning new ones today. This technique, called spaced repetition, is proven to help your brain move information from short-term to long-term memory.
Tip 5: Connect new words to your life

When you learn a new word, think about how it connects to your own experience. For example, when you learn "exasperation", think of a time you felt exasperated. When you learn "advocate", think about what cause you would advocate for. Making personal connections helps you remember words much better than memorising definitions alone.
Tip 6: Use your AI tutor effectively

Your AI tutor is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Use it as a conversation partner, a vocabulary quiz master, a grammar checker, and a writing assistant. Do not be shy -- ask as many questions as you need. There is no such thing as a silly question when you are learning.

Day 1: Vocabulary -- Verbs

Day 4: Story -- First reading

Day 5: Story -- Second reading and grammar spotting

Read the story again, this time looking specifically for grammar structures and vocabulary words. Complete the Vocabulary Spotting and Grammar Spotting exercises in the Story file. Write down examples in your notebook.
Day 6: Grammar -- Lessons 1 and 2

Work through the Grammar file, Lessons 1 (Clauses ending with prepositions) and 2 (Second conditional). Read the explanations, study the examples, and complete the exercises. Check your answers with the answer keys.
Day 7: Grammar -- Lessons 3 and 4
Work through Grammar Lessons 3 (Verbs/Adjectives + Prepositions) and 4 (Present continuous passive). Complete all exercises and check your answers.
Day 8: Speaking practice
Complete Speaking Activities 1 through 4 from this lesson file. Practise with your AI tutor. Focus on using vocabulary and grammar naturally in conversation.
Day 9: Writing tasks
Choose at least two writing tasks from Part 3 of this lesson. Write them in your notebook or type them. Ask your AI tutor to check your grammar and vocabulary usage.
Day 10: Activities, review, and self-assessment
Complete the activities in Part 4. Do the vocabulary reinforcement exercises. Then complete the Self-Assessment at the end of this file to check your progress.

Story  --  Unit 3: Community and Environment
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "Rising Tide"
📖 About this story
Genre: Realistic fiction with elements of reflection and light adventure.
Theme: Facing community challenges with thoughtfulness and resilience.
Setting: A coastal city in Kenya with vibrant neighbourhoods and youth-led clean-up projects.
Moral: Thoughtful action and clear communication can change communities. Sometimes, small voices carry the most power.

As you read, look for vocabulary words from your word list and grammar structures from your grammar file. You will find clauses ending with prepositions, second conditional sentences, present continuous passive, and verbs/adjectives followed by prepositions.
Rising Tide

Zawadi stood by the sea wall, watching the debris dance on the waves below. Plastic wrappers, torn fishing nets, and a cracked sandal floated along the surface, swirling in what had once been the pride of her city.

Kilimo Bay used to be clear and full of fish. Now, on many mornings, it smelled of old oil and soggy cardboard. As she pulled her braids into a bun, Zawadi's eyes narrowed. This was not the future her grandfather had spoken of. She remembered his words: "This ocean has potential far greater than gold -- if only we care for it."

The youth group she led, Coastal Rise, had begun a campaign to clean the shoreline. But progress had been sporadic, and support from the city council had started to wane. She could feel the exasperation building in her chest each time they held a meeting and nothing changed.
"This place used to shine," came a voice behind her.
Zawadi turned to see Jamal, her younger cousin and an amateur photographer. He was tall, with an easy smile and an amiable way of making anyone feel at home.
"I know. But knowing isn't enough. We need more people," Zawadi replied. She looked down at the water again. "We need to act."

Jamal adjusted the camera slung over his shoulder. "Then let's hasten things. What if we created a gallery? Show the bay -- before and after. Real people, real stories. Maybe that will get their attention."
Zawadi blinked. That idea had potential. It could facilitate real dialogue. But doubts tugged at her.
"What if no one comes?" she said quietly, trying to stifle her misgivings.
Jamal shrugged. "If we don't try, we won't know."

The next week, the school auditorium was transformed. The Coastal Rise Gallery was open to all. Jamal's photos lined the walls, from wide shots of the gaunt, trash-laden beach to portraits of children playing near piles of debris. The lighting was low, except for focused spotlights that seemed to illuminate each frame.
Zawadi had written short, succinct captions beneath each photo. They weren't long, but they struck a chord.
"Here's the bay I grew up beside."
"This is what remains after the crowds leave."
"She's the girl we forgot."
People came. Students. Elders. Local artists. Even the mayor.
One old man paused in front of a picture of a child holding a bottle like a toy. "That's my grandson," he whispered. There was dejection in his voice.
A woman murmured, "Why didn't I see this before?"
Zawadi stood at the edge, nervous. Would anyone listen when she spoke? She wasn't a politician. She was just... Zawadi.
Jamal nudged her gently. "You ready to be articulate?"
She stepped forward.

"It is imperative," she began, voice clear, "that we not let Kilimo Bay become only a memory. This is the place we swim, fish, celebrate, dream. But it's vanishing -- because of what we throw away, and because of what we look away from."
She paused.
"If we act now, we can still save it. If we were more aware, we would protect more. If we had stronger voices, maybe we'd be heard."
She spoke slowly, clearly, using every grammar rule her teachers had tried to instil. A clause ended in a preposition. She smiled to herself.
Over the next month, something shifted.
New bins were being affixed along the beach paths. School clubs began hosting weekend clean-ups. Elders told stories at bonfires about the days the bay had been so clear you could see crabs dancing.
The mayor visited again. "I'd like to commemorate this community," she said during a small ceremony. "You reminded us what we forgot."
Zawadi felt warmth in her chest. She'd become an advocate not by shouting, but by being steady. Logical. Rational. Her voice had found its place.

One Saturday, as she walked the bay with Jamal, they passed a mural painted by schoolchildren. It showed waves rising into hands that caught the light. Underneath, someone had written: "Protect what you rise from."
Zawadi smiled. "That's the city I came from."

They decided to take the movement further. With support from new sponsors, Coastal Rise began organising educational events. Zawadi gave talks at other schools, where children asked curious, clever questions.
"If the ocean is sick, does that mean the fish get sick too?" one girl asked.
"Yes," Zawadi said. "If I were a fish, I'd be swimming in danger. If we stopped dumping waste, we'd be swimming with beauty again."
Zawadi was getting used to the second conditional. Her grammar teacher, Mr. Tunde, would've been proud.

They even made a short documentary. Jamal's camera caught the exuberant cheers of volunteers as they cleaned the pier. Kids waved to the drone overhead while painting signs with succinct, bold slogans: "No More Plastic!" and "Our Bay, Our Future."
One night, Zawadi stayed late editing the documentary's script. Her mother came in with some tea. "You look tired," she said.
"This part is tedious," Zawadi admitted. "But it has to be coherent."
Her mother smiled. "You've grown. You're not the same girl who used to hide during debates."
"I was just... unsure. But now I feel like I know what I stand for."

Weeks later, Zawadi sat on a discussion panel with environmentalists from across East Africa. She listened as one man spoke about the dire conditions of mangrove forests in Somalia. Another woman talked about coral bleaching in Zanzibar.
Then it was Zawadi's turn.

She looked out at the room and began: "My name is Zawadi. I come from Kilimo Bay, where the ocean was once forgotten. But we changed that. We weren't experts. Just youth with a view. A perspective. That was enough to start."
She spoke of clean-ups, art shows, stories passed down, and the durable strength of a united community. She finished with a question: "If a story can change a town, what else can it do?"
Afterward, an old journalist approached her. "You speak with clarity. Almost... omniscient in your awareness."
Zawadi laughed. "Only about Kilimo Bay."

One rainy morning, while walking along the newly restored shoreline, she saw a boy picking up a plastic bottle and dropping it into the bin. No one told him to. No one watched. It was simply what he did.
She thought back to her first clean-up. How tired she had been. How emaciated the beach had looked -- not from hunger, but from neglect.
If the city had continued ignoring the bay, what would it look like now? If Coastal Rise had never started, would the water still carry that telltale rainbow sheen of pollution?
The answer made her shiver.
But it didn't happen. And it wouldn't happen again.
That evening, a celebration was held to honour young leaders. Zawadi was given a medal and a small trophy shaped like a wave. On it were the words: "To the voice who made us listen."
As the crowd clapped, Jamal whispered, "Next stop, national TV."
Zawadi rolled her eyes and grinned. "Let's take a break first."
They stepped outside, where the stars blinked above the ocean. The air was cool. Clean.
"You know," Jamal said, "this whole journey started with you watching debris in the water."
She nodded. "It started when I decided not to look away."
They stood in silence, each imagining what the bay would look like five years from now.
If every town had a Zawadi, what would the coastlines of Africa look like?

Discussion and Reflection

tables: To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.
To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time.
As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.
To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony.
We commemorate our school's anniversary every year with a big celebration.
To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively.
She was able to articulate her opinion during the class discussion.
To make something happen faster; to hurry.
We need to hasten our preparations for the school event so everything is ready on time.
Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.
She is an advocate for environmental protection and helps organise beach clean-ups.
To attach or stick something to another thing.
Please affix the stamp to the envelope before mailing it.
To make something easier or help it happen.
The teacher will facilitate the group discussion to help everyone share their ideas.
To light up or make brighter; to make something clear or easier to understand.
The teacher used examples to illuminate the difficult concept.
To wave or hold something in an excited or threatening way.
He brandished his trophy proudly after winning the competition.
The ability to develop into something greater in the future.
She has the potential to become a great leader one day.
A feeling of being very annoyed or frustrated.
She sighed in exasperation when the meeting was cancelled again.
A feeling of doubt or worry about something.
She had misgivings about going to the party because she didn't know anyone there.
A way of looking at or thinking about something.
Everyone has a different perspective on what makes a good community.
Pieces of broken things or waste left after damage or destruction.
After the storm, there was a lot of debris scattered around the park.
Friendly and playful conversation or teasing.
During lunch, my friends and I enjoyed some light banter about our favourite films.
Very thin and weak, usually because of illness or lack of food.
After being sick for a long time, the dog looked gaunt and needed more food.
Easy to understand because it is clear and logical.
Her explanation was coherent, so everyone in the class understood the topic.
Having complete knowledge or understanding of everything.
In the story, the narrator is omniscient, knowing what every character thinks and feels.
Very serious or urgent; needing immediate attention.
The team was in dire need of a win to stay in the tournament.
Happening occasionally or at irregular intervals; not steady.
The rain was sporadic, coming and going throughout the day.
Friendly and pleasant to be around.
She has an amiable personality, making it easy for her to make friends.
After losing the game, he felt dejected and sat quietly on the bench.
Showing or revealing information about something; a revealing sign.
The telltale signs of a storm were visible in the darkening sky.
Very thin and weak because of not eating enough.
The stray cat looked emaciated after weeks without proper food.
Boring and taking a long time to do.
Copying out the same notes every day can be tedious.
Expressing something clearly and in few words.
The teacher asked us to give a succinct summary of the story in just three sentences.
Based on reason or logic; sensible.
It is important to make a rational decision when choosing a course of action.
Able to last a long time without getting damaged.
The backpack is made of durable material, so it will not tear easily.
Full of energy, excitement, and happiness.
The children were exuberant as they played in the park on a sunny day.
Very important; something that must be done.
It is imperative that you finish your homework before the weekend.
a) Full of energy and excitement
b) A feeling of doubt or worry
c) To stop something from happening

paragraphs: Grammar  —  Unit 4: Advertising and Persuasion
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Eight grammar mini-lessons

This unit has eight small grammar lessons. Read one lesson, try the practice, and then check your answers in the Answer Key at the end. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! All examples use the advertising and persuasion theme of Unit 4.
Lesson 1: Prepositions Preceding Nouns
What it means and why it matters

Prepositions are words that show the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence. When we say "prepositions preceding nouns," we mean prepositions that come before nouns to create common phrases. In advertising, these phrases appear constantly: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Learning these fixed phrases will make your English sound natural and help you understand advertisements more easily.
How to form and use them
Identify the noun you want to describe: sale, stock, time, demand, display.
Choose the right preposition: on, in, for, at, with.
Place the preposition before the noun: on + sale = on sale.
Use the phrase in a sentence: “The trainers are on sale until Friday.”
Common prepositional phrases in advertising:
on sale — “The shoes are on sale this week.”
in stock — “The toy is in stock at all our stores.”
for a limited time — “This offer is valid for a limited time.”
at a discount — “You can buy it at a discount today.”
on display — “The new collection is on display in the window.”
in demand — “These phones are in high demand.”
for commercial use — “The software is for commercial use only.”
with interest — “You can pay later with interest.”
Variations and exceptions
Some prepositional phrases are fixed and cannot be changed. You say "on sale" not "in sale."
The same noun can take different prepositions with different meanings: "in time" (not late) vs. "on time" (at the right moment).
Always learn prepositional phrases as complete units, not word by word.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The item is in sale this week.” ✗  (wrong preposition)
Fix: “The item is on sale this week.” ✓
Mistake: “The book is table.” ✗  (preposition missing)
Fix: “The book is on the table.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of prepositions as position words. They help position nouns in relation to other words. When you see an advertising phrase, notice which preposition is used and learn the whole phrase together.
Practice 1
Fill in the blanks with the correct preposition (on, in, for, at):
The special offer is ______ sale until Sunday.
These headphones are ______ stock in all colours.
This discount is available ______ a limited time.
You can buy the jacket ______ a reduced price today.
The new phones are ______ high demand this month.
Lesson 2: Passive Voice (Present and Past)
What it means and why it matters

The passive voice focuses on the action or the receiver of the action, rather than on who performs it. This is important in advertising because companies want to emphasise the product, not themselves. It is also common in news reports, scientific writing, and formal descriptions.
How to form it
Present passive: subject + is/are + past participle.
“The product is sold in fifty countries.”
“The aisles are cleaned every morning.”
Past passive: subject + was/were + past participle.
“The product was dubbed a bonanza by the press.”
“The cake was eaten by the children.”
Optional agent: use "by" + doer. Sometimes the agent is left out:
With agent: “The meal was cooked by the chef.”
Without agent: “The homework is completed every evening.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The book read by the students.” ✗  (missing form of to be)
Fix: “The book is read by the students.” ✓
Mistake: “The chef was cooked by the meal.” ✗  (subject and object reversed)
Fix: “The meal was cooked by the chef.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the passive voice as a backwards sentence. The action is still happening, but the focus flips! Remember: Subject + form of 'to be' + past participle + (by + doer).
Practice 2
Rewrite these active sentences in the passive voice:
The choir sings the song every Sunday.
The students finished the homework yesterday.
The artist painted the picture last year.
The company designs the adverts.
The teacher read the story to the class.
Lesson 3: Clauses Beginning with Past Participles
What it means and why it matters

Past participle clauses are short phrases that begin a sentence with a verb in its past participle form (typically ending in -ed, -en, -t, or -n). They add extra information and make your writing more sophisticated — they are especially popular in product descriptions and advertising copy.
How to form and use them
Choose a past participle: created, designed, built, written, excited, shocked.
Add detail: “Created by a cunning team...”
Add a comma and then the main clause: “Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention.”
Check the subject: the subject of the main clause must be the person or thing described by the participle.
More examples:
“Excited by the surprise party, she smiled widely.”
“Written in a hurry, the note was difficult to read.”
“Built by skilled workers, the bridge is strong and beautiful.”
“Designed for outdoor use, the speaker is waterproof.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “Created by a cunning team the ad drew attention.” ✗  (missing comma)
Fix: “Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention.” ✓
Mistake: Using the wrong form of the verb. Make sure you use the correct past participle.
Memory tip
Think of past participle clauses as a quick snapshot before the main event — they set the scene in just a few words!
Practice 3
Complete the sentences using the correct past participle form:
______ (excite) by the performance, the audience cheered loudly.
______ (forget) his lunch, he had to borrow from a friend.
______ (prepare) for the test, she studied all night.
______ (design) by local engineers, the bridge won an award.
______ (shock) by the news, they sat in silence.
Lesson 4: Reflexive Pronouns
What it means and why it matters

Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same. They end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). We also use them for emphasis — to show that someone did something alone or without help. In advertising, reflexive pronouns appear in emotional appeals: "Treat yourself to something special!"
The reflexive pronouns
I → myself   |   you → yourself   |   he → himself   |   she → herself
it → itself   |   we → ourselves   |   you (plural) → yourselves   |   they → themselves
Examples:
“I enjoyed the concert myself.”
“She taught herself how to play the guitar.”
“We prepared the presentation ourselves.”
“He bought the gift for himself.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “I saw myself the teacher.” ✗  (subject and object are different)
Fix: “I saw the teacher.” ✓  (no reflexive needed here)
Memory tip
Reflexive pronouns have -self in them! They always point back to the subject. When you see "-self," think: "I am doing this to/for myself!"
Practice 4
Fill in the blanks with the correct reflexive pronoun:
I can design the poster by ______ .
She convinced ______ that the product was worth buying.
They built the drone all by ______ .
You should believe in ______ .
He prepared the advertising pitch by ______ .
Lesson 5: Second Conditional
What it means and why it matters

The second conditional talks about imaginary or unreal situations in the present or future. It lets us dream, wish, and speculate. Advertisers use it to help customers imagine owning a product: "If you had this phone, you would never miss a moment." Understanding the second conditional helps you express wishes, give advice, and analyse persuasive language.
How to form it
Structure: If + past simple, subject + would + base verb.
“If I had the aptitude, I would launch a lucrative brand.”
“If she were a superhero, she would save the world.”
“If they had more time, they would travel to space.”
Important: in the second conditional, use were (not "was") for all subjects in the if-clause. This is a special rule.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “If I was a bird, I would fly.” ✗  (use were for all subjects)
Fix: “If I were a bird, I would fly.” ✓
Mistake: “If I have a million dollars, I would travel.” ✗  (present tense in if-clause)
Fix: “If I had a million dollars, I would travel.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the second conditional as a "dream" structure. You are imagining what could be! Always remember: "If I had" + "I would."
Practice 5
Complete the sentences using the second conditional:
If I ______ (be) the president, I ______ (make) the world a better place.
If my friend ______ (have) a car, we ______ (go) on a road trip.
If she ______ (know) the answer, she ______ (tell) us.
If we ______ (have) the funding, we ______ (launch) a pilot programme.
If he ______ (be) an advertiser, he ______ (create) only honest adverts.
Lesson 6: Comparatives with "the more" / "the less"
What it means and why it matters

This structure shows how one thing affects another. As one quality increases, the other also increases (or decreases). It is powerful for analysing advertising because you can describe cause-and-effect relationships: "The more colourful the packaging, the more children want to buy it."
How to form it
Structure: The more/less + [adjective/verb], the more/less + [adjective/verb].
“The more you practise, the better you get.”
“The less sugar you eat, the healthier you feel.”
“The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message.”
“The more exciting the story, the more people want to read it.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “More you study, better you get.” ✗  (missing "the")
Fix: “The more you study, the better you get.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of it as a seesaw: as one side goes up, the other goes up too (or down). "The more" = this side rises. "The more/less" = the other side follows.
Practice 6
Complete the sentences:
The ______ you read, the ______ you learn.
The ______ you exercise, the ______ your body feels.
The ______ noise there is, the ______ we can hear each other.
The ______ creative the advert, the ______ people remember it.
Lesson 7: Compound Adjectives
What it means and why it matters

Compound adjectives are made by combining two or more words to describe a noun more precisely. They are joined by a hyphen (-) when placed before a noun. Advertising uses compound adjectives constantly to make products sound exciting and professional: well-branded, fast-selling, multi-use, future-ready.
How to form them
Identify two words that work together to describe something: well + branded.
Join them with a hyphen when they come before a noun: well-branded products.
No hyphen when they come after the noun: "The products are well branded."
Common compound adjectives:
well-branded — marketed effectively with a strong identity.
fast-selling — selling quickly in large numbers.
multi-use — able to be used for many purposes.
long-lasting — durable; continuing for a long time.
high-quality — of excellent standard.
eco-friendly — not harmful to the environment.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She has a selling fast book.” ✗  (word order wrong)
Fix: “She has a fast-selling book.” ✓
Mistake: “The multi use tool is handy.” ✗  (missing hyphen)
Fix: “The multi-use tool is handy.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of compound adjectives as a team of two words that join forces (with a hyphen) to give your nouns extra detail and power!
Practice 7
Complete the sentences with the correct compound adjective from the box:
(well-branded, fast-selling, multi-use, eco-friendly, long-lasting)
This new gadget is a ______ tool for cooking, cleaning, and repairs.
The ______ shoes are the latest trend among young people.
The ______ toy is flying off the shelves in every store.
Our ______ packaging is made from recycled materials.
This ______ battery will power your device for two whole days.
Lesson 8: Reported Speech
What it means and why it matters

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is a way of sharing what someone else said without using their exact words. You change the pronouns, shift the tense back one step, and remove the quotation marks. This skill is important for summarising interviews, writing news articles, and discussing what advertisers claim about their products.
How to form it
Remove quotation marks and add that (optional): “He said that the offer was valid.”
Change the pronoun: I → he/she, we → they, my → his/her.
Move the tense back one step: present simple → past simple, will → would, can → could.
Examples:
Direct: “The manager said, 'The offer is a concession.'” → Reported: “The manager said that the offer was a concession.”
Direct: “She claimed, 'It is obsolete.'” → Reported: “She claimed that it was obsolete.”
Direct: “He said, 'I will join you later.'” → Reported: “He said that he would join me later.”
Reported questions
Direct: “He asked, 'Are you buying it?'” → Reported: “He asked if/whether I was buying it.”
Direct: “She asked, 'Why is it on sale?'” → Reported: “She asked why it was on sale.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “She said that she speaks three languages.” ✗  (tense not shifted)
Fix: “She said that she spoke three languages.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of reported speech as a Time Machine! You travel back in time: present becomes past, will becomes would, can becomes could.
Practice 8
Change these to reported speech:
The teacher said, “You must complete your homework.”
My friend said, “I will join you later.”
Dad said, “This movie is great!”
The advertiser claimed, “Our product is the best on sale.”
She asked, “Why is this so expensive?”

Answer Key

Unit 4: Advertising and Persuasion
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Self-Paced Lesson
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has twelve sections to work through at your own pace.

You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, and Reported Speech), and the Story "Code Name: Bonanza".
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions twice a week.
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 4 of Year 8: Advertising and Persuasion! Every day, you are surrounded by messages designed to grab your attention, change your mind, or convince you to buy something. From billboards towering over busy Mogadishu streets to sponsored posts sliding through your social media feed, advertising is everywhere. But how does it work? What techniques do advertisers use to persuade people? And how can you, as a critical thinker, tell the difference between honest information and clever manipulation?

In this unit, you will explore the language of advertising and persuasion. You will study how advertisers choose their words carefully, how they appeal to emotions, and how they use visual design to make products seem irresistible. You will also examine how persuasive language appears in everyday life — in speeches, debates, product reviews, and even conversations with friends. Understanding these techniques will make you a more attentive reader, a more shrewd consumer, and a more confident communicator.

The grammar focus of this unit is prepositions preceding nouns — small but powerful words like on, in, for, at, and with that appear before nouns to show relationships. You will notice them everywhere in advertising language: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Alongside prepositions, you will study passive voice, participle clauses, reflexive pronouns, the second conditional, comparatives with "the more / the less", compound adjectives, and reported speech. These grammar tools will help you write more persuasive texts, analyse advertisements critically, and express your own opinions with precision.

You will also read the story "Code Name: Bonanza", set in an East African tech school where student teams compete in an innovation challenge. The story explores how persuasion, branding, and integrity interact in a competitive environment. Through the story, you will see how vocabulary words like cunning, adversary, momentum, and bonanza come alive in context, and you will discover that the most lasting success comes not from clever tricks but from honest effort and ethical strategy.
What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand passages about advertising techniques, persuasion, and ethical marketing, and discuss their ideas confidently.
Identify and use prepositions preceding nouns correctly in phrases like on sale, in stock, for a limited time, at a discount, and with interest.
Form and use passive voice sentences in the present and past: "The product was launched by the company" and "The advert is designed to attract young people."
Begin sentences with past participle clauses: "Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention from thousands."
Use reflexive pronouns correctly: myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves — "She taught herself to code."
Form second conditional sentences to imagine unreal situations: "If I had more time, I would design a better poster."
Use comparatives with "the more / the less": "The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message."
Form and use compound adjectives with hyphens: well-branded, fast-selling, multi-use, future-ready.
Change direct speech into reported speech: "She said that the offer was a concession."
Write a persuasive advertisement, a product review, or an analytical essay about advertising techniques.
Present your opinions about advertising clearly and persuasively in spoken English.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently in speech and writing.
Think critically about how advertising shapes consumer behaviour, the ethics of persuasion, and the difference between information and manipulation.
Your Learning Path
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.

Work through the grammar lessons in the Grammar file (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, Reported Speech).
Read the Story file, "Code Name: Bonanza," and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all twelve sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary Preview

Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
Strategy and Competition
aptitude — a natural ability to do something well.
adversary — a person or group that opposes or fights against another.
cunning — having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
momentum — the force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
bonanza — a situation or event that produces a large amount of something good.
concession — something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement.
accord — an agreement or harmony between people or groups.
Description and Character
insolent — showing a rude and disrespectful attitude.
pungent — having a strong smell or taste that is often sharp or biting.
demeanor — the way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
dainty — small and delicate in a way that is attractive.
belligerent — angry and ready to fight or argue.
intricate — very detailed and complicated, with many small parts.
indifferent — not caring about something; having no strong feelings about it.
Action and Change
impede — to slow down or stop something from happening.
emerge — to come out or appear from somewhere.
diminish — to make something smaller or less in amount, size, or importance.
chastise — to scold or criticize someone for doing something wrong.
dub — to give someone or something a name or title.
undulate — to move in waves or with a smooth, wavy motion.
billow — to rise and move in large waves or clouds.
Evaluation and Scale
lucrative — something that makes a lot of money or profit.
elusive — difficult to find, catch, or achieve.
bleak — cold, empty, and without hope or happiness.
bountiful — having a lot of something; plentiful or abundant.
attentive — paying close attention to something or someone.
desolate — feeling very sad and lonely; a place that is empty.
apprehensive — worried or anxious about something that might happen.
obsolete — no longer used or needed because something newer exists.
capacious — having a lot of space; able to hold a lot of things.
gusto — enjoyment or enthusiasm in doing something.

Reading: The Power of Persuasion
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words and the grammar structures used. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and discuss the ideas.

Walk down any busy street in Mogadishu, Nairobi, or Addis Ababa and you will be surrounded by advertising. Posters shout about products on sale. Shop windows display items in stock. Radio jingles repeat catchy slogans. Sponsored posts fill your phone screen. Advertising is so pervasive that most people barely notice it anymore — yet its influence on our choices is enormous. Understanding how persuasion works is one of the most lucrative skills a young person can develop, not to make money from selling, but to protect yourself from being sold to without your knowledge.

Although advertising has existed for centuries, modern techniques are far more intricate than a simple market trader calling out prices. Today, advertisers use psychology, data analysis, and visual design to target specific audiences. A well-branded product does not just describe what it does — it creates a feeling. A sports drink advert does not simply say "this drink contains vitamins." Instead, it shows athletes running through rain, their muscles glistening, while dramatic music plays. The message is not about vitamins — it is about strength, ambition, and belonging to a winning team. The drink is dubbed a "performance booster" and sold at a premium.

One of the most powerful advertising techniques is the use of emotional appeal. Advertisers know that people make decisions based on feelings first and logic second. A charity advertisement might show a desolate landscape and a child with a sad expression to make viewers feel sympathy. A perfume advertisement might use soft lighting, elegant models, and a dainty bottle to create feelings of luxury and desire. Created by teams of designers and psychologists, these advertisements are carefully crafted to bypass your rational mind and speak directly to your emotions.

Whereas emotional appeals target the heart, logical appeals target the mind. These advertisements use facts, statistics, and comparisons. You might see claims like "9 out of 10 dentists recommend this toothpaste" or "our battery lasts 50% longer than the competition." The more specific the data, the more trustworthy the advert appears. However, an attentive reader will ask: where did these numbers come from? Were the statistics collected fairly? A cunning advertiser can present true facts in misleading ways — if consumers were more critical, fewer false claims would succeed.

Another technique is social proof — the idea that if other people are doing something, it must be good. Reviews, testimonials, and celebrity endorsements all work on this principle. When a fast-selling product displays thousands of five-star reviews, shoppers feel reassured. When a famous footballer wears a particular brand of trainers, fans want the same pair. Advertisers understand that momentum is powerful: the more people buy a product, the more others want it too. This creates a cycle that can turn an ordinary product into a bonanza of sales.

Not all advertising is harmful. Public health campaigns use persuasive techniques to encourage good habits: washing hands, getting vaccinated, staying in school. Environmental organisations use emotional appeals to protect forests and oceans. While commercial advertising aims to sell products, social advertising aims to change behaviour for the better. The key difference is purpose: is the message designed to help you, or is it designed to help the company's bank balance?

In East Africa, advertising is evolving rapidly. Mobile phone companies run some of the most visible campaigns, offering data bundles on sale and airtime at a discount. Small businesses use social media to reach customers they could never have found before. A tailor in Hargeisa can now advertise her bountiful collection of fabrics to customers across the region. Although this creates enormous opportunity, it also means that young people are exposed to more persuasive messages than any previous generation. Learning to analyse these messages — to question who made them, why, and what they want you to do — is no longer optional. It is essential.
🌍 Did You Know?
The global advertising industry spends over 700 billion US dollars every year -- more than the entire GDP of many countries.
The average person sees between 4,000 and 10,000 advertisements every single day, including online ads, product labels, and brand logos.
The word 'propaganda' originally just meant 'spreading information.' It only gained a negative meaning after it was used for political manipulation in the 20th century.

Grammar Focus: Prepositions Preceding Nouns
This section introduces the main grammar topic for Unit 4. You will practise it further in the Grammar Practice section and in the Grammar file, but here is a summary to help you get started.
What are prepositions preceding nouns?

Prepositions are small words that come before a noun to show a relationship — they tell us about place, time, direction, or purpose. In advertising language, prepositional phrases are everywhere because they help describe products and offers quickly and clearly. Learning to use them correctly will make your English sound natural and precise.
Common examples from advertising:
on sale — available at a reduced price.
in stock — available in the shop right now.
for a limited time — the offer will end soon.
at a discount — sold for less than the normal price.
with interest — with an extra charge added over time.
for commercial use — intended for business purposes.
on display — shown for people to look at.
in demand — wanted by many people.
How to form and use them
Identify the noun you want to describe: sale, stock, time, demand.
Choose the right preposition based on the meaning you need: on, in, for, at, with.
Place the preposition before the noun: on + sale = on sale; in + stock = in stock.
Use the phrase in a sentence: "These shoes are on sale until Friday."

Notice that many prepositional phrases are fixed expressions — you cannot swap the preposition for another one. You say "on sale" not "in sale," and "in stock" not "on stock." Learning these as complete phrases is the best approach.
Common mistakes
Mistake: "The item is in sale this week." ✗  (wrong preposition)
Fix: "The item is on sale this week." ✓
Mistake: "The book is table." ✗  (preposition missing)
Fix: "The book is on the table." ✓
Mistake: "She works in commercial use." ✗  (wrong preposition)
Fix: "The product is for commercial use." ✓
Quick Practice
Fill in each blank with the correct preposition (on, in, for, at, with):
The special offer is ______ sale until the end of the month.
These trainers are ______ stock in all sizes.
This discount is available ______ a limited time only.
You can buy it ______ a discount if you order today.
The advertisement was made ______ commercial use.
The new phone is ______ high demand this week.
Answers: 1. on   2. in   3. for   4. at   5. for   6. in

Grammar Focus: Passive Voice
What is the passive voice?

The passive voice is a way of building sentences where the focus is on the action or the thing receiving the action, rather than on who or what performs it. This is extremely useful in advertising because companies often want to emphasise the product, not themselves: "The drone is launched by solar timing" puts the spotlight on the drone, not the person who launches it.
Look at the difference:
Active: The company designed the advert. (focus on the company)
Passive: The advert was designed by the company. (focus on the advert)
How to form the passive voice
Present passive: subject + is/are + past participle. Example: "The product is sold in fifty countries."
Past passive: subject + was/were + past participle. Example: "The product was dubbed a bonanza by the press."
Optional agent: add "by" + doer if you want to say who did it. Example: "The drone was designed by Halima."
Without agent: sometimes the doer is unknown or unimportant. Example: "The aisle is cleaned every morning."
Common mistakes
Mistake: "The book read by the students." ✗  (missing form of to be)
Fix: "The book is read by the students." ✓
Mistake: "The meal was cooked the chef." ✗  (missing by)
Fix: "The meal was cooked by the chef." ✓
Quick Practice
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice:
The designer created the logo. → The logo ______.
The company sells the product online. → The product ______.
The students completed the homework. → The homework ______.
The chef cooked the meal. → The meal ______.
Answers: 1. was created by the designer   2. is sold online by the company   3. was completed by the students   4. was cooked by the chef

Grammar Focus: Clauses Beginning with Past Participles
What are past participle clauses?

A past participle clause is a short phrase at the beginning of a sentence that adds extra information using a verb in its past participle form (usually ending in -ed, -en, -t, or -n). These clauses make your writing more elegant and concise — especially useful when describing products or projects.
Examples:
"Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention from thousands."
"Designed with community spaces in mind, the drone adapts to local streets."
"Built for local conditions, the product is durable and affordable."
"Written in a hurry, the note was difficult to read."
How to use them
Choose a past participle: created, designed, built, written, excited, shocked.
Add detail: by whom, for what, or how — "Created by a cunning team."
Add a comma and then the main clause — "Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention."
Make sure the subject matches: the subject of the main clause must be the same as the person or thing described by the participle.
Common mistakes
Mistake: "Created by a cunning team the ad drew attention." ✗  (missing comma)
Fix: "Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention." ✓
Quick Practice
Complete each sentence with a past participle clause:
______ (excite) by the results, the team celebrated their success.
______ (design) for outdoor use, the speaker is waterproof.
______ (prepare) weeks in advance, the presentation was smooth and professional.
Answers: 1. Excited   2. Designed   3. Prepared
💡 Top Tip
Past participle clauses are very popular in product descriptions and advertisements. Look for them in online shop listings!
Try rewriting two sentences from the reading passage using participle clauses.
Grammar Focus: Reflexive Pronouns
What are reflexive pronouns?

Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same person or thing. They always end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). Reflexive pronouns also add emphasis — they show that someone did something without help.
The reflexive pronouns are:
I → myself   |   you → yourself   |   he → himself   |   she → herself
it → itself   |   we → ourselves   |   you (plural) → yourselves   |   they → themselves
Examples from the unit story:
"She taught herself to speak with calm authority." (Halima learned on her own.)
"We learned from ourselves." (The team reflected on their own mistakes.)
"He bought the gift for himself." (He, not anyone else, is the recipient.)
Quick Practice
Fill in the blanks with the correct reflexive pronoun:
I designed the poster by ______.
She made the decision ______.
They prepared the pitch by ______.
You should believe in ______.
Answers: 1. myself   2. herself   3. themselves   4. yourself
Grammar Focus: Second Conditional
What is the second conditional?

The second conditional talks about imaginary or unreal situations — things that are not true right now but that we can dream about or wish for. It is very useful in advertising ("If you bought this product, you would feel amazing") and in creative writing.
Structure: If + past simple, subject + would + base verb.
"If I had the aptitude, I would launch a lucrative brand."
"If we had more time, we would perfect the drone's navigation."
"If I were the judge, I would choose their project."
Important: in formal English, we use were (not "was") for all subjects in the if-clause: "If I were you, I would study harder." This is a special rule for the second conditional.
Quick Practice
Complete each sentence using the second conditional:
If I ______ (be) the president, I ______ (make) education free.
If she ______ (have) a bigger budget, she ______ (create) a better advert.
If we ______ (know) the answer, we ______ (tell) you immediately.
Answers: 1. were / would make   2. had / would create   3. knew / would tell
Grammar Focus: Comparatives with "the more" / "the less"
How do "the more" / "the less" comparatives work?

This structure shows how one thing affects another. As one quality increases, the other quality also increases (or decreases). This is powerful in advertising analysis because you can describe cause-and-effect relationships.
Structure: The more/less + [adjective/verb], the more/less + [adjective/verb].
"The more you practise, the better you get."
"The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message."
"The less sugar you eat, the healthier you feel."
"The more exciting the story, the more people want to read it."
Quick Practice
Complete the sentences:
The ______ you read, the ______ you learn.
The ______ effort you put in, the ______ your results will be.
The ______ noise there is, the ______ we can concentrate.
Answers: 1. more / more   2. more / better   3. more / less

Writing Workshop

In this section, you will choose one of three writing projects. Each project uses the vocabulary, grammar, and ideas from this unit. The goal is to practise extended writing while thinking critically about advertising and persuasion.
✍️ Writing rules to remember
Start every sentence with a capital letter. End with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark.
Use vocabulary from this unit: cunning, lucrative, momentum, bonanza, diminish, etc.
Include at least two prepositional phrases (on sale, in stock, for a limited time), one passive voice sentence, one reflexive pronoun, and one second conditional sentence.
Read your work out loud, or ask your AI tutor to check it, before you finish.
Option A: Create a Persuasive Advertisement

You have been hired to create an advertisement for a new product. The product can be anything you choose: a gadget, a food product, a piece of clothing, or a service. Write a full-page advertisement (about 250–300 words) that would convince people to buy it.
Your advertisement should include:
A catchy name and slogan for the product. Use a compound adjective: "Our future-ready device changes everything."
An emotional appeal: how will the product make the customer feel? Use vivid description.
A logical appeal: give at least one statistic or fact about the product's quality or performance.
Social proof: mention reviews, endorsements, or how many people have already bought it. Use a comparative: "The more people discover it, the more they love it."
A call to action: tell the reader what to do next. Use a prepositional phrase: "Available on sale for a limited time."
At least one passive voice sentence: "Designed by award-winning engineers..."

Example opening: "Introducing the AquaPure Water Bottle — the well-branded, fast-selling hydration solution that everyone is talking about. Designed by award-winning engineers, the AquaPure keeps your water cold for 24 hours. If you had just one product to improve your daily routine, this would be it."
Option B: Write a Critical Product Review
Choose a product you own or have used (a phone, a bag, a pair of shoes, a food item). Write a detailed review (about 250–300 words) that analyses the product honestly.
Your review should include:
An introduction naming the product and how you got it.
What the advertising promised: describe the claims made by the advert or packaging. Use reported speech: "The company claimed that the product was the best on the market."
Your actual experience: did the product live up to its promises? Use reflexive pronouns: "I tested it myself over three weeks."
A balanced judgement: use contrast language. "Although the design was elegant, the quality did not match the price."
A recommendation: would you recommend it? Use the second conditional: "If I had known about the poor quality, I would have chosen a different brand."
Option C: Analytical Essay — How Advertising Shapes Our Choices
Write a short essay (about 250–300 words) analysing how advertising influences the choices young people make. Use ideas from the reading passage and your own experience.
Your essay should include:
An introduction explaining what advertising is and why it is important to understand.
Body paragraph 1: describe one advertising technique (emotional appeal, logical appeal, or social proof) and explain how it works. Use an example.
Body paragraph 2: discuss whether advertising is always honest. Use vocabulary: cunning, elusive, attentive.

A conclusion: give your opinion on how young people can protect themselves from misleading advertising. Use a comparative: "The more we understand about persuasion, the better we can make our own choices."

Listening and Speaking
Listening: A Conversation about Advertising Ethics
Ask your AI tutor to read this dialogue aloud while you listen. Then read it yourself.
Interviewer: What do you think about advertising in East Africa today?
Amina: Honestly, it is everywhere. Every time I open my phone, I see adverts for data bundles on sale, new clothes, food delivery apps. It is hard to escape.
Interviewer: Do you think these adverts influence your choices?
Amina: They do, even when I try to resist. Although I know that many adverts exaggerate, I still find myself wanting things I see online. The well-branded products look so appealing.
Interviewer: Can you give an example?

Amina: Last month, I saw an advert for a fast-selling pair of trainers. The advert said they were "the most comfortable shoes ever made." Created by a famous designer, the trainers looked amazing. I convinced myself that I needed them. But when I bought them, they were not as comfortable as the advert claimed. I felt apprehensive about wasting my money.
Interviewer: What did you learn from that experience?

Amina: I learned to be more attentive when I see adverts. Now I always check reviews myself before buying anything. My mother said that if she were my age, she would have the same problem, because advertising is much more intricate now than it used to be. The more I learn about persuasion techniques, the less likely I am to be tricked.
Interviewer: What advice would you give to other young people?

Amina: Do not believe everything you see. The more colourful and exciting an advert looks, the more carefully you should examine it. Teach yourself to ask: who made this advert, and what do they want me to do? If every young person asked those questions, misleading advertising would diminish quickly.
After listening, answer these questions:
What types of adverts does Amina see on her phone?
What happened when Amina bought the trainers she saw advertised?
What did Amina's mother say about advertising? Write the answer in reported speech.
Find two examples of reflexive pronouns in the dialogue.
Find one example of a prepositional phrase (preposition + noun) used in advertising language.
What advice does Amina give to young people about advertising? Do you agree with her?
Find one example of a second conditional sentence and one "the more / the less" comparative.
Speaking 1: My Experience with Advertising
Prepare a short spoken presentation (about two minutes) on this topic: "A time when an advertisement influenced me — positively or negatively." Follow this structure:
Introduction: Describe the advert you remember. What product was it for? Where did you see it?
The persuasion techniques: What techniques did the advert use? Use vocabulary: cunning, well-branded, fast-selling, emotional appeal, social proof.
Your response: What did you do? Did you buy the product? Use reflexive pronouns: "I convinced myself that I needed it."
What you learned: How has this experience changed the way you look at adverts? Use a comparative: "The more I learn about advertising, the more carefully I examine adverts."
Conclusion: Give one piece of advice to other young consumers. Use the second conditional: "If everyone questioned adverts more carefully, companies would have to be more honest."
Record yourself and listen back. Check that you spoke clearly and used grammar from this unit.
Speaking 2: Debate — Should Social Media Advertising Be More Regulated?
In pairs or with your AI tutor, discuss this question: Should governments create stricter rules for advertising on social media?
One person argues for and the other argues against. Use evidence, passive voice, and reported speech to support your arguments:

For: "Misleading adverts should be banned from social media because young people are especially vulnerable." / "Experts have said that social media advertising is more manipulative than traditional advertising."

Against: "Although some adverts are misleading, strict regulation would impede small businesses that rely on social media." / "Business owners have argued that social media advertising creates jobs and economic growth."
After the debate, write one paragraph explaining which side you agree with and why. Use at least two vocabulary words and one grammar structure from this unit.

Activities and Exercises
These activities help you apply what you have learned about advertising and persuasion in creative and practical ways. Choose at least three activities to complete.
Activity 1: Advert Analysis
Find a real advertisement — from a newspaper, a website, a poster, or a screenshot from social media. Stick it in your notebook or describe it in detail. Then answer:
What product or service is being advertised?
Who is the target audience (children, teenagers, adults, parents)?
What persuasive technique(s) does it use: emotional appeal, logical appeal, social proof, or celebrity endorsement?
Does it use any prepositional phrases like on sale, in stock, or for a limited time?
Does it use any compound adjectives like well-branded or fast-selling?
Is the advert honest? Does it make any claims that seem exaggerated or misleading?
How effective do you think the advert is? Rate it out of 10 and explain your rating.
Activity 2: Before and After

Many products that were once considered lucrative have become obsolete. Think of three examples of products or technologies that are no longer widely used (for example: cassette tapes, fax machines, film cameras). For each one:
Write one sentence explaining what the product was and why it was popular. Use passive voice: "Cassette tapes were used by millions of people to listen to music."
Write one sentence explaining why it became obsolete. Use a comparative: "The more digital music improved, the less people needed cassettes."

Write one sentence imagining what would happen if it came back. Use the second conditional: "If cassette tapes were reintroduced today, young people would probably find them charming but impractical."
Activity 3: Create a Brand Identity
Imagine you are starting a small business in your local area (a bakery, a tutoring service, a clothing shop, a tech repair business). Create a complete brand identity:
Business name (use a compound adjective if possible: "Quick-Fix Tech Repairs").

Logo description (you do not need to draw it — describe what it would look like in three to four sentences).
