Story  —  Unit 4: Advertising and Persuasion
Year 8 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  "Code Name: Bonanza"
📖 About this story
Genre: Realistic fiction with drama and social insight.
Theme: Resilience, persuasion, and ethical strategy in competitive environments.
Setting: Present-day Mogadishu, Somalia -- a modern tech high school with urban surroundings.
Moral: The more cunning the path, the less lasting the reward. Integrity and collaboration outpace deception.
The Unit 4 vocabulary words are in bold the first time they appear. When you meet one, say it out loud!
Code Name: Bonanza

The morning air in Mogadishu was pungent with the smell of roasted coffee and car fumes as the courtyard of Nuur Academy buzzed with students. Posters flapped against walls, advertising the annual "Innovation for Impact" challenge. The national competition brought together top student teams to pitch projects that combined technology with social good.
This year's theme: sustainable urban living.

Halima eyed the poster as she passed, already knowing every detail. She had emerged as one of the most promising coders in school, known for her quiet focus and sharp aptitude in algorithms. But she was not competing alone.
"We have got five days," said Yusuf, her teammate. He had a bold demeanor and the annoying habit of tapping pens when thinking. "We can still win if we push."
"If we had more time," Halima muttered, "we would perfect the drone's navigation."
Their project, a waste-sorting drone, was promising but glitchy. Sometimes it confused recyclable plastic for food waste. It was clever — maybe too clever — and the mechanics were intricate.
The school's tech lab, usually quiet, had turned into a battlefield. Tools and tablets were everywhere. Tension built with each hour.
Their biggest adversary was Team Horizon, led by Faarax — the kind of competitor who smiled sweetly while planting doubts in your mind.
"Nice drone," he said one afternoon, passing Halima's table. "Shame it is not... reliable."
Halima raised an eyebrow. "Our testing has just begun."
Faarax grinned. "So has ours. We have dubbed it 'The Beacon.'"
The name had already shown up on flyers around the campus. Well-branded. Fast-selling. Future-ready.
Created by a cunning team, The Beacon seemed flawless. Too flawless. Rumours swirled that they had copied elements from online builds.
When Halima told Yusuf, he looked apprehensive.
"If they cheated, should we report it?"
Halima shook her head. "Only if we have proof. Otherwise, it is just... noise."
On Wednesday, all teams gave a short demo. When it was Team Spark's turn, Yusuf stepped up, voice steady.
"Our drone sorts waste mid-air using object recognition. It runs on solar and can recharge at smart stations."
The drone rose. For a moment, all was smooth.
Then it wobbled, bumped into a shelf, and dropped a piece of cardboard meant to be sorted.
A few students laughed.
Halima clenched her fists, blaming herself.
The judges nodded politely, saying little. Their eyes moved to Team Horizon, whose prototype hovered like a dream, sorting items into labelled bins with almost robotic grace.
Applause followed. One judge even whispered, "Now that is a bonanza of an idea."
Halima felt the momentum diminish. Their odds shrank like morning mist.
Later that evening, Yusuf slammed a tablet down. "It is not fair. He is using tech we cannot trace. I bet it is not even original."
Halima nodded, thinking. "Then let us beat them differently."
"How?"
"The more belligerent we get, the less people listen. But the more we refine, the more we show who we are."
The team worked with new gusto, rewriting the sorting code and simplifying the drone's shell to improve balance. Halima even wrote a script in passive voice for the final pitch:
"The drone is launched by solar timing. Waste is identified by AI and sorted automatically. Maintenance is managed remotely."
She trained herself to speak with calm authority. Yusuf changed his tone from fiery to firm. They practised until the pitch felt smooth.
Final day.
Bright lights filled the auditorium. Parents, local reporters, and school heads sat in rows. Each team had ten minutes.
Team Horizon went first. Faarax's tone was slick. His smile wide.
"Our product, The Beacon, will transform waste into wealth."
He walked through their process, but avoided technical questions. One judge asked about source code.
"Proprietary," Faarax replied. "Protected for future commercial use."
Halima caught a flash of confusion among the judges.
Then it was Team Spark's turn.
Created through weeks of testing, their new version was lighter, steadier, and clear in function.
Halima spoke: "Built for local streets, the drone adapts to changes in light and wind. It was designed with community spaces in mind."
Their presentation included video clips, one where the drone dropped waste neatly into bins outside a park. The judges murmured approval.
Afterward, during Q&A, a judge asked, "How did you overcome your early bugs?"
"We evaluated each failure," Halima answered. "And improved based on them. We learned from ourselves."
There was a long pause. Then applause.
Later, as everyone waited for results, Faarax approached.
"That was... solid," he said.
Halima nodded, cautious.
"You are calm," he added. "I would be panicking if I were you."
"We did our best," she said, refusing to be drawn into his insolent games.
He stepped closer. "I could offer a concession. Maybe we say we collaborate next year. Share ideas."
Yusuf narrowed his eyes. "We are not interested."
"Fine." Faarax walked away, his demeanor suddenly tense.
Moments later, the announcements began.
In third place: a drone that composted banana peels.
In second: The Beacon.
The hall gasped.
First place: Team Spark.
A roar followed. Halima barely heard anything beyond the sound of Yusuf yelling in joy and hugging her.
The judges explained: "Team Spark impressed not only with innovation but with transparency and teamwork. They demonstrated growth, strategy, and an eye for ethical application."
Back at the lab, a breeze entered through open windows. Plans and sketches billowed off desks. Halima caught one.
"Looks like we need a filing system," she said, laughing.
The room felt capacious, suddenly full of promise. Empty space had become opportunity.
Yusuf was already scribbling ideas. "Maybe we create a multi-use drone next — well-branded and bright."
Halima smiled. "We will need to study more. Better sensors. Better balance."
"If we had the funding," Yusuf mused, "we would launch a full pilot in the summer."
"If we had the funding," Halima echoed, "we would create jobs too."
Outside, clouds undulated in waves across the sky. The competition was over, but momentum was building.
Halima looked at her hands. They smelled faintly of soldering metal and soap. She had fought an uphill battle — not with belligerence, but with reason.
And that, she thought, was how to win with integrity.
After Reading: Understanding the Story
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the story to support your answers.

Find three examples of passive voice in the story. Write them out.

Think and Discuss
These questions have no single right answer. Discuss them with your AI tutor, a classmate, or your family.
Faarax used slick branding and marketing language to promote The Beacon. Is strong branding the same as dishonesty? Where is the line between persuasion and manipulation?
Halima chose not to report her suspicions about Team Horizon without proof. Was this the right decision? What would you have done?
The story compares two approaches to competition: cunning tricks versus honest hard work. Can you think of a real-life example where integrity won over deception?
Yusuf says he wants to create a "well-branded" drone next. Is it possible to be both well-branded and ethical? How?
The judge called The Beacon "a bonanza of an idea." How does the meaning of bonanza change between the judge's comment and the final outcome of the story?

tables: A natural ability to do something well.
She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.
A person or group that opposes or fights against another.
In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.
Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.
The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.
A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good.
The school fair was a bonanza of fun activities and delicious food.
Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement.
After a long discussion, they made a concession and agreed to meet halfway.
An agreement or harmony between people or groups.
The students reached an accord about how to work together on the project.
Showing a rude and disrespectful attitude.
The insolent student talked back to the teacher in class.
Having a strong smell or taste that is often sharp or biting.
The pungent smell of garlic filled the kitchen as my mother cooked dinner.
The way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
Her calm demeanor made everyone feel relaxed during the meeting.
Small and delicate in a way that is attractive.
She wore a dainty necklace that sparkled in the light.
Angry and ready to fight or argue.
The belligerent dog barked loudly at anyone who came near.
Very detailed and complicated, with many small parts or patterns.
The artist created an intricate design on the pottery that amazed everyone.
Not caring about something; having no strong feelings about it.
She was indifferent about which movie to watch, so she let her friends choose.
To slow down or stop something from happening.
Heavy traffic can impede our journey to school.
To come out or appear from somewhere.
After the rain stopped, a rainbow began to emerge in the sky.
To make something smaller or less in amount, size, or importance.
The loud music began to diminish as the party came to an end.
To scold or criticize someone for doing something wrong.
The teacher had to chastise the student for not doing his homework.
To give someone or something a name or title.
The teacher decided to dub the new student 'The Math Wizard.'
To move in waves or with a smooth, wavy motion.
The flag began to undulate in the wind, creating beautiful waves of colour.
To rise and move in large waves or clouds.
The smoke began to billow from the chimney into the sky.
Something that makes a lot of money or profit.
She started a lucrative business selling handmade jewellery online.
Difficult to find, catch, or achieve.
The elusive butterfly flew away before I could catch it.
Something that is cold, empty, and without hope or happiness.
The landscape was bleak, with no trees or flowers in sight.
Having a lot of something; plentiful or abundant.
The garden was bountiful, filled with many fruits and vegetables.
Paying close attention to something or someone.
The attentive student listened carefully to the teacher's instructions.
Feeling very sad and lonely; a place that is empty and has no people or life.
After the storm, the beach looked desolate with no one around.
Worried or anxious about something that might happen.
She felt apprehensive about taking the test because she had not studied.
No longer used or needed because something newer exists.
Many people think that printed newspapers are becoming obsolete.
Having a lot of space; able to hold a lot of things.
The capacious backpack could fit all of my books and supplies.
Enjoyment or enthusiasm in doing something.
She danced with great gusto at the school talent show.

paragraphs: Grammar Reference
Year 8 – Unit 5: Complex Noun Phrases and More

This grammar reference covers the key grammar topics for Unit 5. Use it alongside your lesson and story to deepen your understanding. Each section includes explanations, examples, exercises, and answer keys so you can practise independently.
A. Complex Noun Phrases
What Is a Complex Noun Phrase?

A complex noun phrase is a group of words built around a head noun that gives detailed information about a person, place, thing, or idea. Instead of using a single noun on its own, you add words before it (pre-modifiers) or after it (post-modifiers) to make the description richer and more precise.
For example, compare these two sentences:
Simple: The mistake caused damage.
Complex: The egregious environmental mistake that went unnoticed for years caused damage.

The second sentence uses a complex noun phrase. It tells us much more about the mistake: it was egregious, it was environmental, and it went unnoticed for years. This is what makes your writing more informative and engaging.
How to Build a Complex Noun Phrase
You build a complex noun phrase in layers. Think of the head noun as the centre of a target, and the modifiers as the rings around it.
Start with the head noun. This is the main word. For example: water, mistake, data, boy.
Add a determiner. This is a word like the, a, an, this, that, some, or my. For example: the water, a mistake.
Add adjectives before the noun (pre-modifiers). Adjectives describe the noun. For example: the pristine water, the egregious environmental mistake.
Add phrases or clauses after the noun (post-modifiers). These give extra detail. For example: the pristine water in the lake, the egregious environmental mistake that went unnoticed.
Combine everything. The full complex noun phrase might be: the pristine water in the lake, or an adamant group of students researching the coastline.
Pre-Modifiers: Order of Adjectives
When you use more than one adjective before a noun, English has a natural order. While native speakers feel this order instinctively, learners need to practise it. The general order is:
Opinion (beautiful, terrible, egregious)
Size (large, small, tiny)
Age (old, young, ancient)
Shape (round, flat, square)
Colour (blue, green, dark)
Origin (Somali, British, coastal)
Material (wooden, plastic, cotton)
Purpose (fishing, cleaning, writing)
For example: a beautiful large old Somali fishing boat. You would not say "a fishing old large beautiful Somali boat." The order matters for natural-sounding English.
Post-Modifiers
Post-modifiers come after the head noun. The most common types are:
Prepositional phrases: the data on the table, the town along the coastline
Relative clauses: the boy who helped gather samples, the evidence that proved the pollution
Past participle phrases: the waste thrown behind the market, the systems ordered from the capital
Present participle phrases: the students researching the decline, the wind blowing across the shore
Examples from the Story
"a dedicated group of students researching the coastline's decline" — head noun: group; pre-modifiers: a, dedicated; post-modifiers: of students, researching the coastline's decline
"the egregious result of ignorance" — head noun: result; pre-modifier: the, egregious; post-modifier: of ignorance
"a small team of councillors" — head noun: team; pre-modifiers: a, small; post-modifier: of councillors
"copious charts and graphs" — head noun: charts; pre-modifier: copious; joined with: and graphs
"the older doctrine that says change is disrespect" — head noun: doctrine; pre-modifiers: the, older; post-modifier: that says change is disrespect
Common Mistakes
Wrong adjective order: "a environmental egregious mistake" should be "an egregious environmental mistake." Opinion adjectives come before origin or category adjectives.
Missing determiners: "Pristine water was found" is acceptable, but "I found pristine water in lake" needs a determiner: "I found pristine water in the lake."
Overloading the noun phrase: Do not stack too many modifiers. "The very extremely incredibly large old broken dark wooden fishing boat" is too much. Aim for two or three modifiers at most.
Confusing relative pronouns: Use who for people, which for things, and that for either. "The boy which helped" should be "The boy who helped."

B. Future Passive
What Is the Future Passive?

The future passive voice is used when you want to talk about actions that will happen in the future, but you want to focus on the action or the thing receiving the action rather than the person doing it. This is especially useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious from the context.
Compare these two sentences:
Active: The council will install the purification systems. (focus on the council)
Passive: The purification systems will be installed by the council. (focus on the systems)
How to Form It
The structure is: Subject + will + be + past participle + (by + doer)
The "by + doer" part is optional. You include it only when it matters to know who performs the action.
Step-by-step:
Identify the object of the active sentence. This becomes the new subject.
Write will be after the subject.
Add the past participle of the main verb.
Optionally add by + the original subject (the doer).
Example transformation:
Active: Scientists will test the water samples.
Passive: The water samples will be tested (by scientists).
Examples from the Story
"The first units will be installed by December."
"Solar-powered purification systems were ordered from the capital." (past passive, for comparison)
"Grants were secured for the town." (past passive)
Common Mistakes
Forgetting "be": "The homework will finished" is wrong. It must be "The homework will be finished."
Using the wrong verb form: "The project will be complete" should be "The project will be completed." Use the past participle, not the base form or adjective.
Using future passive when active is clearer: Sometimes the active voice is simpler. Use the passive when the focus on the receiver matters.
Exercises: Future Passive
Exercise A: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence using the future passive form of the verb in brackets.
The new bins ____________ (deliver) to the market next week.
A report on water quality ____________ (publish) by the research team.
The community hall ____________ (clean) before the presentation.
Reusable bags ____________ (distribute) to every household.
The damaged fishing nets ____________ (replace) by the end of the month.
Exercise B: Rewrite in the Passive
Change each active sentence into the future passive.
The mayor will open the new recycling centre.
Students will present the findings at the summit.
The government will fund the clean-up programme.
Engineers will build a new water treatment plant.
Volunteers will collect plastic waste from the beach.
Answer Key: Future Passive
Exercise A:
The new bins will be delivered to the market next week.
A report on water quality will be published by the research team.
The community hall will be cleaned before the presentation.
Reusable bags will be distributed to every household.
The damaged fishing nets will be replaced by the end of the month.
Exercise B:
The new recycling centre will be opened by the mayor.
The findings will be presented at the summit (by students).
The clean-up programme will be funded by the government.
A new water treatment plant will be built (by engineers).
Plastic waste will be collected from the beach (by volunteers).
C. Connectives for Contrast and Purpose
What Are Connectives?

Connectives are words or phrases that link ideas together. In this unit, you will focus on two types: connectives that show contrast (when two ideas are different or surprising) and connectives that show purpose (the reason for doing something).
Connectives for Contrast
These words signal that the second idea is unexpected or different from the first.
Key rule: Do not use "but" after "although." This is a very common mistake. "Although it was cold, but we went outside" is wrong. Remove the "but."
Connectives for Purpose
These words explain why an action is done.
Exercises: Connectives
Exercise A: Choose the Correct Connective
Fill in each blank with the best connective from the box: although, however, in order to, so that, yet, despite.
____________ the evidence was clear, many people refused to change. (contrast)
Yusuf rehearsed his speech ____________ he could deliver it confidently. (purpose)
The students worked hard. ____________, not everyone listened. (contrast)
They placed bins outside ____________ reduce waste. (purpose)
____________ the resistance from elders, the club continued their work. (contrast)
The figures spoke clearly, ____________ the mood remained somber. (contrast)
Exercise B: Combine the Sentences
Join each pair of sentences using an appropriate connective for contrast or purpose.
The sea looked blue and clean. It was actually polluted. (use although)
Halima studied the data carefully. She wanted to find the truth. (use in order to)
Yusuf was nervous. He gave an excellent speech. (use despite)
The students made posters. They wanted everyone to attend. (use so that)
Answer Key: Connectives
Exercise A:
Although the evidence was clear, many people refused to change.
Yusuf rehearsed his speech so that he could deliver it confidently.
The students worked hard. However, not everyone listened.
They placed bins outside in order to reduce waste.
Despite the resistance from elders, the club continued their work.
The figures spoke clearly, yet the mood remained somber.
Exercise B: Sample Answers
Although the sea looked blue and clean, it was actually polluted.
Halima studied the data carefully in order to find the truth.
Despite being nervous, Yusuf gave an excellent speech.
The students made posters so that everyone would attend.
D. Past Modals: Should Have / Should Not Have
What Do They Mean?

We use should have + past participle to talk about something that was the right thing to do in the past, but it did not happen. We use should not have + past participle to talk about something that happened in the past, but it was the wrong thing to do.
Should have = it was a good idea, but it did not happen.
Should not have = it was a bad idea, but it happened anyway.
How to Form It
The structure is: Subject + should have + past participle
For the negative: Subject + should not have (shouldn't have) + past participle
Examples:
"I should have practised public speaking more." (Yusuf wishes he had practised more, but he did not.)
"We should have listened to each other sooner." (They wish they had listened, but they did not.)
"They should have joined us." (Halima wishes they had joined, but they did not.)
"You shouldn't have thrown waste behind the market." (It was wrong, but they did it.)
"I shouldn't have ignored the warning signs." (It was a mistake to ignore them.)
Common Mistakes
Forgetting "have": "They should practised" is wrong. It must be "They should have practised."
Using the base form instead of the past participle: "She should have tell me" is wrong. It must be "She should have told me."
Confusing with "must have": "Must have" expresses a deduction about the past ("She must have left early"), not regret. "Should have" expresses regret or advice about the past.
Exercises: Past Modals
Exercise A: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence using should have or should not have and the correct past participle.
We ____________ (start) the clean-up earlier. Now there is too much waste.
They ____________ (throw) their rubbish into the sea. It caused pollution.
Yusuf ____________ (prepare) his notes the night before. He felt nervous.
The council ____________ (ignore) the students' report. It contained valuable data.
She ____________ (bring) more water samples to the meeting.
Exercise B: Write Your Own
Think of three situations where someone made a mistake or missed an opportunity. Write a sentence using should have or should not have for each one.
Answer Key: Past Modals
Exercise A:
We should have started the clean-up earlier.
They should not have thrown (shouldn't have thrown) their rubbish into the sea.
Yusuf should have prepared his notes the night before.
The council should not have ignored (shouldn't have ignored) the students' report.
She should have brought more water samples to the meeting.
E. Reported Speech (Review)
Quick Review

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is used to tell someone what another person said, without quoting their exact words. You change the pronouns, tense, and time expressions to fit the new perspective.
Key changes:
Examples from the Story
Direct: "The transition has already begun." → Reported: Halima stated that the transition had already begun.
Direct: "The first units will be installed by December." → Reported: The mayor said that the first units would be installed by December.
Direct: "Change does not mean disrespect." → Reported: Grandfather told Yusuf that change did not mean disrespect.
Exercise: Convert to Reported Speech
Yusuf said, "I will speak at the summit." → Yusuf said that ____________.
Halima said, "We need precise data." → Halima said that ____________.
Amina asked, "Are you nervous?" → Amina asked ____________.
Grandfather said, "The sea will heal." → Grandfather said that ____________.
The shopkeeper asked, "What have you done?" → The shopkeeper asked ____________.
Answer Key: Reported Speech
Yusuf said that he would speak at the summit.
Halima said that they needed precise data.
Amina asked if he was nervous.
Grandfather said that the sea would heal.
The shopkeeper asked what they had done.
F. Spotting Inconsistencies in Arguments
What Is an Inconsistency?

An inconsistency in an argument happens when two or more statements contradict each other, or when the evidence does not support the claim being made. Learning to spot inconsistencies helps you become a critical thinker who can evaluate information carefully.
How to Spot Them
Identify the main claim. What is the speaker or writer arguing?
Check the evidence. Does the evidence actually support the claim?
Look for contradictions. Do any statements disagree with each other or with the evidence?
Consider the context. Sometimes a claim is true in one situation but not in another.
Make a judgement. Is the argument strong, or does the inconsistency weaken it?
Example from the Story

In the story, some elders claim that their traditional ways of waste disposal are harmless. However, the scientific data shows that fish populations are declining and water quality is dropping. This is an inconsistency: the claim ("our ways are harmless") contradicts the evidence (declining fish and poor water quality).
Exercise: Spot the Inconsistency
Read each pair of statements and explain the inconsistency.
Claim: "Our coastline is perfectly healthy." Evidence: Water tests show dangerous pH levels and declining fish populations.
Claim: "Young people have done nothing to help." Evidence: Students have been testing water, cleaning the beach, and educating the community for months.
Claim: "Modern methods always bring dismal outcomes." Evidence: Solar-powered purification systems have improved water quality in many other towns.
Answer Key: Inconsistencies
The claim says the coastline is healthy, but the evidence (dangerous pH, declining fish) directly contradicts this. The argument is inconsistent because the facts do not match the claim.
The claim says young people have done nothing, but the evidence shows extensive work by students. The speaker is ignoring or denying observable facts.
The claim says modern methods always bring dismal outcomes, but the evidence from other towns shows positive results. The word "always" makes the claim too absolute and easy to disprove.
tables: Although it was raining, we continued the clean-up.
The data was clear. However, many people still disagreed.
The evidence was strong, yet the town's mood remained somber.
Despite the resistance, the students kept working.
Some supported the project. On the other hand, others were sceptical.
While Yusuf believed in science, his grandfather trusted tradition.
They collected data in order to prove the pollution was real.
She practised every day so that she could speak confidently.
He jogged the shore to note what others missed.
They modified their approach so as to gain the elders' trust.
"I am happy" → She said she was happy
"I am working" → He said he was working
"I have finished" → She said she had finished
"I will help" → He said he would help
"I can swim" → She said she could swim

paragraphs: Year 8 – Unit 5 Lesson
Natural Resources and Sustainability
Overview

Welcome to Unit 5 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the important topic of natural resources and sustainability. You will learn how communities around the world—and especially in East Africa—depend on natural resources like water, fish, forests, and clean air, and what happens when those resources are threatened by pollution, carelessness, or outdated practices.

The centrepiece of this unit is a story called "Shifting Currents," which follows a young man named Yusuf who lives in a coastal town on the Somali shoreline. Yusuf notices that the sea his community depends on is being slowly destroyed by pollution and waste. Together with his sister Amina and a group of classmates, he works to raise awareness and change attitudes—even when powerful elders resist the message. The story explores themes of change, belief, tradition, and environmental responsibility.

Alongside the story, you will build your vocabulary with 31 new words that relate to thought, strategy, environment, and persuasion. Many of these words—such as pristine, adamant, egregious, and doctrine—appear naturally in the story, so you will see them in context before studying their definitions in detail.

Your grammar focus for this unit is complex noun phrases. You will learn how to build rich, detailed descriptions by adding modifiers before and after a noun. You will also review and practise the future passive voice, connectives for contrast and purpose, and past modals (should have / should not have). These grammar structures will help you write more precisely, argue more convincingly, and understand more advanced texts.

This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through each section at your own speed, using your AI tutor for help whenever you need it. You will also have two teacher sessions per week where you can ask questions, practise speaking, and discuss the story and its themes with your classmates. By the end of this unit, you will have stronger vocabulary, more confident grammar skills, and a deeper understanding of sustainability issues in your own community.
ℹ️ How This Unit Works
Read through each section in order, completing activities as you go.
Use the story, grammar reference, and vocabulary documents alongside this lesson.
Ask your AI tutor whenever you are stuck or want more practice.
Bring your questions and ideas to your twice-weekly teacher sessions.
Track your progress using the self-assessment checklist at the end.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Understand and use 31 new vocabulary words related to thought, strategy, environment, and persuasion in your reading, writing, and speaking.
Identify and construct complex noun phrases using pre-modifiers (adjectives, determiners) and post-modifiers (prepositional phrases, relative clauses, participle phrases).
Form and use the future passive voice (will be + past participle) to describe actions that will happen in the future.
Use connectives for contrast and purpose (although, however, in order to, so that) to link ideas clearly and logically in your writing.
Use past modals (should have / should not have + past participle) to express regret, advice, and criticism about past events.
Convert direct speech to reported speech, changing pronouns, verb tenses, and time expressions appropriately.
Identify inconsistencies in arguments by checking whether evidence supports claims and spotting contradictions.
Read and analyse a narrative text ("Shifting Currents") for themes, character development, vocabulary in context, and grammar patterns.
Speak confidently about environmental issues, using formal and informal registers as appropriate.
Write persuasive and descriptive paragraphs that use complex noun phrases, connectives, and vocabulary from this unit.
Reflect on sustainability issues in your own community and discuss them using evidence-based reasoning.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you explain what a learning objective is and why it helps me study?
Or ask: Which of these objectives will be hardest for me, and how can I prepare?
Vocabulary Preview

Before you read the story or study the grammar, take some time to preview the vocabulary for this unit. Previewing vocabulary means looking at new words before you encounter them in a text, so that when you see them in context, you already have some idea of what they mean. This makes reading easier and helps words stick in your memory.
The 31 Words at a Glance

Here are the vocabulary words for this unit, grouped into categories to help you see connections between them. You do not need to memorise all of them right now—just get a sense of the words and their general meanings. You will study each one in detail later.
Feelings and Attitudes: somber, adamant, haughty, disdain, naive, persistent
Describing Quality and Condition: pristine, deceptive, dismal, egregious, threadbare, terse, copious
Actions and Processes: falter, modify, surpass, avail, subside, pivot, discern, instill
Ideas and Language: doctrine, dialect, antonym, tactic, transition, attribute, credible
Problems and Mistakes: amiss, awry, precise
Preview Activity: First Impressions

Look at the words listed above. For each category, choose two words that you have never seen before. Write them down in your notebook. Then try to guess their meaning based on the category name. For example, if you chose egregious from the "Describing Quality and Condition" group, you might guess it describes something in a strong way. After guessing, check the vocabulary document to see how close you were.

This preview activity activates your brain and prepares it to recognise the words when they appear in the story and grammar exercises. Research shows that even brief exposure to a new word before reading helps you learn it faster and remember it longer.
Words You Already Know

Some of these words may already be familiar to you. Words like precise, naive, and transition appear in everyday English. If you already know a word, that is great—focus your energy on the words that are completely new. However, even familiar words can have surprising uses. For example, avail is an unusual word that most English speakers rarely use in casual conversation, but it appears in formal writing and in set phrases like "to no avail." Pay attention to how each word is used in the story, because context often reveals shades of meaning that a dictionary definition cannot capture.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you give me three example sentences using the word egregious?
Or ask: What is the difference between naive and innocent?
Grammar Introduction

This unit covers several important grammar topics. Your main focus is on complex noun phrases, but you will also work with the future passive voice, connectives for contrast and purpose, and past modals. In this section, you will get an overview of each topic. For detailed explanations, examples, exercises, and answer keys, refer to the Grammar Reference document.
Complex Noun Phrases (Main Focus)

A complex noun phrase is a noun with extra words that give more information about it. Instead of writing "the mistake," you might write "the egregious environmental mistake that went unnoticed for years." The extra words—called modifiers—make your writing more detailed and precise.

There are two types of modifiers. Pre-modifiers come before the noun and are usually adjectives or determiners: "the pristine blue water." Post-modifiers come after the noun and can be prepositional phrases, relative clauses, or participle phrases: "the water in the lake," "the boy who helped gather samples."

Mastering complex noun phrases is one of the most important steps in improving your academic English. When you can describe things in detail using a single noun phrase, your writing becomes more efficient and more sophisticated. You will practise identifying complex noun phrases in the story and building your own in the exercises.

Why does this matter for you? In academic writing, in exam answers, and in formal speaking, you need to communicate a lot of information clearly and concisely. Complex noun phrases let you pack meaning into fewer words. Instead of writing three short sentences—"There was a mistake. It was environmental. It was egregious."—you can write one powerful sentence: "The egregious environmental mistake caused widespread damage."
Future Passive Voice

The future passive follows the pattern: Subject + will be + past participle. You use it when the important thing is what will happen, not who will do it. For example: "The purification systems will be installed by December." This structure appears in formal writing, news reports, and official announcements. In the story, the mayor uses the future passive when announcing the town’s plans.

You already know the present and past passive from earlier units. The future passive simply adds "will" to the pattern. The key challenge is remembering to include "be" and to use the correct past participle form of the verb. Common errors include writing "will installed" instead of "will be installed" or using the base form instead of the past participle.
Connectives for Contrast and Purpose
Connectives are the words and phrases that join ideas together and show the relationship between them. In this unit, you will focus on two categories:
Contrast connectives show that two ideas are different or that the second idea is surprising. Examples: although, however, yet, despite, while, on the other hand.
Purpose connectives explain why an action is done. Examples: in order to, so that, to, so as to.

Using connectives well is essential for writing strong arguments and clear explanations. When you write a persuasive paragraph about sustainability, for example, you might use "although" to acknowledge a different viewpoint before presenting your own: "Although some people believe traditional methods are sufficient, the scientific evidence suggests otherwise."

A common mistake is using "but" after "although." Remember: although already signals contrast, so "but" is redundant. "Although it was cold, but we went outside" is wrong. Simply write: "Although it was cold, we went outside."
Past Modals: Should Have / Should Not Have

The structure should have + past participle expresses regret or criticism about the past. It tells us that something was a good idea, but it did not happen. For example: "I should have practised public speaking more" means Yusuf wishes he had practised, but he did not.

The negative form, should not have + past participle, tells us that something happened but it was the wrong thing to do. For example: "They should not have thrown waste into the sea" means the action happened, but it was a mistake.

Past modals are useful when discussing environmental issues because they allow you to reflect on mistakes and suggest what could have been done differently. In your writing and speaking activities, you will use past modals to discuss what communities should have done to protect natural resources.
Reported Speech (Review)

You have studied reported speech in earlier units. In this unit, you will review and practise converting direct speech (exact words in quotation marks) to reported speech (telling someone what was said, without quoting directly). The key changes involve shifting the verb tense back one step, changing pronouns, and adjusting time expressions. For example: "I am happy" becomes "She said she was happy."
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you explain complex noun phrases using examples from the story?
Or ask: What is the difference between should have and must have?
You could also ask: Give me five sentences using connectives for contrast.
Reading and Listening
Before You Read

Before you begin reading "Shifting Currents," take a few minutes to activate your background knowledge. This means thinking about what you already know about the topic so that your brain is ready to make connections as you read.
Think about these questions:
What natural resources does your community depend on? Think about water, land, animals, and plants.
Have you ever noticed pollution or environmental damage in your area? What did it look like?
What do you think the title "Shifting Currents" might mean? What could be shifting?
Do you think it is disrespectful to question traditional practices? Why or why not?
What role should young people play in protecting the environment?
Write brief answers to at least three of these questions in your notebook. You will return to them after reading the story to see if your ideas have changed.
Reading the Story
Now open the Story Document and read "Shifting Currents" from beginning to end. As you read, pay attention to the following:
Vocabulary words in bold — notice how each word is used in context. Can you guess the meaning before checking the vocabulary list?
Grammar patterns — look for complex noun phrases, future passive sentences, connectives, and past modals.
Character development — how does Yusuf change from the beginning to the end? What about his grandfather?
Theme — what is the story really about? Is it just about pollution, or is there a deeper message about people and change?

Read the story once for overall understanding. Do not worry about every unfamiliar word on the first reading. Just enjoy the narrative and get a sense of the plot, characters, and setting. Then read it a second time more carefully, looking up vocabulary words and noting grammar patterns.
Comprehension Check
After reading the story, answer the following questions in complete sentences. Try to use vocabulary from the unit in your answers.
What is the main environmental problem in Yusuf’s town?
Why does Yusuf’s grandfather initially resist the idea of change?
What does the Green Guardians Club do to gather evidence about the pollution?
How does the town respond to the students’ presentation?
What does Yusuf mean when he says "the currents are shifting"?
Why do you think the story is called "Shifting Currents"? What different kinds of "currents" might it refer to?
Find one sentence in the story that uses a complex noun phrase and write it down. Label the head noun and the modifiers.
Find one sentence that uses the future passive and explain why the passive is used there instead of the active voice.
Listening Activity

If you have access to a text-to-speech tool or your AI tutor can read aloud, listen to the story being read while you follow along with the text. Pay attention to how the vocabulary words are pronounced. After listening, practise reading one paragraph aloud yourself. Focus on clear pronunciation, appropriate pauses, and natural intonation. Record yourself if possible and listen back to check your fluency.

If you do not have access to audio, practise reading the story aloud on your own. Choose a paragraph that you find particularly powerful or interesting and read it three times, each time trying to sound more natural and confident.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you read the first paragraph of the story aloud for me?
Or ask: How do you pronounce the word egregious?
You could also ask: Can you give me a summary of the story in simple English?
Speaking
Speaking Activity 1: Story Discussion
Prepare to discuss the story with your teacher and classmates in your next session. Think about the following discussion questions and make brief notes so you are ready to share your ideas.
Do you agree with Yusuf that traditional practices should be questioned when they cause harm? Explain your reasoning.
The grandfather says, "Change does not mean disrespect. It means survival." What do you think he means? Do you agree?
Halima says, "If we do nothing, our message will subside like the wind." Can you think of a time when you stayed silent about something important? What happened?
How does the story connect to the Islamic principle of stewardship (being a good caretaker of the earth)?

When you speak, try to use at least three vocabulary words from this unit and at least one complex noun phrase in your responses. For example, instead of saying "the problem," you could say "the serious pollution problem that affects our coastline."
Speaking Activity 2: Persuasive Mini-Speech

Prepare a short speech (about one to two minutes) on one of the following topics. Your speech should include at least one example of each grammar pattern studied in this unit: a complex noun phrase, a future passive sentence, a connective for contrast or purpose, and a past modal.
Why our school should start a recycling programme.
What our community should have done differently to protect local water sources.
Although technology can help the environment, people must also change their habits.
The most important natural resource in our region and why it must be protected.
Structure your speech like this:
Opening: State your main argument clearly in one or two sentences.
Body: Give two or three supporting points with evidence or examples.
Conclusion: Summarise your argument and end with a call to action or a thought-provoking question.

Practise your speech at least three times before your teacher session. Time yourself to make sure you stay within two minutes. Ask a family member to listen if possible, or record yourself and play it back.
Speaking Activity 3: Role Play
Work with a partner (or use your AI tutor as a conversation partner) to role-play one of the following scenarios:

Yusuf and the shopkeeper: Yusuf tries to persuade a shopkeeper to stop throwing waste behind the market. The shopkeeper thinks the old ways are fine. Use connectives for contrast (although, however, yet) in your conversation.

Halima and an elder: Halima explains the scientific evidence to a sceptical elder. The elder believes in traditional stewardship. Use complex noun phrases to describe the evidence (for example, "the precise data collected from multiple water samples").

Yusuf and a radio host: The radio host interviews Yusuf about his success. Yusuf reflects on what the community should have done earlier. Use past modals (should have / should not have) in your answers.
After the role play, reflect on how it went. Did you use the target grammar? Did you sound natural? What would you do differently next time?
Pronunciation Focus
Several vocabulary words in this unit contain sounds that learners sometimes find tricky. Practise saying these words aloud, paying attention to the stressed syllable (shown in capital letters):
pristine — pris-TEEN
egregious — eh-GREE-jus
adamant — AD-uh-munt
doctrine — DOK-trin
deceptive — deh-SEP-tiv
copious — KOH-pee-us
threadbare — THRED-bair
discern — dih-SURN
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you practise a role play with me? You be the shopkeeper and I will be Yusuf.
Or ask: Can you listen to me pronounce these words and tell me if I sound correct?
You could also ask: Give me feedback on my mini-speech about recycling.
Writing
Writing Task 1: Descriptive Paragraph

Write a descriptive paragraph (100–150 words) about a natural place you have visited or know well. It could be a beach, a river, a forest, a garden, or any outdoor space. Use at least three complex noun phrases and five vocabulary words from this unit.
Planning your paragraph:
Choose your place and think about what makes it special.
List sensory details: what do you see, hear, smell, and feel there?
Select vocabulary words that fit naturally. For example, you might describe the place as pristine, or mention that something has gone awry.
Build complex noun phrases to add detail. Instead of "the trees," write "the tall, ancient trees along the riverbank."
Write a topic sentence that introduces the place, then add supporting sentences with your descriptions.
End with a concluding sentence that expresses your feelings about the place.
Example opening sentence: "The wide, sandy beach near my grandmother’s house is one of the most pristine places I have ever seen."
Writing Task 2: Persuasive Letter
Imagine you are Yusuf writing a formal letter to the town council, asking them to fund a clean-up programme for the coastline. Your letter should be 150–200 words and include the following:
A formal greeting and introduction stating the purpose of the letter.
At least two reasons why the clean-up is necessary, supported by evidence.
At least one sentence using the future passive (for example, "Waste will be collected from the shore every Saturday").
At least one sentence using a connective for contrast (for example, "Although some residents believe the sea will heal on its own, the evidence shows otherwise").
At least one sentence using a past modal (for example, "We should have acted sooner to prevent this damage").
A formal closing.
Structure your letter like this:
Greeting: Dear Mayor and Council Members,
Introduction: State who you are and why you are writing.
Body paragraph 1: Present the problem with evidence.
Body paragraph 2: Propose your solution and explain how it will help.
Conclusion: Summarise and make your request.
Closing: Yours sincerely, [Your name]
Writing Task 3: Reflective Journal Entry

Write a journal entry (100–150 words) from Yusuf’s perspective after the town hall meeting. How does he feel? What does he think went well? What does he wish had gone differently? Use at least two examples of past modals and three vocabulary words from the unit.

Think about Yusuf’s emotions at this point in the story. He has just given a public speech for the first time. Some people responded positively, while others were hostile. How would that feel? What would he be thinking about as he walks home?
Writing Tips for This Unit
Here are some tips to make your writing stronger in this unit:
Use complex noun phrases to pack information into your sentences. This shows a higher level of English proficiency.
Vary your connectives. Do not use "but" for every contrast—try although, however, yet, or despite instead.
Show, do not tell. Instead of writing "the beach was dirty," describe what you see: "Plastic wrappers and broken bottles littered the once-pristine sand."
Use vocabulary words naturally. Do not force words into sentences where they do not fit. Only use a word if it genuinely improves the sentence.

Check your grammar after writing. Look specifically for the patterns you have studied: Are your noun phrases correctly ordered? Have you used "be" in your passive sentences? Did you use the past participle after "should have"?
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you check my descriptive paragraph for grammar mistakes?
Or ask: Can you give me feedback on my persuasive letter and suggest improvements?
You could also ask: Help me make my noun phrases more complex in this paragraph.
Activities and Exercises
This section contains mixed-skill activities that bring together vocabulary, grammar, reading, and writing. Complete them in order, or choose the ones your teacher recommends.
Activity 1: Vocabulary in the Story
Go back to the story and find each of the following vocabulary words. For each word, copy the sentence where it appears and write a short explanation of what the word means in that context.
pristine (appears twice — find both uses)
faltered
adamant
copious
egregious
threadbare
instilled
credible
doctrine
discern

After finding each word, write a new sentence of your own using the same word. Try to use a different context from the story. For example, if the story uses "pristine" to describe water, use it to describe something else—perhaps a classroom, a piece of clothing, or a natural landscape you know.
Activity 2: Grammar Hunt
Read through the story one more time, looking specifically for the following grammar patterns. Write down one example of each.
A complex noun phrase with at least two modifiers (for example, "a dedicated group of students researching the coastline’s decline").
A sentence in the future passive voice (for example, "The first units will be installed by December").
A sentence using a connective for contrast (for example, "Although many resisted change, we remained adamant").
A sentence using a past modal (for example, "I should have practised public speaking more").
A sentence that could be converted to reported speech (for example, direct speech in quotation marks that you can rewrite indirectly).
For the reported speech example, write both the original direct speech and your converted reported speech version. Remember to shift the tense back and change the pronouns.
Activity 3: Debate Preparation
The story features a debate about traditional practices vs. modern environmental policies. Prepare your own debate notes on the following motion:
"This house believes that traditional practices should be modified when scientific evidence shows they cause environmental harm."
Divide a page into two columns:
For the motion (agree): List at least three arguments in favour. Use evidence from the story and your own knowledge.
Against the motion (disagree): List at least three arguments against. Consider the value of tradition, cultural identity, and community trust.

For each argument, write a supporting sentence that uses at least one grammar pattern from this unit. For example: "Although traditional methods have served communities for generations, the precise data collected by environmental researchers shows that some practices cause serious harm that should have been addressed decades ago."
Activity 4: Complex Noun Phrase Challenge
Expand each of the following simple sentences by replacing the underlined noun with a complex noun phrase. The expanded sentence should be grammatically correct and sound natural.
The students presented their findings. → ____________ presented their findings.
We tested the water. → We tested ____________.
The man disagreed with Yusuf. → ____________ disagreed with Yusuf.
Halima designed a programme. → Halima designed ____________.
The town needs help. → ____________ needs help.
Sample answers:
The persistent young students from the Green Guardians Club presented their findings.
We tested the murky brown water collected from near the harbour.
The haughty old man who refused to look at the evidence disagreed with Yusuf.
Halima designed an ambitious community recycling programme for the entire coastline.
The small, struggling town along the Somali coast needs help.
Activity 5: Tense Transformation
Rewrite each sentence in the future passive voice.
The volunteers will clean the beach. → ____________
The government will fund the project. → ____________
Students will present the data at the conference. → ____________
Engineers will install solar panels on the school roof. → ____________
The community will elect a new environmental committee. → ____________
Answers:
The beach will be cleaned by the volunteers.
The project will be funded by the government.
The data will be presented at the conference (by students).
Solar panels will be installed on the school roof (by engineers).
A new environmental committee will be elected by the community.
Activity 6: Connective Cloze Passage
Fill in the blanks with the correct connective from the box. Each word is used once.
Word bank: although, however, in order to, so that, yet, despite

Yusuf and his friends worked hard (1) ____________ prove that the coastline was in danger. They collected water samples and documented fish populations (2) ____________ the town council would take them seriously. (3) ____________ the evidence was clear, many elders refused to change their habits. (4) ____________ this resistance, the students did not give up. They organised a town meeting, (5) ____________ not everyone attended. (6) ____________, the people who did come were moved by the presentation and agreed to support the clean-up.
Answers: 1. in order to, 2. so that, 3. Although, 4. Despite, 5. yet, 6. However

Vocabulary Reinforcement

By now, you have encountered the Unit 5 vocabulary words in the story, in grammar examples, and in exercises. This section gives you additional practice to help the words move from your short-term memory to your long-term memory. The more ways you engage with a word—reading it, writing it, speaking it, and hearing it—the better you will remember it.
Strategy 1: Context Clue Practice
Read each sentence below. Use the context (the surrounding words) to work out the meaning of the vocabulary word in bold. Then check your answer against the vocabulary list.
The once-pristine river was now filled with plastic bags and oil stains, a sad reminder of how much had changed.
Despite his persistent efforts to change their minds, the committee refused to reconsider their decision.
She spoke with such disdain that everyone in the room felt uncomfortable and unwelcome.
The coach decided to modify the training schedule after several players complained about exhaustion.
His argument seemed credible at first, but a closer look revealed several logical gaps.
The old map was threadbare at the folds, with some parts completely worn through.
After the initial panic, the crowd’s fear began to subside as the firefighters took control of the situation.
Her terse response—just a single word, "fine"—told me she was still upset about what had happened.
Strategy 2: Word Association Web

Choose five vocabulary words from the list. For each word, create a word association web in your notebook. Write the vocabulary word in the centre and draw lines connecting it to related words, synonyms, antonyms, and example situations. For instance, for the word adamant, your web might include: stubborn, determined, firm, refuse to change, Halima insisting on precise data.
Word association webs help you build connections between new words and words you already know. The more connections a word has in your memory, the easier it will be to recall when you need it.
Strategy 3: Vocabulary Sentences Challenge

Challenge yourself to write a short paragraph (five to seven sentences) about an environmental topic using at least eight vocabulary words from this unit. Your paragraph should make sense as a whole—do not just list sentences. Try to connect your ideas using connectives.

Example paragraph: "The pristine coastline that our grandparents once knew has changed dramatically. The transition from small fishing boats to industrial trawlers brought copious amounts of waste. Although many people remained adamant that the old ways were best, the precise data collected by scientists showed an egregious decline in fish populations. We should have acted sooner, but it is not too late to pivot and adopt more sustainable practices."
Notice how the example uses vocabulary words naturally and connects ideas with the connective "although." Your paragraph should flow just as smoothly.
Strategy 4: Flashcard Routine

Create a set of flashcards for the 31 vocabulary words. On the front of each card, write the word. On the back, write the definition, the part of speech, and one example sentence. Review your flashcards daily using the following method:
Day 1: Go through all 31 cards. Sort them into three piles: "I know it," "I sort of know it," and "I do not know it."
Day 2: Focus only on the "I sort of know it" and "I do not know it" piles. Try to move cards into the "I know it" pile.
Day 3: Go through all cards again. Any card you get wrong goes back to the review pile.
Day 4–7: Repeat the process. By the end of the week, aim to have all 31 cards in the "I know it" pile.
This spaced repetition approach is one of the most effective ways to memorise vocabulary. If you prefer digital flashcards, you can ask your AI tutor to quiz you instead.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you quiz me on the Unit 5 vocabulary words?
Or ask: Give me a fill-in-the-blank exercise using five random vocabulary words.
You could also ask: What are some synonyms for the word adamant?
Self-Study Tips
This unit is designed for self-paced learning, which means you are in charge of your own progress. Here are some tips to help you study effectively and get the most out of each section.
Tip 1: Read Actively

When you read the story or any other text, do not just let your eyes move over the words. Read actively by highlighting or underlining vocabulary words, writing questions in the margin, and making mental predictions about what will happen next. Active reading helps you engage with the text more deeply and improves both your comprehension and vocabulary retention.
Tip 2: Practise Grammar in Context

Grammar rules are easier to remember when you see them used in real sentences. After studying a grammar pattern in the Grammar Reference, go back to the story and find examples. Then try to write your own sentences using the same pattern but with different content. The goal is to move from recognising a pattern to producing it confidently.
Tip 3: Speak Out Loud Every Day

Even if you do not have a conversation partner, reading aloud for ten minutes a day will improve your pronunciation, fluency, and confidence. Choose a paragraph from the story, an exercise answer, or your own writing and read it out loud. Pay attention to stress patterns, pauses, and intonation.
Tip 4: Use Your AI Tutor Strategically

Your AI tutor is available around the clock and can help with almost anything: explaining grammar rules, checking your writing, quizzing you on vocabulary, practising conversations, or answering questions about the story. Do not be afraid to ask for help—that is what it is there for. The more specific your question, the better the answer will be. Instead of asking "Help me with grammar," try asking "Can you explain why we use should have instead of should in this sentence?"
Tip 5: Keep a Learning Journal

Set aside a notebook or a section of your exercise book for reflections. After each study session, write two or three sentences about what you learned, what you found difficult, and what you want to practise next. This helps you track your progress and identify areas that need more work. Review your journal entries before your teacher sessions so you can ask targeted questions.
Tip 6: Connect to Your Own Life

The best way to remember new language is to use it to talk about things that matter to you. Think about how the themes of this unit—natural resources, sustainability, community action, tradition vs. change—relate to your own life and community. Write or speak about these connections using the vocabulary and grammar from the unit.

Use this suggested schedule to spread your study across the week. You can adjust it to fit your own timetable, but try to study a little every day rather than doing everything in one long session. Consistent daily practice is more effective than occasional cramming.

Shifting Currents
Year 8 – Unit 5 Story
About This Story
Theme: Change, belief, and environmental awareness
Setting: A coastal fishing town on the Somali shoreline of East Africa
Genre: Realistic fiction with reflective, social, and emotional layers
Moral: Persistent awareness and careful questioning can shift even the most rigid beliefs.
Vocabulary to Watch For
As you read, look out for the following words. They appear in bold throughout the story. Try to work out their meaning from the context before checking the vocabulary list.
Shifting Currents
Chapter 1: Beneath the Surface
The sea was still—pristine, at first glance. But Yusuf knew better. A closer look revealed plastic bottles snagged in old fishing nets and wrappers drifting near the ancient coral rocks.

The town of Marka nestled along the shoreline like an old friend who had seen better days. Once a thriving hub of fishing and family gatherings on the sand, the coastline had become a quiet shadow of its former self. The transition from small-scale traditional fishing to larger commercial boats had changed the rhythm of life, and the amount of waste along the beach had started to surpass what the sea could naturally break down.
Still, every morning after Fajr prayer, Yusuf jogged the shore, noting what others missed. "They think it is clean," he muttered, pausing to pick up a straw. "But it is a deceptive beauty."
Back home, his sister Amina sat by the window with a cup of shaah, scribbling in a notebook. Her school was organising an environmental debate.
"Topic: Traditional practice vs. modern policy," she read aloud. "We have to present both sides."
"Why both?" Yusuf asked. "The old ways failed us."
"Some would disagree," she replied. "They think change is disrespectful."

Their grandfather entered the room, his walking stick tapping gently on the tiled floor. "You two speak as if nature is new. In my day, no one needed science to respect the sea. The antonym of respect is not science—it is carelessness."
"True," Yusuf said gently. "But now we have evidence. Pollution. Fish loss."
Grandfather frowned. "The sea will heal, if left alone."
Chapter 2: The Green Guardians
At school, Yusuf joined the Green Guardians Club, a dedicated group of students researching the coastline’s decline. He was surprised how quickly the group formed a bond, driven by a shared purpose.
Their leader, Halima, was adamant that they collect precise data. "We cannot just feel sad. We need to prove what is happening."
They tested water samples and documented fish populations. "This part smells awful," one student said, holding a tube up to the light.
"That is the pristine part—gone," Yusuf replied.
Still, he often faltered when speaking to older residents. Many clung to the belief that their old ways were flawless.
"Your tactics are too aggressive," said one shopkeeper. "We have always thrown our waste behind the market. What is the harm?"
"It flows into the sea," Yusuf explained. "Small actions avail big consequences."
Some nodded slowly. Others shook their heads. Yusuf could sense something amiss in the way some of the elders dismissed their findings without even looking at the evidence.

One evening, the club reviewed all the coastal data, spread across a table in copious charts and graphs. The pH levels were dangerously off. Fish counts were down by nearly half. The figures spoke clearly. Yet the town’s mood remained somber.
"I do not understand," said a member named Farah. "The evidence is right here. Why does not everyone believe us?"
"It is not just about facts," Halima replied. "Some people follow an older doctrine—one that says any change is disrespect. To them, we are just noisy children."

Over time, Yusuf learned to discern the difference between genuine concern for tradition and stubborn refusal to accept evidence. Not everyone who disagreed was wrong—some simply needed more time and more patient conversation.
Chapter 3: Preparing to Speak
Halima suggested they make a presentation. "A formal one. In the community hall near the masjid."
"But will anyone come?" a student asked.
"If we do nothing," Halima replied, "our message will subside like the wind."

They planned for weeks. Posters. Flyers distributed after Jumu’ah prayers. Yusuf rehearsed his speech with persistent effort. He memorised key phrases and practised pauses, adjusting his tone each time. He knew they needed to modify their approach—not abandon their message, but present it in a way that respected the elders while still delivering the truth.
"I should have practised public speaking more," he said nervously the night before the event.
"You are doing fine," Amina encouraged. "Just be yourself. Not too terse, but not long-winded either."
Chapter 4: The Town Hall Meeting
Finally, the day arrived.

Chairs filled slowly inside the large community hall. A few elders sat with folded arms. Others leaned forward with interest. The town’s mayor even arrived, bringing with her a small team of councillors.
Yusuf stood at the front, his heart beating fast.
"This coastline is not dying. It is being killed. Not on purpose, perhaps. But by habit. Tradition is important, yes. But when habits hurt, they must be questioned."
He projected slides showing algae blooms and piles of dead fish washed up on the sand. "This is the egregious result of ignorance, not evil. And we have the power to change it."
A murmur ran through the crowd.
One man stood up. "You blame us. But what have you done?"
Halima stepped up beside Yusuf. "We test. We clean. We educate. And we ask you to help us pivot. To change not our values, but our methods."
She pointed to a small boy in the front row. "He helped gather water samples. He skipped cartoons for this."
Laughter rippled through the room, lightening the mood.
Chapter 5: Shifting Ground
Over the weeks that followed, something shifted.

Younger children helped collect waste along the beach. Some shopkeepers placed bins outside their stores. A local tailor even designed reusable bags printed with coastal slogans in both Somali and English.

But resistance remained. One haughty elder looked at the students with open disdain and refused to attend any further meetings. A community leader who misunderstood the movement claimed it mocked their elders. "It insults our doctrine of stewardship," he argued at the evening gathering. "Modern ways bring dismal outcomes."
"They should have joined us," Halima said, her eyes flashing. "Instead they speak in anger."
"They are not bad people," Yusuf said quietly. "Just naive about the science."
Still, not everyone could be reached. One afternoon, an old man shouted at Yusuf as he was picking up rubbish near the harbour. "Go preach somewhere else!"
Yusuf walked away, his heart heavy. "Maybe it has all gone awry," he whispered.

Amina disagreed. "Look again." She pointed across the beach to a small boy carefully picking up plastic wrappers and placing them in a bag. "You have instilled new habits in people. That matters more than you realise."
Chapter 6: A Wider Stage
A month later, Yusuf was invited to speak at the regional youth summit in Mogadishu.
He wore a clean white shirt and shoes that felt a bit threadbare, but he stood tall at the podium.
"We believed the sea was healthy because it looked blue," he said to the packed hall. "But beneath the surface, we found decay. Some data had been hidden. Some opinions were loud but not credible."
He paused and looked out at the audience. "Although many resisted change, we remained adamant. Not out of pride, but out of hope."
His speech earned a standing ovation. Later, a local radio host asked him, "What do you attribute your success to?"
Yusuf thought for a moment. "To listening. To questioning. And to never letting doubt falter our progress."
Chapter 7: New Beginnings

In the following months, grants were secured for the town. Solar-powered water purification systems were ordered from the capital. According to the mayor, "The first units will be installed by December." Halima reported that the council had confirmed the timeline, and she stated that the transition to cleaner water management had already begun.
Halima was invited to join the national youth council for environmental leadership. Yusuf received an internship at a marine research centre near the coast.
At home, their grandfather started walking with Yusuf along the shore again, just as they had done when Yusuf was a small child.
"You were right," the old man said softly. "Change does not mean disrespect. It means survival."
Yusuf nodded. "We should have listened to each other sooner."
Chapter 8: Still Waters, Moving Hearts

On a quiet Thursday morning, Yusuf visited the shore alone. A heron lifted its wings and glided low over the surface of the water. The air was still, but not dead. It was alive with momentum—slow but sure.
He thought about dialects. About how people say the same thing in different words. Maybe the word "clean" meant something different to his grandfather. Maybe "progress" felt like loss to some elders.
But in every tongue, care was still care. And in every heart, the sea had meaning.
Yusuf smiled and whispered a quiet du’a of thanks. The currents were shifting—not just in the water, but in the minds and hearts of his community.

Vocabulary List
Year 8 – Unit 5: Natural Resources and Sustainability
This document contains all the vocabulary for Unit 5. Study the words, their meanings, and the example sentences carefully. Then complete the practice exercises to strengthen your understanding.
Word List
Words Grouped by Theme
Feelings and Attitudes
somber — dark or serious in mood
adamant — not willing to change your mind
haughty — thinking you are better than others
disdain — a feeling of strong dislike or lack of respect
naive — lacking experience; easily fooled
persistent — continuing even when it is difficult
Describing Quality and Condition
pristine — clean, fresh, unspoiled
deceptive — misleading; making someone believe something untrue
dismal — sad and gloomy
egregious — very bad or shocking
threadbare — very worn out, with holes
terse — using few words, sometimes rudely
copious — very large in amount
Actions and Processes
falter — to hesitate or lose strength
modify — to change slightly to improve
surpass — to go beyond a limit or do better than
avail — to make use of; to help
subside — to become less strong or intense
pivot — to turn or change direction
discern — to notice or understand clearly
instill — to gradually teach a value or feeling
Ideas and Language
doctrine — a set of beliefs taught by a group
dialect — a form of a language spoken in a specific area
antonym — a word that means the opposite of another
tactic — a plan or method to achieve a goal
transition — a change from one state to another
attribute — a quality or feature; or to assign a cause
credible — believable or trustworthy
Problems and Mistakes
amiss — not quite right; something is wrong
awry — not happening as planned
precise — exact and accurate (the opposite of vague)
