The haughty student refused to work in a group with anyone else.
If you study hard, you can surpass your own expectations.
The grey sky and cold rain made the morning feel dismal.
Our tactic for winning the debate was to present the strongest evidence first.
When I arrived and saw the empty classroom, I knew something was amiss.
People in different parts of Somalia speak different dialects of the Somali language.
Exercise 4: Antonym Pairs (Sample Answers)
pristine → dirty / polluted
copious → scarce / meagre
somber → cheerful / bright
haughty → humble / modest
terse → wordy / verbose
credible → unbelievable / incredible
naive → experienced / worldly / sophisticated
persistent → quitting / giving up easily
Exercise 5: Word Families
tables: to hesitate or lose strength when doing something
She began to falter in her speech when she saw the large crowd.
clean, fresh, and in its original condition; not spoiled or damaged
The lake was so pristine that we could see all the fish swimming in it.
making someone believe something that is not true; misleading
The magician's tricks were so deceptive that the audience could not figure them out.
lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived
She was so naive that she believed every story she heard.
a word that means the opposite of another word
a plan or method used to achieve a goal
The team used a clever tactic to win the game.
continuing to do something even when it is difficult or when others give up
She was persistent in studying for her exams, even when the subjects were hard.
a change from one state or condition to another
The transition from middle school to high school can be exciting and challenging.
dark or serious in mood; not cheerful
The sky was somber and grey, making everyone feel a little sad.
not willing to change your mind or opinion about something
She was adamant that she would not change her plans for the weekend.
to change something slightly to make it better or more suitable
We can modify our project to include more pictures.
to do better than someone or something; to go beyond a limit or expectation
She worked hard to surpass her previous test scores.
to make use of something or to help someone in a situation
She decided to avail herself of the extra study time before the exam.
to become less strong or intense; to calm down or decrease
After the storm passed, the wind began to subside and everything became quiet.
not quite right; something is wrong or not as it should be
When I saw the broken window, I knew something was amiss.
not in the correct position or not happening as planned
The plan went awry when it started to rain during the picnic.
to turn or rotate around a central point; to change direction or approach
In basketball, a player can pivot to change direction while keeping one foot on the ground.
to notice or understand something clearly
She could discern the difference between the two paintings.
believable or trustworthy; something that can be trusted
The scientist gave a credible explanation for the experiment.
exact and accurate; not vague or general
The teacher asked for a precise answer to the maths problem.
a quality or feature; or to say that something was caused by
One important attribute of a good friend is honesty.
to gradually teach someone an idea or feeling so it becomes part of their character
Teachers try to instill a love of reading in their students.
thinking you are better than others; proud and unfriendly
The haughty girl looked down on her classmates and never wanted to play with them.
a set of beliefs or ideas that are taught by a group or organisation
The school's doctrine emphasises respect and kindness among students.
a form of a language spoken in a particular area or by a specific group
In England, people in different regions speak different dialects.
using few words, often in a way that seems rude or unfriendly
His terse reply made it clear that he did not want to talk.
very large in amount or quantity
She took copious notes during the lecture to remember everything.
sad and gloomy; not cheerful or happy
The weather was dismal, with dark clouds and rain all day.
The student's egregious mistake on the test surprised the teacher.
very worn out or old, often with holes or thin spots
His threadbare jacket had so many holes that it was hard to keep warm.
a feeling of strong dislike or lack of respect for someone or something
She looked at the messy room with disdain, thinking it needed to be cleaned right away.
b) a word that means the opposite of another
c) to hesitate or lose strength
e) a set of beliefs taught by a group
f) to change slightly to improve
g) using few words, sometimes rudely
h) not willing to change your mind
j) a feeling of strong dislike or lack of respect

paragraphs: Year 8 — Unit 6 Grammar
Comparatives, Superlatives, and More

This document is your comprehensive grammar reference for Unit 6. It covers all seven grammar topics in detail, with explanations, examples, exercises, and answer keys. Study each section carefully, complete the exercises, and check your answers. If you need extra help, ask your AI Tutor.
Topic 1: Comparatives and Superlatives
What This Rule Means

Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives that help you compare things. When you want to describe how one thing is different from another, you use a comparative. When you want to show that something stands out the most in a group of three or more, you use a superlative.
How to Form Comparatives
The way you form a comparative depends on the length of the adjective:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -er to the end. Example: tall becomes taller, fast becomes faster, old becomes older.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: Change the -y to -i and add -er. Example: happy becomes happier, easy becomes easier, busy becomes busier.
Adjectives with two or more syllables: Place more before the adjective. Example: beautiful becomes more beautiful, difficult becomes more difficult, interesting becomes more interesting.
After the comparative, use than to introduce the second item in the comparison. For example: "The mountain trail is steeper than the coastal path."
How to Form Superlatives
Superlatives follow a similar pattern:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -est to the end. Example: tall becomes the tallest, fast becomes the fastest.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: Change the -y to -i and add -est. Example: happy becomes the happiest, easy becomes the easiest.
Adjectives with two or more syllables: Place most before the adjective. Example: beautiful becomes the most beautiful, difficult becomes the most difficult.
Always use the before a superlative. For example: "Mount Kilimanjaro is the tallest mountain in Africa."
Spelling Rules
If a one-syllable adjective ends in a single vowel followed by a single consonant, double the consonant before adding -er or -est. Example: big becomes bigger / biggest, hot becomes hotter / hottest.
If an adjective ends in -e, just add -r or -st. Example: large becomes larger / largest.
If an adjective ends in -y, change the -y to -i before adding -er or -est.
Common Mistakes
Double marking: Do not use "more" and "-er" together. Say "taller," not "more taller." Say "most beautiful," not "most beautifulest."
Using comparatives for three or more items: Use superlatives, not comparatives, when comparing three or more things. Say "She is the tallest of the three," not "She is the taller of the three."
Forgetting 'the' before superlatives: Always include "the" before a superlative. Say "the best," not just "best."
Examples from the Story
"His actions were often too bold, too fast, too audacious." (Here, comparisons are implied through repetition.)
"The sun is hitting harder now." (Comparative adverb)
"The most crucial choice is to wait." (Superlative)
Exercise 1A — Fill in the Blanks
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
The Simien Mountains are __________ (high) than the hills near the coast.
This is the __________ (beautiful) sunset I have ever seen.
Amina is __________ (careful) than Khalid when making decisions.
The highland air is __________ (thin) than the air at sea level.
Of all the routes, the long path was the __________ (safe).
Today's weather is __________ (hot) than yesterday's.
The citadel is the __________ (impressive) building in the region.
Walking is __________ (slow) than running, but it is also __________ (steady).
Answer Key — Exercise 1A
higher
most beautiful
more careful
thinner
safest
hotter
most impressive
slower / steadier
Topic 2: Irregular Comparative Forms
What This Rule Means
Some adjectives do not follow the regular rules for forming comparatives and superlatives. These are called irregular forms, and you must memorise them. The most common irregular forms are:
Examples
"Amina's plan was better than Khalid's shortcut idea."
"The weather today is worse than it was yesterday."
"This is the best solution for the whole team."
"That was the worst storm the highlands had seen in years."
"The second campsite is farther from the citadel than the first."
Common Mistakes
Do not say "gooder" or "goodest" — always use better and best.
Do not say "badder" or "baddest" — always use worse and worst.
Do not say "more good" or "most good" — these forms are incorrect.
Exercise 2A — Fill in the Blanks
My grandmother's cooking is __________ (good) than the school canteen's.
This maths problem is __________ (bad) than the last one.
Of all the students, Amina is the __________ (good) at leading the team.
That test was the __________ (bad) I have ever taken.
The village is __________ (far) from the city than I thought.
He ate __________ (little) food today than yesterday.
Answer Key — Exercise 2A
better
worse
best
worst
farther / further
less
Topic 3: Comparative Adverb Structures
What This Rule Means

Comparative adverbs help you compare how actions are performed. Just as you compare adjectives (bigger, more beautiful), you can compare adverbs (faster, more carefully). This allows you to describe differences in how people do things.
How to Form Comparative Adverbs
One-syllable adverbs: Add -er. Example: fast becomes faster, hard becomes harder.
Adverbs of two or more syllables (usually ending in -ly): Use more or less before the adverb. Example: quickly becomes more quickly, carefully becomes more carefully, quietly becomes less quietly.
Irregular adverbs: Well becomes better, badly becomes worse, far becomes farther or further.
Examples
"Amina climbed more carefully than Khalid."
"He completed the trail faster than anyone expected."
"She spoke less audibly when she realised others were listening."
"Farhan works harder than most of the other students."
"Amina speaks English better than she did last year."
Exercise 3A — Complete the Sentences
She can run __________ (quick) than her friend.
He speaks __________ (quiet) when he is thinking.
They completed the project __________ (efficient) than expected.
Khalid moved __________ (reckless) than the rest of the group.
The team worked __________ (hard) on the second day than on the first.
Answer Key — Exercise 3A
more quickly
more quietly
more efficiently
more recklessly
harder
Topic 4: Pronouns and Quantifiers
What This Rule Means

Pronouns are words that replace nouns to avoid repetition (he, she, it, they, anyone, nothing). Quantifiers tell you how much or how many of something there is (few, a few, all, some, every, none). Using these correctly makes your language clearer and more natural.
Key Pronouns and Quantifiers
Common Mistakes
Confusing few and a few: "Few" has a negative sense (not many), while "a few" is more positive (some, enough).
Confusing anyone and everyone: "Anyone" means any single person (it does not matter which), while "everyone" means all people without exception.
Using nothing when you mean anything: In negative sentences, use "anything." Say "I do not have anything," not "I do not have nothing" (which is a double negative).
Exercise 4A — Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word: anyone, nothing, few, a few, all, everyone.
__________ can answer this question if they think carefully.
I have __________ to do today — I finished everything yesterday.
__________ of the players scored a goal — only two out of fifteen.
__________ the cookies are gone! Someone ate the last one.
__________ enjoyed the trip — not a single person complained.
__________ students volunteered to stay late and help clean up.
Answer Key — Exercise 4A
Anyone
nothing
Few
All
Everyone
A few
Topic 5: Future Passive Voice
What This Rule Means
The future passive voice is used when you want to focus on the action being done rather than the person doing it. It describes something that will happen to the subject in the future.
Structure
Subject + will + be + past participle (+ by + agent)
The "by + agent" part is optional. You include it if you want to say who will perform the action. You leave it out if the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious.
Examples
"The trail will be cleared by the rangers."
"New trees will be planted along the river."
"The results will be announced tomorrow."
"The homework will be completed by the students."
"A presentation will be given by a local expert."
When to Use the Future Passive Voice
When the action is more important than who does it: "The bridge will be repaired next month."
When the doer is unknown: "The decision will be made soon."
In formal writing, such as reports and announcements: "Prizes will be awarded at the ceremony."
Common Mistakes
Forgetting "be" after "will": Say "will be done," not "will done."
Using the wrong verb form: Always use the past participle (completed, written, seen), not the base form or present tense.
Exercise 5A — Rewrite in the Future Passive Voice
Rewrite each sentence so that the object becomes the subject.
The volunteers will clean the park.
The school will organise a conservation day.
The chef will prepare the food.
The students will write the reports.
The city will build a new library.
Answer Key — Exercise 5A
The park will be cleaned by the volunteers.
A conservation day will be organised by the school.
The food will be prepared by the chef.
The reports will be written by the students.
A new library will be built by the city.
Topic 6: Reported Speech and Requests
What This Rule Means

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is how you tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. You change the tense, pronouns, and sometimes the time and place references. This is essential for storytelling, writing, and everyday conversation.
How to Convert Direct Speech to Reported Speech
Follow these steps:
Identify the speaker and the message. Example: Khalid said, "I am going to the citadel."
Change the reporting verb. Use "said," "told," "asked," "warned," "explained," etc.
Shift the tense back. Present simple becomes past simple. Present continuous becomes past continuous. "Will" becomes "would." "Can" becomes "could."
Change the pronouns. "I" often becomes "he" or "she." "We" may become "they."
Optionally add 'that'. "He said that he was going" or "He said he was going" — both are correct.
Tense Shift Table
Reporting Requests and Commands
For requests, use asked + person + to + base verb. For commands, use told + person + to + base verb. For negative commands, use told + person + not to + base verb.
Direct: "Please close the door." Reported: "He asked me to close the door."
Direct: "Do not take the shortcut." Reported: "The instructor told Khalid not to take the shortcut."
Direct: "Can you help me?" Reported: "She asked him to help her."
Examples from the Story
Khalid said: "I will meet you there." → Khalid said he would meet them there.
The instructor said: "He was warned not to break formation." → The instructor said that Khalid had been warned not to break formation.
Amina told the group: "We are a team." → Amina told the group that they were a team.
Exercise 6A — Convert to Reported Speech
"I love reading adventure stories," Halima said.
"We will finish the hike tomorrow," Farhan said.
"You must not leave the trail," the instructor warned.
"Can you help me carry this?" Yusuf asked Amina.
"I have never climbed a mountain this high," Khalid said.
"The guides will meet us at the citadel," the instructor announced.
Answer Key — Exercise 6A
Halima said (that) she loved reading adventure stories.
Farhan said (that) they would finish the hike the next day / tomorrow.
The instructor warned (them) not to leave the trail.
Yusuf asked Amina to help him carry it.
Khalid said (that) he had never climbed a mountain that high.
The instructor announced (that) the guides would meet them at the citadel.
Topic 7: Zero and First Conditionals
What This Rule Means

Conditionals are sentences that describe a condition and its result. The zero conditional is for things that are always true (facts, scientific truths, general rules). The first conditional is for things that are possible in the future.
Zero Conditional
Structure: If + present simple, present simple
Use: To talk about facts, general truths, and things that always happen.
"If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils."
"If it rains in the highlands, the trails become muddy."
"If you mix yellow and blue, you get green."
"If the sun sets, it gets dark."
You can also use "when" instead of "if" in zero conditionals, because the result always happens: "When you heat ice, it melts."
First Conditional
Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb
Use: To talk about real, possible future situations and their likely results.
"If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the hike."
"If you study hard, you will pass the exam."
"If they antagonize the guide, he will confiscate the supplies."
"If Khalid apologises, the instructor will forgive him."
You can also use might, can, or may instead of will to express different levels of certainty: "If I study hard, I might pass the test."
Common Mistakes
Using past tense in zero conditionals: Say "If you heat ice, it melts" (not "melted").
Using will in the "if" clause of a first conditional: Say "If it rains, I will stay" (not "If it will rain").
Confusing zero and first conditionals: Zero = always true. First = possible future.
Examples from the Story
Zero conditional: "If one fails, we all fail."
First conditional: "If we stop too often, we will not make it by nightfall."
First conditional: "If you do, you will break the rules."
First conditional: "If he antagonizes a guide, they might confiscate our radios."
Exercise 7A — Complete the Conditionals
Fill in the blanks with the correct form of the verb in brackets.
If you __________ (plant) trees, the soil __________ (hold) better. (Zero)
If she __________ (study) hard, she __________ (pass) the exam. (First)
If it __________ (snow), we __________ (build) a snowman. (First)
If you __________ (add) salt to water, the boiling point __________ (rise). (Zero)
If the group __________ (stay) together, they __________ (arrive) safely. (First)
If you __________ (touch) a hot pan, it __________ (burn) you. (Zero)
Answer Key — Exercise 7A
plant / holds
studies / will pass
snows / will build
add / rises
stays / will arrive
touch / burns
Topic 8: Phrasal and Prepositional Verbs
What This Rule Means

A phrasal verb is a verb combined with a preposition or particle that creates a new meaning different from the original verb. Phrasal verbs are extremely common in English, and understanding them is essential for natural communication.
Key Phrasal Verbs in This Unit
Separable vs. Inseparable Phrasal Verbs
Some phrasal verbs can be separated by an object (the noun goes between the verb and the particle). Others are inseparable (the verb and particle must stay together).
Separable: "She gave her fears in to." (Less natural) → "She gave in to her fears." (More natural and recommended)
Inseparable: "I need to get through this problem." (Cannot say: "I need to get this problem through.")
When in doubt, keep the phrasal verb together and put the object after it.
Exercise 8A — Choose the Correct Phrasal Verb
Complete each sentence with the correct phrasal verb: get through, back down from, stand up to, give in to.
Amina decided to __________ Khalid's reckless plan.
Do not __________ pressure from people who want you to take shortcuts.
If we work as a team, we can __________ any obstacle.
Khalid refused to __________ his position, even when he was clearly wrong.
It takes courage to __________ someone who is being unfair.
After hours of argument, he finally __________ the group's decision.
Answer Key — Exercise 8A
stand up to
give in to
get through
back down from
stand up to
gave in to
Comprehensive Review Exercise
This final exercise tests your understanding of all seven grammar topics. Complete each item, then check your answers at the end.
Write the comparative and superlative forms of: beautiful, good, bad, easy, far.
Rewrite in reported speech: "I will finish the project tomorrow," she said.
Complete the zero conditional: If you freeze water, it __________.
Complete the first conditional: If we protect the forests, wildlife __________.
Rewrite in the future passive voice: The council will build a new park.
Choose the correct quantifier: __________ (Few / A few) students volunteered, which was disappointing.
Use the correct comparative adverb: She completed the task __________ (efficient) than he did.
Fill in the phrasal verb: Do not __________ to pressure when making important decisions.
Answer Key — Comprehensive Review
more beautiful / most beautiful; better / best; worse / worst; easier / easiest; farther (further) / farthest (furthest)
She said (that) she would finish the project the next day / tomorrow.
turns into ice / freezes
will thrive / will survive / will be protected (any appropriate first conditional completion)
A new park will be built by the council.
Few (the negative sense matches 'disappointing')
more efficiently
give in
End of Grammar Reference
tables: Anyone can help with the project.
There is nothing in the bag.
A few students reached the summit.
Some of the trail was damaged.
Past continuous ("He said he was going")
Past perfect ("He said he had gone")
Had to ("He said he had to go")
To complete or survive something difficult
We will get through this challenge together.
To withdraw from a position or confrontation
She did not back down from her opinion.
To resist or confront someone bravely
It is brave to stand up to bullies.
To surrender or yield to pressure
Do not give in to fear.

paragraphs: Year 8 — Unit 6
Natural Wonders and Conservation
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 6: Natural Wonders and Conservation! In this unit, you will explore the extraordinary landscapes of East Africa and beyond, discovering how mountains, valleys, rivers, and wildlife shape the world around us. You will read an original adventure story called "The Citadel of Wind and Stone," set in the dramatic highlands of the Horn of Africa, where a group of young people must work together, make difficult decisions, and learn the meaning of true leadership. Through this story, you will encounter powerful vocabulary, practise important grammar structures, and develop your speaking and writing skills.

This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through each section at your own speed, practising reading, listening, speaking, and writing every day. Your AI Tutor is available around the clock to help you with anything you find challenging, and you will also have two teacher sessions per week to discuss your progress, ask questions, and get personalised feedback.

The theme of this unit is natural wonders and conservation. As you work through the lessons, you will think about why it matters to protect the environment, how human actions affect natural landscapes, and what role leadership and teamwork play in solving problems. You will learn to express comparisons clearly, talk about possibilities and consequences, report what others have said, and describe future events using the passive voice. By the end of this unit, you will be a more confident, expressive, and thoughtful communicator in English.

Throughout this unit, you will build on the grammar and vocabulary skills you developed in earlier units. The grammar focus is on comparatives and superlatives, but you will also work with comparative adverbs, pronouns and quantifiers, zero and first conditionals, reported speech and requests, the future passive voice, and phrasal and prepositional verbs. Each of these grammar areas will help you communicate with greater precision and variety.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use comparative and superlative forms of adjectives correctly, including irregular forms like better, worse, best, and worst.
Form and use comparative adverb structures to compare actions (for example, "She completed the task more quickly than he did").
Understand and use pronouns and quantifiers such as anyone, nothing, few, and all in your writing and speech.
Construct sentences using the future passive voice (for example, "The report will be completed by Friday").
Convert direct speech into reported speech and report requests accurately.
Recognise and use zero conditionals (facts and general truths) and first conditionals (real future possibilities).
Identify and use common phrasal and prepositional verbs such as get through, back down from, stand up to, and give in to.
Read and comprehend the story "The Citadel of Wind and Stone" and discuss its themes of leadership, teamwork, and conservation.
Use all 31 vocabulary words from this unit accurately in your own sentences, conversations, and written work.
Write structured paragraphs and short essays that incorporate comparatives, superlatives, conditionals, and reported speech.
Speak confidently about natural wonders, conservation, and personal experiences using the grammar and vocabulary from this unit.
Reflect on your own learning and set goals for continued improvement.
Vocabulary Preview

Before you begin reading the story, it is important to familiarise yourself with the key vocabulary for this unit. These words appear throughout the story and the grammar exercises. Knowing them in advance will make your reading smoother and your understanding deeper.

Below is a preview of some of the most important words. You will find the complete vocabulary list with definitions, parts of speech, and example sentences in the separate Vocabulary document. For now, look through these words and think about whether you have encountered any of them before.
Group A — Words About People and Behaviour
Group B — Words About Actions and Conflict
Group C — Words About Places and Nature
There are 31 vocabulary words in total for this unit. Make sure you study all of them using the Vocabulary document. Try to learn five or six words each day, and practise using them in sentences.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you quiz me on five vocabulary words from Unit 6?
Or: Can you give me a sentence with the word 'audacious' so I can see how it is used?
Or: What is the difference between 'deter' and 'desist'?
Grammar Introduction

In this unit, you will study seven grammar topics. The primary focus is on comparatives and superlatives, which allow you to compare people, places, things, and actions. You will also study several supporting grammar structures that will help you express yourself with greater range and accuracy. Here is a brief introduction to each topic. Full explanations, examples, and exercises are in the Grammar document.
1. Comparatives and Superlatives

Comparatives help you compare two things. You form them by adding -er to short adjectives (tall becomes taller) or by using more before longer adjectives (beautiful becomes more beautiful). Superlatives help you compare three or more things to show the highest degree. You form them by adding -est (tallest) or using most (most beautiful). For example: "The Simien highlands are higher than the coastal plains" and "Mount Everest is the tallest mountain in the world."
2. Irregular Comparative Forms

Some adjectives do not follow the regular pattern. The most important irregular forms are good / better / best and bad / worse / worst. You must memorise these because they do not take -er or -est. For example: "Her plan was better than his" and "That was the worst storm this year."
3. Comparative Adverb Structures

Adverbs describe how an action is done. To compare actions, you use more or less with adverbs of two or more syllables. For one-syllable adverbs, add -er. For example: "She climbed more carefully than he did" and "He runs faster than his brother." Irregular adverbs like well become better: "She speaks English better than before."
4. Pronouns and Quantifiers

Pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition. Quantifiers tell you how much or how many. Key words include anyone, nothing, few, a few, all, and some. Understanding the difference between few (negative, meaning not many) and a few (positive, meaning some) is especially important. For example: "Few students attempted the climb" versus "A few students reached the summit."
5. Future Passive Voice

The future passive voice is used to describe actions that will be done to something or someone. The structure is: Subject + will + be + past participle. For example: "The trail will be cleared by the rangers" and "New trees will be planted along the river." This is useful when you want to focus on the action rather than who performs it.
6. Reported Speech and Requests

Reported speech allows you to tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. You change the tense and pronouns. For example, direct speech: "I am going to the citadel." Reported speech: "She said she was going to the citadel." For requests, you use asked or told plus to and the base verb: "He asked them to stop."
7. Zero and First Conditionals

Zero conditionals express facts and general truths: If + present simple, present simple. For example: "If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils." First conditionals express real future possibilities: If + present simple, will + base verb. For example: "If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the hike." These are essential for talking about consequences and possibilities.
8. Phrasal and Prepositional Verbs

Phrasal verbs combine a verb with a preposition or particle to create a new meaning. The four key phrasal verbs in this unit are: get through (to complete or survive something), back down from (to withdraw from a position), stand up to (to resist or confront), and give in to (to surrender or yield). Learning these will help you understand natural, everyday English.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you explain the difference between zero and first conditionals with three examples each?
Or: Can you help me practise changing direct speech to reported speech?
Or: What are some common irregular comparatives I should memorise?
Reading and Listening

The centrepiece of this unit is the story "The Citadel of Wind and Stone." This is an original adventure story set in the highlands of the Horn of Africa, where a group of young students take part in a challenging leadership programme. The story explores themes of leadership, teamwork, resilience, conservation, and ethical decision-making.
Before You Read
Before you open the Story document, take a few minutes to think about the following questions. You can write brief answers in your notebook or discuss them with your AI Tutor:
Have you ever been on a long walk or hike? What was it like?
What do you think makes a good leader? Is it being the fastest or the strongest, or is it something else?
What does the word "citadel" mean? Why do you think the story is called "The Citadel of Wind and Stone"?
What are some natural wonders you know about in East Africa or the wider world?
Why is it important to protect natural environments like mountains, forests, and rivers?
Reading the Story
Now open the Story document and read "The Citadel of Wind and Stone" carefully. As you read, pay attention to the following:
Characters: Who are the main characters? What are their personalities like? How do they change?
Setting: Where does the story take place? How does the author describe the landscape?
Vocabulary: Which vocabulary words from this unit appear in the story? Underline or highlight them.
Grammar in context: Can you spot examples of comparatives, superlatives, conditionals, reported speech, or passive voice?
Moral and theme: What lesson does the story teach about leadership and working together?

Read the story at least twice. The first time, read for general understanding. The second time, read more slowly, looking for vocabulary and grammar structures. If you encounter words you do not know, look them up in the Vocabulary document or ask your AI Tutor.
After You Read
After reading the story, answer these comprehension questions in your notebook. Write in complete sentences.
Where is the story set, and what is the students' destination?
Describe Khalid's personality at the beginning of the story. What kind of person is he?
Why does Amina decide not to follow Khalid into the ravine?
What happens to Khalid when he takes the shortcut?
How does Amina show leadership during the hike?
What does Khalid learn by the end of the story?
Find a sentence in the story that uses a comparative form. Write it down and identify the comparative.
Find a sentence that uses a first conditional (if... will). Write it down.
What is the moral of the story? Do you agree with it? Why or why not?
How does this story connect to the theme of conservation and respecting nature?
Listening Practice
To practise your listening skills, try the following activities:

Read aloud: Read one section of the story aloud to yourself or a family member. Focus on pronunciation, intonation, and expression. Pay special attention to words like "grueling," "conspicuous," "audacious," and "brusque."
AI Tutor dictation: Ask your AI Tutor to give you a dictation exercise using sentences from the story. Listen carefully and write down what you hear, then check your spelling and grammar.
Retell the story: Without looking at the text, try to retell the story in your own words. Start with: "The story is about a group of young students who..." This helps you practise fluency and recall.

Dialogue practice: Choose a dialogue from the story (for example, the conversation between Amina and Khalid about the shortcut) and practise reading it with a partner, family member, or your AI Tutor. Try to match the characters' emotions.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you give me a dictation exercise using five sentences from the Unit 6 story?
Or: Can you ask me comprehension questions about The Citadel of Wind and Stone?
Or: Can you help me retell the story in my own words and correct my grammar?
Speaking

Speaking is one of the most important skills you will develop this year. In this section, you will practise using the vocabulary and grammar from Unit 6 in spoken English. Try to speak out loud, even if you are studying alone. The more you practise speaking, the more natural it will feel.
Speaking Activity 1 — Comparing Natural Wonders

Choose two natural wonders that you know about (for example, the Simien Mountains and the Great Rift Valley, or the Nile River and the Indian Ocean). Prepare a short speech of one to two minutes in which you compare them using comparatives and superlatives. Try to include at least five comparison sentences. Here are some ideas to get you started:
"The Simien Mountains are higher than the hills near my town."
"The Nile is the longest river in Africa."
"The Indian Ocean is more vast than any lake I have ever seen."
"The weather on the highlands is colder than on the coast."
"Of all the natural wonders I have learned about, the Great Rift Valley is the most fascinating."
Speaking Activity 2 — Role Play: The Leadership Debate

Imagine you are one of the characters in the story. With a partner, family member, or your AI Tutor, act out a debate about whether Khalid's decision to take the shortcut was right or wrong. One person argues in favour of taking risks, and the other argues in favour of caution and teamwork. Use conditionals in your arguments:
"If Khalid had waited, he would not have been injured."
"If we always play it safe, we will never discover anything new."
"If you split from the group, you put everyone at risk."
Speaking Activity 3 — Reporting What Others Said
Practise reported speech by telling a partner or your AI Tutor what the characters in the story said. For example:
Khalid said that he could do the hike in half the time.
Amina told the group that they were a team.
The instructor said that Khalid had been warned not to break formation.
Halima said that she had learned that fear can be wise.
After practising with story examples, try reporting real things that people around you have said today. For example: "My mother told me that dinner would be ready at seven."
Speaking Activity 4 — Conservation Discussion
Prepare a two-minute talk on one of the following topics. Use vocabulary from this unit and try to include at least two conditionals and two comparatives:
Why is it important to protect natural environments like mountains and forests?
What are the biggest threats to wildlife in East Africa?
What can young people do to help with conservation efforts?
Describe a natural place you have visited or would like to visit. What makes it special?
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that caution is better than risk-taking.
Or: Can you listen to my two-minute talk about conservation and give me feedback?
Or: Can you help me practise reported speech by having a conversation I can then report?
Writing

Strong writing requires planning, drafting, and revising. In this section, you will practise several types of writing, all connected to the themes and grammar of Unit 6. Remember to write in complete sentences, use paragraphs, and include the grammar structures you have learned.
Writing Task 1 — Comparative Paragraph

Write a paragraph of 80 to 120 words comparing two places you know. These could be two cities, two schools, two parks, or two natural features. Use at least five comparative or superlative forms. Here is an example opening:

"My grandmother's village is smaller than the city where I live, but it is also more peaceful. The air there is cleaner, and the nights are quieter. Of all the places I have visited, it is the most beautiful."

After writing your paragraph, underline every comparative and superlative form you used. Check that you have used at least five. Then ask your AI Tutor to review your paragraph and suggest improvements.
Writing Task 2 — Conditional Sentences

Write eight conditional sentences — four using the zero conditional and four using the first conditional. Your sentences should relate to the themes of nature, conservation, or leadership. For example:
Zero: "If you cut down too many trees, the soil erodes."
First: "If we plant more trees this year, the forest will grow back."
After writing your sentences, label each one as "Zero" or "First." Check that you have used the correct verb tenses in each clause.
Writing Task 3 — Reported Speech Transformation
Below are five direct speech sentences. Rewrite each one in reported speech. Pay careful attention to tense changes and pronoun changes.
Khalid said: "I am the fastest climber in the group."
Amina said: "We need to stay together."
The instructor said: "You must not leave the trail."
Halima asked: "What will happen if someone gets lost?"
Farhan said: "I have never seen mountains this high before."
Write your answers in your notebook, then check them against the answer key in the Grammar document or ask your AI Tutor to verify them.
Writing Task 4 — Short Essay: Conservation Matters
Write a short essay of 150 to 200 words on the following topic:
"Why is it important to conserve natural environments, and what can young people do to help?"
Your essay should have three paragraphs:
Introduction: State your main argument. Why does conservation matter?
Body: Give at least two specific reasons or examples. Use comparatives, conditionals, and vocabulary from this unit.
Conclusion: Summarise your argument and suggest one action young people can take.
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit in your essay. After writing, read your essay aloud to check that it flows well. Then share it with your AI Tutor for feedback.
Writing Task 5 — Future Passive Voice Paragraph

Imagine that your school is planning an environmental conservation day. Write a paragraph of 60 to 80 words describing what will happen, using the future passive voice for at least four sentences. For example:

"Trees will be planted in the school garden. Rubbish will be collected from the nearby river. A presentation about endangered animals will be given by a local expert. Posters will be displayed in every classroom."
After writing, underline every future passive construction and make sure each one follows the pattern: subject + will + be + past participle.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you check my comparative paragraph and tell me if I used at least five comparatives correctly?
Or: Can you help me rewrite direct speech as reported speech? I want to practise.
Or: Can you give me feedback on my conservation essay and suggest ways to improve it?
Activities and Exercises

This section contains a range of exercises to help you consolidate what you have learned. Work through them carefully, checking your answers as you go. If you get stuck, revisit the Grammar and Vocabulary documents, or ask your AI Tutor for help.
Exercise 1 — Comparative and Superlative Forms
Complete each sentence with the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
The highland trail is __________ (difficult) than the coastal path.
Khalid is the __________ (fast) runner in the group.
This route is __________ (safe) than the one through the ravine.
Amina is __________ (cautious) than Khalid when making decisions.
Of all the students, Halima was the __________ (young).
The citadel is the __________ (old) building in the region.
Today's climb was __________ (grueling) than yesterday's.
The view from the summit is the __________ (beautiful) I have ever seen.
Answers: 1. more difficult 2. fastest 3. safer 4. more cautious 5. youngest 6. oldest 7. more grueling 8. most beautiful
Exercise 2 — Irregular Comparatives
Fill in each blank with the correct irregular comparative or superlative form.
Amina's plan was __________ (good) than Khalid's shortcut idea.
The weather today is __________ (bad) than it was yesterday.
Of all the options, the long route was the __________ (good).
Khalid's injury was the __________ (bad) thing that could have happened.
She speaks English __________ (well) than anyone else in the class.
The second day of hiking was __________ (far) than the first.
Answers: 1. better 2. worse 3. best 4. worst 5. better 6. farther / further
Exercise 3 — Zero and First Conditionals
Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets. Decide whether each sentence is a zero or first conditional.
If you __________ (heat) ice, it __________ (melt).
If they __________ (split) from the group, they __________ (face) consequences.
If it __________ (rain) tomorrow, the trail __________ (be) too slippery to climb.
If you __________ (mix) red and blue paint, you __________ (get) purple.
If Khalid __________ (apologise), the instructor __________ (forgive) him.
If water __________ (reach) 100 degrees Celsius, it __________ (boil).
Answers: 1. heat / melts (zero) 2. split / will face (first) 3. rains / will be (first) 4. mix / get (zero) 5. apologises / will forgive (first) 6. reaches / boils (zero)
Exercise 4 — Reported Speech
Rewrite each direct speech sentence as reported speech.
"I am going to the citadel," Khalid said.
"We must stay together," Amina told the group.
"I have never seen such beautiful mountains," Farhan said.
"Will the guides help us?" Halima asked.
"Do not take the shortcut," the instructor warned Khalid.

Answers: 1. Khalid said he was going to the citadel. 2. Amina told the group they must / had to stay together. 3. Farhan said he had never seen such beautiful mountains. 4. Halima asked whether the guides would help them. 5. The instructor warned Khalid not to take the shortcut.
Exercise 5 — Future Passive Voice
Rewrite each sentence in the future passive voice.
The rangers will clear the trail. → The trail __________.
The volunteers will plant new trees. → New trees __________.
The school will organise a conservation day. → A conservation day __________.
The instructor will announce the results. → The results __________.
The team will repair the bridge. → The bridge __________.
Answers: 1. will be cleared by the rangers 2. will be planted by the volunteers 3. will be organised by the school 4. will be announced by the instructor 5. will be repaired by the team
Exercise 6 — Phrasal Verbs
Choose the correct phrasal verb to complete each sentence: get through, back down from, stand up to, give in to.
You must __________ injustice whenever you see it.
Do not __________ pressure from people who want you to break the rules.
If we work together, we can __________ any challenge.
Khalid decided not to __________ his position, even though he was wrong.
Answers: 1. stand up to 2. give in to 3. get through 4. back down from
Exercise 7 — Vocabulary in Context
Choose the correct vocabulary word to complete each sentence.
The old tree had __________ branches that twisted in every direction. (gnarled / ardent)
The bright red flag was __________ against the grey sky. (furtive / conspicuous)
After hours of climbing, the group found a __________ in the shade of a rock. (respite / transgression)
She was __________ at solving difficult puzzles. (proficient / brusque)
The marathon was absolutely __________; every runner was exhausted. (grueling / furtive)
His __________ reply made her feel unwelcome. (ardent / brusque)
The student's __________ behaviour broke the school rules. (transgression / headway)
We are making good __________ on our conservation project. (headway / anarchy)
Answers: 1. gnarled 2. conspicuous 3. respite 4. proficient 5. grueling 6. brusque 7. transgression 8. headway
Exercise 8 — Sentence Building
Write your own sentences using the structures given. Each sentence should relate to nature, conservation, or the story.
Write a sentence using a comparative adjective: ___________________
Write a sentence using a superlative adjective: ___________________
Write a sentence using a zero conditional: ___________________
Write a sentence using a first conditional: ___________________
Write a sentence using the future passive voice: ___________________
Write a sentence using the phrasal verb stand up to: ___________________
Write a sentence using the word crucial: ___________________
Write a sentence using reported speech: ___________________
Check your sentences with your AI Tutor or bring them to your next teacher session for feedback.

anarchy          d) showing strong feelings or enthusiasm

Self-Study Tips

Successful language learning depends on consistent daily practice. Here are some strategies to help you get the most out of this unit and continue building your English skills between teacher sessions.
Tip 1 — Use a Vocabulary Notebook

Keep a dedicated notebook for new vocabulary. For each word, write the word, its part of speech, the definition, and an example sentence that you create yourself. Review your notebook every evening before bed. Research shows that reviewing words just before sleep helps your brain store them in long-term memory.
Tip 2 — Speak English Every Day

Even if you are studying alone, make it a habit to speak out loud in English for at least ten minutes each day. You can describe what you see around you, retell the story from this unit, or practise the speaking activities listed above. The more you hear yourself speak, the more confident you will become. Use your AI Tutor as a conversation partner when no one else is available.
Tip 3 — Read Beyond the Lesson

Try reading short English texts outside of your coursework. You can read simple news articles, short stories, or even the English labels on everyday products. Reading widely exposes you to grammar and vocabulary in natural contexts, which helps your brain absorb patterns more quickly.
Tip 4 — Practise Grammar in Writing

After studying each grammar topic, write at least five sentences using that structure in your notebook. Do not just read the rules — apply them. Writing forces you to think actively about grammar, which strengthens your understanding far more than passive reading.
Tip 5 — Review Mistakes

When your teacher or AI Tutor corrects your work, do not simply read the correction and move on. Copy the corrected version into your notebook and write a brief note explaining what you got wrong and why. This practice turns mistakes into valuable learning opportunities.
Tip 6 — Set Small, Clear Goals

At the start of each study session, set a small goal for yourself. For example: "Today I will learn five new vocabulary words" or "Today I will write a paragraph using three conditionals." Small goals keep you focused and give you a sense of achievement when you complete them.
Tip 7 — Use English in Your Daily Life

Try to incorporate English into your everyday activities. Think in English when you are walking to school. Describe your meals in English. When you see something interesting, try to describe it using a comparative or superlative. The more you use English in real situations, the more natural it will become.

paragraphs: The Citadel of Wind and Stone
Year 8 — Unit 6 Story
Theme: Conflict, decision-making, and resilience under pressure
Setting: The Simien Highlands, northern Ethiopia — Horn of Africa
Genre: Adventure drama with psychological and ethical tension
Part One — The Ascent

The mountain trail twisted like a coiled rope through the Simien Highlands, dust rising in swirls around the students' boots. Their destination lay far above — a stone fortress locals called "the Citadel of Wind and Stone." It had once protected an ancient kingdom, and now it served as the final challenge in a national youth leadership programme.

Amina adjusted the straps on her pack and glanced behind. The trail team was still together, though some were slowing. The air thinned as they climbed, and the path grew rougher. To reach the citadel was no small task — it was grueling, designed to test not just physical endurance, but judgment.
"We need a rest soon," said Farhan, panting. "The sun is hitting harder now."
"If we stop too often," Amina replied, "we will not make it by nightfall."
Khalid, who had taken the lead early, scoffed. "Some of you are too soft. I could do this in half the time."

His tone was always brusque, bordering on arrogant. He had already clashed with others several times, but he was undeniably proficient — fast, clever, and athletic. Yet Amina watched him with caution. His actions were often too bold, too fast, too audacious.
Still, the group moved on, following a gnarled ridge where the wind began to howl. Eddies of dust and air spun off the edges, making the cliffs feel alive.
Part Two — The Argument
They reached a small respite beneath a rock outcrop by midday. The group huddled in the shade, eating dried dates and roasted chickpeas.
"This whole hike feels like a punishment," muttered Halima, one of the younger students. "What if someone gets hurt?"
"If they do," Khalid said, "they should not have come. No one forced them."
Amina frowned. "We are a team. If one fails, we all fail. You were told that."
Khalid stood abruptly. "Then you had better keep up."
As he strode ahead, others murmured.
"He will get us into trouble," Yusuf said. "The guides warned us. If we split, we are responsible for what happens."
"If he antagonizes a guide," another added, "they might confiscate our radios."
"We cannot afford that," Amina said. "Let us catch up — but carefully."
Part Three — The Shortcut
An hour later, they reached a sharp bend where the path narrowed along a steep drop. Khalid stood at the edge, arms crossed.
"What are you doing?" Amina called.
"There is a shortcut through that ravine. Less distance."
"That was not on the map," she warned.
"It will get us there faster."
"It is dangerous."
He stepped forward. "I am going."
"If you do," she said firmly, "you will break the rules. That is a transgression we agreed to avoid."
Khalid hesitated. But pride surged in his chest. "I will meet you there."
Before anyone could stop him, he leapt down the slope and disappeared into the ravine's shadow.
Halima quavered, her voice shaking. "What if he falls?"
"If he does," Amina said, her voice steady, "we go after him. But not now. He made a choice."
They took the long route — slower but safer.
Part Four — The Citadel

By late afternoon, the citadel came into view. It stood high atop a ridge, stone walls wrapped around jagged rock like a crown. The final climb was steep and exposed. Wind pushed against them, deterring even the strongest from rushing.
"Almost there," Farhan gasped.
The path forked. One route went around a sheer cliff — longer, but protected. The other crossed a narrow bridge of rock — fast but perilous.
Amina halted. "Two options."
"The short one looks... outlandish," Yusuf said.
"Khalid would take it," someone muttered.
Amina did not respond. She studied the terrain, noting conspicuous signs of erosion near the narrow bridge. Stones were loose. A crack split the path near the end.
"We take the long path," she decided.
"No argument here," said Farhan.
They moved cautiously. Wind tore at them, but the trail held. They reached the citadel gates as the sun dipped behind the mountains. An instructor greeted them.
"You made it — intact," he said. "Any sign of Khalid?"
"No," Amina replied.
The instructor's face grew dark. "He was warned not to break formation."
"He took a shortcut through the ravine. He said he would meet us here."
The instructor radioed the outposts. No one had seen him. Concern flickered.
Part Five — The Wait
Night fell. The stone walls echoed with silence. Amina could not sleep. Her mind spun with questions.
What if Khalid was hurt? What if they had let him go too easily?
Just before dawn, a call came through: "Found him. Minor injury. He is being escorted back."
Relief flooded the group.
By sunrise, Khalid limped into the courtyard, clothes torn and covered in dust.
"I tripped near a crevice," he admitted. "It took hours to climb out."
"You are lucky," the instructor said. "A few more steps, and the path would have crumbled."
"I was trying to show I could lead," Khalid murmured. "But I failed."
"You were reckless," Amina said, not unkindly. "Leadership is not proving you are the fastest. It is knowing when to wait."
He looked at her, shame in his eyes.
Part Six — Reflections
That afternoon, the group was debriefed. Each student had to present a reflection.
Halima stood first. "I learned that fear can be wise. That sometimes the most crucial choice is to wait."
Farhan followed. "I thought I was strong until I realised strength comes from others."
Amina stepped forward. "I have always been cautious. But I learned to trust instinct and facts. I did not want to command — I wanted us all to arrive."
Then Khalid stood.
"I was wrong. I mistook daring for wisdom. I thought rules were limits. But now I see — they are tools."
Applause filled the citadel's stone walls.
Part Seven — The Descent
As they descended the mountain the next day, wind trailing behind them, the group stayed close. No shortcuts. No prideful sprints. They stopped to help each other. They moved like a single thought.
At the base, a village child offered them water. Amina handed over her bottle in return. The child smiled.
"You look tired," she said.
"Not tired," Amina answered. "Just finished something important."
If Khalid had reached the citadel first, he might have claimed victory — but learned nothing.
If Amina had given in to pride, the group might have fractured.
But they did not. And that made all the difference.
Vocabulary and Grammar Coverage
Vocabulary Used in the Story

atrocity, destination, desist, deter, authority, anarchy, furtive, antagonize, kindle, audacious, quaver, deplore, transgression, astute, gnarled, resent, outlandish, culminate, respite, conspicuous, claim, proficient, exploit, brusque, confiscate, eddy, citadel, headway, crucial, grueling, ardent
Grammar Structures Used
Comparatives and superlatives (faster, the strongest, more cautious)
Irregular comparative forms (better, worse, best, worst)
Comparative adverbs (more carefully, more quickly)
Pronouns and quantifiers (some, anyone, few)
Future passive voice (He is being escorted back)
Reported speech (He said he would meet us here)
Zero and first conditionals (If we stop too often, we will not make it; If one fails, we all fail)
Phrasal and prepositional verbs (give in to, stand up to, back down from, get through)
End of Story
Year 8 — Unit 6 Vocabulary
Natural Wonders and Conservation

This document contains the complete vocabulary list for Unit 6, along with practice exercises to help you learn and remember each word. There are 31 words in total. Study them carefully, practise using them in sentences, and review them regularly.
Complete Vocabulary List
Vocabulary Practice Exercises
