End of Vocabulary Reference
tables: A very cruel or terrible act
The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
A place where someone is going or something is being sent
Our destination for the field trip is the national park.
The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.
To make someone decide not to do something
The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.
The power or right to give orders and make decisions
The park ranger has the authority to close the trail if conditions are unsafe.
A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion
After the leader resigned, the organisation fell into anarchy.
Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed
He gave a furtive glance at the map before anyone noticed.
To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words
If you keep interrupting, you will only antagonize the speaker.
To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
Reading about the Simien Mountains kindled her interest in conservation.
Bold and daring; willing to take risks
The audacious explorer crossed the ravine without any safety equipment.
To shake or tremble while speaking, often from nervousness
Her voice began to quaver when she spoke about the dangerous climb.
To feel or express strong disapproval of something
Environmentalists deplore the destruction of ancient forests.
A wrong action that breaks a rule or law
Breaking formation during the hike was a serious transgression.
Able to understand things quickly and make good decisions
The astute leader noticed the signs of danger before anyone else.
Twisted and rough, usually describing old trees or branches
The gnarled branches of the ancient tree looked like outstretched arms.
To feel angry or upset because something seems unfair
He began to resent Khalid for slowing down the group with his reckless choices.
Strange or unusual in a surprising or hard-to-believe way
His outlandish plan to climb the cliff at night worried the whole team.
To reach the highest point or final stage of something
The leadership programme will culminate with a ceremony at the citadel.
A short break or rest from something difficult
After hours of climbing, they found a respite beneath a shady rock.
Easy to see or notice; very obvious
The bright red flag was conspicuous against the grey mountain rock.
To say that something is true, often without proof
Khalid claimed he could reach the citadel faster than anyone else.
Skilled and able to do something well
She is proficient at reading maps and navigating difficult terrain.
To use something in a way that helps you but may be unfair
Some people exploit natural resources without thinking about the future.
Speaking in a very direct and rude way, without friendliness
Khalid's brusque reply made the younger students uncomfortable.
To take something away, usually because it is not allowed
The instructor had to confiscate the radio after the rules were broken.
A small, circular movement of water or air against the main flow
The leaves floated in an eddy in the river, spinning in circles.
A strong fortress that protects a city or area
The ancient citadel stood tall on the hill, guarding the valley below.
Progress or forward movement towards a goal
We are making good headway on the conservation project.
Very important or necessary for something to succeed
Teamwork is crucial when facing difficult challenges in nature.
The grueling climb tested the limits of every student in the group.
Showing strong feelings or enthusiasm for something
She is an ardent supporter of wildlife conservation.

paragraphs: Year 8 -- Unit 7 Grammar Reference
Past Simple vs Past Continuous, Past Perfect, Reported Speech, and More
1. Past Simple vs Past Continuous
What These Tenses Mean and Why They Matter

The Past Simple and the Past Continuous are two of the most important tenses for telling stories about things that happened before. The Past Simple tells you that something happened and is finished. The Past Continuous shows you that an action was happening over a period of time in the past. Understanding how to use these two tenses -- both separately and together -- is essential for narrating events clearly and creating vivid descriptions of past scenes.
How to Form Them
Past Simple -- Regular Verbs: Add -ed to the base form of the verb.
play --> played
walk --> walked
finish --> finished
Past Simple -- Irregular Verbs: These have special forms that you need to memorise.
go --> went
write --> wrote
find --> found
have --> had
see --> saw
Past Continuous: Use was (I/he/she/it) or were (you/we/they) + the verb with -ing.
I was writing
They were playing
She was reading
When to Use Each Tense
Use the Past Simple when you want to describe a completed action in the past:
"She led a protest." (The protest happened and finished.)
"He found the notebook." (The finding happened at a specific moment.)
Use the Past Continuous when you want to describe an action that was in progress at a specific time:
"She was leading a protest at noon." (The leading was ongoing at that time.)
"They were studying when the bell rang." (The studying was ongoing when the bell interrupted.)
Combining the Two Tenses
When you combine these tenses in one sentence, you show that one action was happening (Past Continuous) when another action interrupted it or happened during it (Past Simple).
"Amari was writing an essay when the prefect found his notebook."
"While the students were playing football, Amari slipped behind the assembly hall."
"She was reading the essay aloud when she realised its importance."
You can also use the Past Continuous twice to show two actions happening at the same time:
"While she was leading the discussion, others were bartering ideas."
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong verb form.
Wrong: "She lead a protest."
Correct: "She led a protest." (Led is the past form of lead.)
Mistake 2: Confusing which tense to use.
Wrong: "While she led a protest, others barter supplies."
Correct: "While she was leading a protest, others were bartering supplies."
Memory Tip

Think about timing. If an action is completed and done, use the Past Simple (like finishing your homework). If an action was happening over time in the background, use the Past Continuous (like painting a picture while music was playing).
Practice Exercise
Complete each sentence using either the Past Simple or Past Continuous form of the verb in brackets.
She __________ (lead) a protest while others __________ (barter) supplies.
They __________ (play) outside when it __________ (start) to rain.
I __________ (watch) a movie when my sister __________ (call) me.
While the headmaster __________ (speak), the students __________ (whisper) to each other.
Amari __________ (write) in his notebook when the candle __________ (go) out.
Answers:
led; were bartering
were playing; started
was watching; called
was speaking; were whispering
was writing; went
2. Past Perfect
What the Past Perfect Means and Why It Matters

The Past Perfect tense is used to talk about an action that was completed before another action in the past. It helps you show the order of events clearly. When you use the Past Perfect, your reader or listener can immediately understand which action happened first.
How to Form It
The Past Perfect is formed with had + the past participle of the main verb.
Regular verbs: had + -ed form (had played, had walked, had finished)
Irregular verbs: had + special past participle (had gone, had written, had seen, had eaten)
Structure: Subject + had + past participle + rest of sentence
When to Use It
Use the Past Perfect when you want to make it clear that one past action happened before another past action.
"He had concluded the speech before the defect was revealed."
"She had finished her homework before dinner started."
"They had left the party when the rain began."
"By the time the headmaster arrived, Amari had already hidden his notebook."
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake: Using the Past Simple instead of the Past Perfect when talking about the earlier action.
Wrong: "He concluded the speech before the defect was revealed." (This implies both happened at roughly the same time.)
Correct: "He had concluded the speech before the defect was revealed." (This clearly shows the speech was finished first.)
Memory Tip
Think of the Past Perfect as a time traveller. It goes further back in time to show what happened first. When you see "had," it is helping you tell the story in the right order.
Practice Exercise
Complete each sentence using the Past Perfect form of the verb in brackets.
By the time she arrived, I __________ (finish) my book.
They __________ (see) the movie before it was released in cinemas.
He __________ (eat) all the biscuits before his friends came over.
By the time the bell rang, the students __________ (complete) the test.
She __________ (leave) the room before the announcement was made.
Answers:
had finished
had seen
had eaten
had completed
had left
3. Reported Speech
What Reported Speech Is and Why It Matters

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is when you tell someone what another person said without using their exact words. Instead of quoting them directly, you adjust the pronouns, verb tenses, and sometimes the time expressions. Reported speech is essential for sharing information, retelling conversations, and writing about what people have said.
How to Convert Direct Speech to Reported Speech
Follow these steps:
Identify the speaker and the original statement. Example: She said, "The response is emphatic."
Change the pronouns to match who is being talked about. "I" might become "he" or "she."
Shift the verb tense back one step. Present becomes past; past becomes past perfect.
Add "that" (optional) to connect the reporting verb to the reported statement.
Put it all together. Example: She said that the response was emphatic.
Tense Shift Table
Reporting Questions and Commands
Questions: Use "asked" + if/whether (for yes/no questions) or the question word (for wh- questions). Do not use a question mark in reported speech.
Direct: He asked, "Are you coming?"
Reported: He asked if I was coming.
Direct: She asked, "Where are you going?"
Reported: She asked where I was going.
Commands: Use "told" + person + to + base verb.
Direct: He said, "Justify your actions."
Reported: He told them to justify their actions.
Direct: She said, "Please close the door."
Reported: She asked me to close the door.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Forgetting to shift the verb tense.
Wrong: She said the response is emphatic.
Correct: She said that the response was emphatic.
Mistake 2: Not changing questions properly.
Wrong: He asked are you happy.
Correct: He asked if I was happy.
Memory Tip
Think of reported speech as a time traveller: when you report what someone said, you travel back in time, so the verb tense shifts one step into the past.
Practice Exercise
Change each sentence from direct speech to reported speech.
She said, "The game is fun."
They asked, "Where are you going?"
He told her, "Finish your homework."
Amari said, "I believe in questions."
Dalton asked, "Did you write this?"
Answers:
She said that the game was fun.
They asked where I was going.
He told her to finish her homework.
Amari said that he believed in questions.
Dalton asked whether he had written that.
4. Verbs Followed by Prepositions
What This Rule Means and Why It Matters

In English, some verbs must be followed by a specific preposition to make the sentence correct and natural. Using the wrong preposition -- or leaving it out entirely -- can change the meaning or make the sentence sound unnatural. Learning which prepositions go with which verbs is an important part of building fluent English.
Key Verb + Preposition Combinations
Other Common Verb + Preposition Pairs
look at = to see something / look for = to search for something
depend on = to rely on something or someone
listen to = to pay attention to sounds or someone speaking
agree with = to have the same opinion as someone
belong to = to be owned by someone
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Leaving out the preposition.
Wrong: "I insist going to the party."
Correct: "I insist on going to the party."
Mistake 2: Using the wrong preposition.
Wrong: "I believe on fairies."
Correct: "I believe in fairies."
Memory Tip
Create a rhyme to remember the pairs: "Insist on a plan, believe in the magic, waver between choices, implore for help when it's tragic!"
Practice Exercise
Fill in each blank with the correct preposition.
I always insist __________ going to school on time.
Do you believe __________ the importance of reading?
She keeps wavering __________ two colours for her project.
He implored __________ his parents to let him stay up late.
Answers:
on
in
between
for (Note: implored is past tense here)
5. Phrasal Verbs and Idioms
What Phrasal Verbs Are and Why They Matter

Phrasal verbs are verbs that combine with a preposition or adverb to create a new meaning that is different from the meaning of the original verb alone. They are extremely common in everyday English and appear frequently in stories, conversations, and informal writing. Learning phrasal verbs will make your English sound much more natural.
Key Phrasal Verbs for This Unit
How Phrasal Verbs Work

A phrasal verb has two (or sometimes three) parts: the base verb + a particle (a preposition or adverb). The particle changes the meaning of the verb. For example, "get" alone means to receive or obtain, but "get on with" means to continue doing something.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong particle.
Wrong: "get on to" instead of "get on with"
Correct: "Let's get on with our work."
Mistake 2: Translating literally from your first language.
Phrasal verbs often cannot be translated word for word. Focus on learning the meaning of the whole phrase, not each word separately.
Memory Tip

Create a short story or mental picture for each phrasal verb. For example, imagine a friend trying to "get on with" their homework while their cat keeps jumping on the desk. This visual connection helps you remember the meaning.
Practice Exercise
Choose the correct phrasal verb to complete each sentence: get on with, get into trouble, ebb away, roil over.
If you don't listen to the teacher, you might __________.
After a while, the excitement of the game started to __________.
It's important to __________ your work, even if it gets difficult.
When I heard the unfair news, my feelings began to __________.
Answers:
get into trouble
ebb away
get on with
roil over
6. Time Phrases
What Time Phrases Are and Why They Matter

Time phrases are expressions that help your reader or listener understand when something happened. They are essential for storytelling because they signal whether an action is in the past, present, or future, and they help create a clear timeline of events.
Key Time Phrases for This Unit
Where to Place Time Phrases
Time phrases can go at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence.
Beginning: "In those days, I played outside every day."
End: "I did not understand it at the time."
Middle: "She had, by 1948, become a confident speaker."
Placing the time phrase at the beginning is the most common position and often sounds the most natural in storytelling.
Memory Tip -- T.I.M.E.
Use the acronym T.I.M.E. to remember the steps:
T -- Think about the action (what happened?)
I -- Identify the time phrase (when did it happen?)
M -- Make your sentence (put them together)
E -- Ensure it makes sense (does the time phrase match the action?)
Practice Exercise
Fill in each blank with the correct time phrase: In those days, Eventually, At the time, By 1948.
__________, children used to play outside until dark.
I wanted to learn to ride a bike. __________, I finally did it!
__________, I did not know how to cook.
Many people had jobs __________, and life was different.
Answers:
In those days
Eventually
At the time
7. Reflexive Pronouns
What Reflexive Pronouns Are and Why They Matter

Reflexive pronouns are special words you use when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same person or group. They add a personal touch to your writing and help you express actions that someone does to or for themselves.
The Reflexive Pronoun Forms
When to Use Reflexive Pronouns
Use a reflexive pronoun when the subject does something to itself:
"She looked at herself in the mirror."
"The children played by themselves in the park."
"He completed the project all by himself."
"I taught myself how to play the guitar."
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Using a reflexive pronoun when the subject and object are different.
Wrong: "She gave it to herself." (if "she" is giving it to another person)
Correct: "She gave it to her."
Mistake 2: Forgetting the reflexive pronoun when it is needed.
Incomplete: "I made a cake."
With reflexive emphasis: "I made a cake myself." (This emphasises that nobody helped.)
Memory Tip

Think of reflexive pronouns as "self-action" words. If you are doing something to yourself or by yourself, you need a reflexive pronoun. "I wash myself," but "I wash my dog" (the dog is not "myself").
Practice Exercise
Fill in each blank with the correct reflexive pronoun.
Maryam baked a cake by __________.
The cats are grooming __________ in the sun.
I can do this project all by __________.
Yusuf showed __________ how to ride a bike.
We can solve this puzzle by __________.
Answers:
herself
themselves
myself
himself
ourselves
8. Discourse Markers
What Discourse Markers Are and Why They Matter

Discourse markers are words or phrases that connect your ideas when you are speaking or writing. They act like signposts on a road -- they guide your reader or listener and help them follow your thoughts smoothly. Using discourse markers makes your communication clearer and more engaging.
Key Discourse Markers for This Unit
Where to Place Discourse Markers
Most discourse markers go at the beginning of a sentence, followed by a comma.
"Well, I think we should go."
"Actually, I prefer the blue one."
"In fact, the test was easier than I expected."
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Forgetting the comma after the discourse marker.
Wrong: "Well I think we should go."
Correct: "Well, I think we should go."
Mistake 2: Overusing discourse markers.
Use them when they genuinely help clarify your ideas. Do not force one into every sentence.
Memory Tip
Think of discourse markers as signposts on the road of your conversation. They tell your listener: "Here is an example," "Here is something surprising," or "Here is a simpler way to say it."
Practice Exercise
Choose the correct discourse marker to complete each sentence: Actually, In fact, Basically, Well, For example.
__________, I really like playing football after school.
I enjoy many sports. __________, I love basketball and tennis.
__________, we can have a picnic this weekend!
__________, I think we should go to the library first.
You can improve your English in many ways. __________, try reading a book every week.
Answers:
Actually
In fact
Basically
Well
For example
Grammar Summary
Here is a quick reference of all the grammar topics covered in this unit:
tables: I insist on finishing my homework before playing.
She believes in the power of education.
Let's get on with the lesson.
If you break the rules, you will get into trouble.
The excitement began to ebb away.
His emotions began to roil over.
Refers to a period in the past
In those days, children played outside without screens.
Eventually, I learned to be brave.
At a specific moment in the past
At the time, I did not understand why it was important.
Before or at a certain year
Shows hesitation or a change in thought
Well, I think we should try a different approach.
Basically, you need to practise every day.
For example, you can read a book in English.
In fact, many people find grammar rewarding.
Actually, I enjoyed the grammar lesson.
Actions in progress in the past
Action completed before another past action
She had finished before he arrived.
She said that she was happy.

paragraphs: Year 8 -- Unit 7
Historical Figures and Legacy
Unit Overview

Welcome to Unit 7 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the theme of Historical Figures and Legacy -- a topic that invites you to think about how individuals shape history and how their actions echo through generations. You will read a powerful story set in 1948 East Africa, learn essential grammar structures for narrating past events, and build a rich vocabulary for discussing argument, leadership, emotion, and change.

The centrepiece of this unit is an original story called "The Rebellion of Silence." It follows a quiet but determined student named Amari at a fictional East African boarding school during the late colonial period. Through Amari's journey, you will see how silence can be a form of protest and how one person's initiative can change the tone of an entire community. The story raises questions about obedience, courage, freedom of expression, and what it truly means to leave a legacy.

Throughout this unit, you will practise telling stories about the past using Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses. You will also study Past Perfect, Reported Speech, Verbs Followed by Prepositions, Phrasal Verbs, Reflexive Pronouns, Time Phrases, and Discourse Markers. These grammar tools will help you narrate events clearly, report what others have said, and connect your ideas in both speech and writing.

This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through each section at your own speed, with support from your AI Tutor available around the clock and live teacher sessions twice a week. Every section includes activities, reflection questions, and practice exercises to keep you engaged and help you track your progress.
How This Unit Works
Read through each section carefully at your own pace.
Complete all activities and exercises in your notebook or on screen.
Use the Ask Your AI Tutor prompts whenever you need help or want to go deeper.
Attend your twice-weekly teacher sessions to ask questions and practise speaking.
Check your understanding with the Self-Assessment at the end of the unit.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use the Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses correctly to narrate events and describe actions in progress in the past.
Form and use the Past Perfect tense to show which action happened first when telling a story.
Convert direct speech into Reported Speech, adjusting pronouns, verb tenses, and word order correctly.
Identify and use verbs followed by prepositions such as insist on, believe in, waver between, and implore for.
Recognise and use common phrasal verbs including get on with, get into trouble, ebb away, and roil over.
Use reflexive pronouns (myself, yourself, himself, herself, themselves, etc.) correctly in sentences.
Employ time phrases such as in those days, eventually, at the time, and by 1948 to signal when events happened.
Use discourse markers such as well, basically, for example, in fact, and actually to connect ideas smoothly.
Read and analyse a historical fiction story set in East Africa, identifying themes of legacy, protest, and leadership.
Expand your vocabulary with over 30 new words related to argument, emotion, historical reasoning, and character.
Write a short narrative essay using past tenses, reported speech, and new vocabulary.
Speak confidently about historical figures, personal heroes, and the concept of legacy.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Which of these learning objectives do I need to focus on most? Can you give me a quick quiz on one of them?
Vocabulary Preview

Before you begin reading the story, it is important to become familiar with the key vocabulary you will encounter. This unit introduces 33 words that relate to argument, emotion, character, and historical settings. Take a few minutes now to scan the list below. You do not need to memorise every word immediately -- you will meet them again in the story, grammar exercises, and vocabulary activities later in the unit.
Quick-Scan Word List
Here are all 33 words for this unit, grouped by theme to help you see how they connect.
Character and Personality
Argument and Persuasion
Change and Movement
Difficulty and Effort
Systems and Structures
Actions and Exchange

You will find a complete vocabulary reference with example sentences in the separate Vocabulary document for this unit. Return to that document whenever you need a reminder of what a word means or how to use it in a sentence.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you use the words benign, emphatic, and initiative in three sentences about school life?
Grammar Introduction

This unit covers several grammar topics that all work together to help you tell stories about the past. Here is a brief overview of what you will learn. Each topic is covered in full detail in the separate Grammar document, but this introduction will give you a clear starting point.
Past Simple vs Past Continuous

The Past Simple tense describes actions that happened and finished in the past. You form it by adding -ed to regular verbs (played, walked, finished) or by using the special form for irregular verbs (went, wrote, found). The Past Continuous tense describes actions that were in progress at a particular time in the past. You form it with was/were + verb-ing (was writing, were playing).

When you combine these two tenses, you can show that one action interrupted another, or that two actions were happening at the same time. For example: "Amari was writing an essay when the prefect found his notebook." Here, the Past Continuous (was writing) shows the ongoing action, and the Past Simple (found) shows the action that interrupted it.
This combination is essential for storytelling. In "The Rebellion of Silence," you will see many examples of these tenses working together to create a vivid picture of events unfolding in time.
Past Perfect

The Past Perfect tense helps you show which action happened first when you are describing two past events. You form it with had + past participle (had finished, had written, had seen). For example: "By the time the headmaster arrived, Amari had already hidden his notebook." The Past Perfect makes the order of events crystal clear.
Reported Speech

Reported Speech (also called indirect speech) is what you use when you tell someone what another person said, without quoting their exact words. You change the pronouns, shift the verb tense back, and adjust time expressions. For example, if Kito said, "We need to keep this going," you would report it as: Kito said that they needed to keep that going. You will practise converting direct speech into reported speech throughout this unit.
Verbs Followed by Prepositions

Some English verbs must be followed by a specific preposition to make sense. For this unit, the key combinations are: insist on, believe in, waver between, and implore for. Getting the preposition right is important because using the wrong one can change the meaning or make the sentence sound unnatural.
Phrasal Verbs

Phrasal verbs combine a verb with a preposition or adverb to create a new meaning. In this unit, you will learn: get on with (to continue doing something), get into trouble (to find yourself in difficulty), ebb away (to gradually decrease), and roil over (to become disturbed or agitated). These are common in everyday English and appear naturally in the story.
Reflexive Pronouns

Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and object of a sentence are the same person. The forms are: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. For example: "Amari taught himself to write persuasive essays." Reflexive pronouns add a personal touch to your writing and help you express self-directed actions.
Time Phrases and Discourse Markers

Time phrases like in those days, eventually, at the time, and by 1948 help you signal when events took place. Discourse markers like well, basically, actually, in fact, and for example help you connect your ideas and guide your reader or listener through your argument. Both are essential for smooth, natural storytelling.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you explain the difference between Past Simple and Past Continuous using an example from everyday school life?
Reading and Listening

The story for this unit is called "The Rebellion of Silence." It is set in 1948 at a fictional East African boarding school called Kambazi Boys' School, in the hills of northern Tanganyika (modern-day Tanzania). The story follows Amari, a quiet but thoughtful student who uses the power of writing to challenge an unjust rule banning debate and political discussion on campus.
You will find the complete story in the separate Story document for this unit. Before you read, take a moment to prepare yourself with the pre-reading activities below.
Pre-Reading: Setting the Scene
Think about the following questions before you begin reading. You do not need to write answers now, but let them guide your thinking as you read.
What do you know about East Africa in the 1940s? What was happening politically at that time?
Have you ever been told to stay quiet about something you felt strongly about? How did it make you feel?
What does the word "rebellion" mean to you? Can a rebellion be silent?
What qualities do you think make someone a historical figure? Do they have to be famous?
While You Read: Active Reading Tasks
As you read "The Rebellion of Silence," keep your notebook open and complete the following tasks.
Vocabulary spotting: Underline or note any vocabulary words from the preview list that appear in the story. Try to understand their meaning from the context before checking the definition.
Grammar awareness: Look for examples of Past Simple, Past Continuous, and Past Perfect tenses. Write down at least three examples of each.
Character tracking: As you read, note how Amari changes from the beginning to the end of the story. What events cause him to change?
Theme identification: The story explores themes of silence, protest, leadership, and legacy. Write down one quote from the story that represents each theme.
Reported speech practice: Find at least two examples of dialogue in the story and convert them into reported speech in your notebook.
Post-Reading: Comprehension Questions
After you have finished reading the story, answer these questions in complete sentences. Use past tenses and vocabulary from the unit wherever possible.
Why did Headmaster Dalton ban debate and political discussion at Kambazi Boys' School?
What did Amari find when he slipped behind the old assembly hall? Who was there, and what were they doing?
How did Amari contribute to the group's efforts? What did he decide to do?
What was the title of Amari's first anonymous essay, and what happened when it was found?
How did the school's atmosphere change after more students began writing essays?
What happened during the Saturday dorm inspection, and what were the consequences for Amari?
How did Amari respond to Kito's criticism that his "quiet rebellion" was not working?
Describe what happened at the speech contest. What did Amari say, and how did the audience react?
What was the new initiative that the school board approved near the end of the story?
At the end of the story, Amari returns to Kambazi years later. What has changed, and what has stayed the same?
Post-Reading: Deeper Thinking
These questions ask you to think beyond the surface of the story. There are no single correct answers -- what matters is that you support your ideas with evidence from the text.
Amari says, "Resistance is not always noise." Do you agree? Can you think of real historical examples where silence or quiet action created change?
Miss Kamari tells Amari, "That depends on who follows you." What does she mean? Why is this important for the idea of legacy?
The story is set during the late colonial period in East Africa. How does the political setting shape the characters' actions and choices?
Compare Amari and Kito. How are their approaches to resistance different? Which approach do you think is more effective, and why?
The head prefect Mwanaidi joins the discussion club despite her conservative views. Why is her participation significant?
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you help me analyse why Amari chose to write anonymously at first? What does this tell us about his character?
Speaking Practice

Speaking is a skill that grows with practice. The activities below are designed to help you practise speaking about the themes and language of this unit. You can do these activities with a classmate, a family member, or your AI Tutor.
Activity 1: Story Retelling

Retell the story of "The Rebellion of Silence" in your own words. Try to include at least five vocabulary words from the unit and use a mix of Past Simple and Past Continuous tenses. Aim to speak for two to three minutes without stopping. If you are working with a partner, take turns retelling different sections of the story.
Activity 2: Discussion Questions
Discuss the following questions with a partner, in a small group, or with your AI Tutor. Give full answers and support your opinions with reasons.
Who is someone you consider a historical figure? It does not have to be someone famous -- it could be a grandparent, a community leader, or a teacher. Why do you admire them?
Do you think students should be allowed to discuss politics at school? Why or why not?
What does "legacy" mean to you? What kind of legacy would you like to leave behind?
In the story, Amari says, "Speech is breath made brave." What does this mean to you?
Activity 3: Reported Speech Role-Play

Work with a partner. One person plays the role of a character from the story (Amari, Kito, Dalton, Miss Kamari, or Mwanaidi) and says something in direct speech. The other person reports what they said using reported speech. Then swap roles. Here are some example starting lines:
Amari: "I believe in learning and questions."
Dalton: "Debate and politics will be forbidden on school grounds."
Kito: "We need to keep this going."
Miss Kamari: "You have done something rare."
Mwanaidi: "I do not agree with all this liberal chatter, but I respect the courage it takes to speak."
After your partner speaks, report what they said. For example: Amari said that he believed in learning and questions.
Activity 4: Mini-Debate

Choose one of the following topics and prepare a short argument (one to two minutes). Use discourse markers (well, basically, in fact, actually, for example) to connect your ideas. Then present your argument to a partner or your AI Tutor.
Silence is a more powerful form of protest than shouting.
Young people have the right to discuss politics at school.
One person can change the direction of a whole community.
It is better to follow rules even when you disagree with them.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that silence is a powerful form of protest. Please argue the other side.
Writing Practice

Writing allows you to bring together everything you have learned -- vocabulary, grammar, ideas, and structure. The activities below will guide you through different types of writing, from short exercises to a full narrative essay.
Activity 1: Sentence Transformation
Rewrite the following sentences using the grammar structure indicated in brackets. Write your answers in your notebook.
"I am happy with my essay," said Amari. (Change to reported speech.)
The students played football. Amari wrote an essay. (Combine using Past Continuous and Past Simple.)
Amari finished the essay. The headmaster called him. (Rewrite using Past Perfect to show which happened first.)
She taught herself to speak three languages. (Identify the reflexive pronoun and explain its use.)
In those days, silence was enforced through rules and fear. (Identify the time phrase and explain what it signals.)
Activity 2: Paragraph Writing

Write a paragraph of 80 to 100 words describing a time when you or someone you know had to be brave. Use at least three vocabulary words from this unit and at least one example each of the Past Simple, Past Continuous, and Past Perfect tenses. Begin your paragraph with a time phrase such as "In those days" or "At the time."
Activity 3: Narrative Essay

Write a narrative essay of 200 to 300 words on one of the following topics. Your essay should include an introduction, a body with at least two paragraphs, and a conclusion. Use the grammar structures and vocabulary from this unit throughout your essay.
Write about a person in your community who has made a lasting impact. Describe what they did, why it mattered, and what their legacy is.
Imagine you are a student at Kambazi Boys' School in 1948. Write a diary entry describing the day the headmaster banned debate. Include your feelings, what you saw and heard, and what you plan to do.
Write about a time when silence spoke louder than words. Describe the situation, the people involved, and what happened as a result.
Writing Checklist
Before you submit your essay, check the following:
Have you used at least five vocabulary words from this unit?
Have you used the Past Simple and Past Continuous correctly?
Have you included at least one example of the Past Perfect?
Have you used at least one example of reported speech?
Have you included time phrases and discourse markers to connect your ideas?
Have you checked your spelling and punctuation?
Does your essay have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you read my essay and give me feedback on my use of past tenses and vocabulary? Here is what I wrote...
Activities and Exercises

This section brings together grammar, vocabulary, and reading comprehension in a series of varied exercises. Work through each one carefully. The answers are provided at the end of this section so you can check your work.
Exercise 1: Past Simple vs Past Continuous -- Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in brackets. Decide whether to use the Past Simple or the Past Continuous.

Vocabulary Reinforcement

Learning new words takes repetition and active use. The activities below are designed to help you move vocabulary from your short-term memory into your long-term memory. Do not try to rush through these -- take your time and think carefully about each word.
Activity 1: Word Families
Many vocabulary words belong to word families. For each word below, write the related forms (noun, verb, adjective, adverb) where they exist. Use a dictionary or your AI Tutor if you need help.
Activity 2: Synonym and Antonym Pairs

Activity 3: Vocabulary Storytelling

Write a short paragraph (50 to 80 words) using at least six vocabulary words from this unit. Your paragraph should be about a real or imagined event at a school. Try to use the words naturally, not just as a list. Here is an example of how to start:
"At the time, the school had a benign atmosphere. Students were ecstatic about the new discussion club. However, some teachers found the initiative strenuous to manage..."
Activity 4: Flashcard Creation

Create flashcards for the ten words you find most challenging. On one side, write the word and its part of speech. On the other side, write the definition and an original example sentence. Review your flashcards every day this week. You can also ask your AI Tutor to quiz you on them.

Self-Study Tips
Effective self-study is about building good habits and using your time wisely. Here are some strategies to help you get the most out of this unit.
Reading Strategies
Read twice: The first time you read the story, focus on understanding the plot. The second time, focus on grammar and vocabulary.
Annotate: Use a pencil or sticky notes to mark words you do not know, grammar structures you notice, and ideas you want to think about more.
Summarise: After each section of the story, pause and write a one-sentence summary of what happened. This helps you track the plot and improves your comprehension.
Read aloud: Reading the story aloud helps you practise pronunciation and hear the rhythm of English sentences. It also helps you notice grammar structures more easily.
Grammar Strategies
Learn the patterns: Grammar is about patterns. Once you understand the pattern for Past Simple (subject + past form) and Past Continuous (subject + was/were + -ing), you can apply it to any verb.
Practise with your own sentences: Do not just read examples -- write your own. The more you create your own sentences, the faster you will learn.
Use colour coding: When you are studying grammar, use different colours for different tenses. For example, underline Past Simple verbs in blue and Past Continuous verbs in green.
Test yourself: Cover the answers and try to complete the exercises from memory. Check your answers only after you have finished.
Vocabulary Strategies
Use new words in conversation: Try to use at least two new vocabulary words each day in your conversations, even if it feels a little awkward at first.
Keep a vocabulary journal: Write each new word in a dedicated notebook. Include the definition, part of speech, an example sentence, and a personal note about how you remember it.

Group words by theme: The vocabulary in this unit is grouped by theme (character, argument, change, etc.). Learning words in groups helps you remember them because your brain creates connections between related ideas.
Review regularly: Do not wait until the end of the unit to review your vocabulary. Spend five minutes every day going over your flashcards or vocabulary journal.
Speaking Strategies
Talk to your AI Tutor: Your AI Tutor is available around the clock. Use it to practise retelling the story, answering discussion questions, or rehearsing your debate arguments.
Record yourself: Use your phone or computer to record yourself speaking. Listen to the recording and notice areas where you can improve.
Practise with a partner: Find a classmate, sibling, or parent to practise with. The speaking activities in this unit are designed for pair or group work.
Do not worry about mistakes: Mistakes are a natural part of learning. The more you speak, the more confident you will become.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: I find it hard to remember vocabulary. Can you suggest a memorisation technique that works well for visual learners?

paragraphs: The Rebellion of Silence
Year 8 -- Unit 7 Story
Theme: Voice, leadership, and ideological change in a tense political setting
Setting: A fictional East African boarding school in 1948
Genre: Historical fiction with emotional depth and political debate
Part One: The Announcement

In 1948, the stone paths of Kambazi Boys' School twisted between mango trees and unsightly dormitory blocks. The buildings bore the wear of time -- peeling paint, rusted windows -- but the minds inside them were ecstatic with questions.

Among the Form 4 students was Amari, a boy with a quiet disposition and a habit of observing more than speaking. He kept a leather notebook in his pocket, filled with phrases, speeches, and small essays scribbled in the margins. His teachers called him bright. His classmates called him strange. But most saw him as harmless -- a benign shadow in the back row.
That was before the announcement.
Headmaster Dalton, a stiff British man with a stiff red nose, summoned the students to the courtyard. His voice boomed: "Starting next week, debate and politics will be forbidden on school grounds."
There was a long pause. Then murmurs.
"For those who do not comply," Dalton added, "we will have no choice but to banish them."
Amari's hand tightened around his notebook.
Part Two: The Secret Group

By 1948, whispers of independence had begun to drift across the continent like wind across long grass. Radios crackled with stories from Nairobi, Accra, and Dar es Salaam. But here, in the hills of northern Tanganyika, silence was still enforced -- through rules, expectations, and fear.

The students were instructed to focus on their studies and not "get into trouble." One evening, while others played football, Amari slipped behind the old assembly hall. There, he found Kito, his classmate, and three others gathered around a candle. They were sharing a cache of newspapers and pamphlets hidden in a biscuit tin.
"We need to keep this going," Kito whispered. "Debate, writing, everything. We can't let them take our voices."
Amari wavered. "But what if they catch us?"
"They can't expel us all."
Actually, they could. And they had before. But something in Kito's voice kindled a flame inside Amari -- something bold, profound.
"I'll write," Amari said.
Part Three: The Anonymous Essays

He began crafting essays at night, under the blankets. One was titled The Burden of Obedience. Another: When Laws Become Shackles. His tone was cautious but emphatic, each sentence carefully justified. He used phrases like "In those days" and "Eventually, the silence will end."
He submitted the first anonymously, sliding it under the common room door where staff sometimes found lost items. To his surprise, it was read aloud during morning literature class.
"This is... daring," said Miss Kamari, the Kiswahili teacher. She raised an eyebrow but said nothing more.
Word spread. Students began slipping quotes into conversation. Debates re-emerged -- furtive but fierce. Kambazi's silence was ebbing.
Part Four: Discovery
Then, during a Saturday dorm inspection, a prefect found Amari's notebook hidden behind a loose brick. It was taken straight to the headmaster.
"You wrote this?" Dalton asked the next morning, waving a sheet with red annotations.
Amari nodded, trying not to waver.
Dalton sighed. "You've caused... unrest. And now you'll replenish it with order."
He assigned Amari to write a formal apology and clean every classroom for a week.
"But sir," Amari began.
"No counterarguments," Dalton snapped.
Part Five: Quiet Resistance
That week was strenuous. By midday each day, Amari's arms ached. Still, he noticed things -- how quickly silence returned, how quickly fear was implored.
Kito was furious. "You should have resisted! Why didn't you make a scene?"
"I did what I had to," Amari replied. "Resistance isn't always noise."
"Well," Kito muttered, "your quiet rebellion isn't working."
Actually, it was.
Two days later, another essay appeared -- this time by someone else. Then another. And another. They were printed by hand, passed under desks. A system had formed. It was systematic.
Part Six: Cracks in the Wall
One night, Dalton addressed the students.
"I know some of you believe in change. But this school is not a parliament. It is a place of learning."
He implored them to focus on books, not battles.
But even as he spoke, the walls of silence were starting to crack.
Part Seven: The Speech Contest
At the term's end, the school hosted a speech contest. Dalton considered canceling it, but the board insisted. "It teaches confidence," they said.
Students submitted their topics in sealed envelopes. Amari's title was simple: On the Edge of Thought.
When he stood to speak, the hall quieted.
"In those days," he began, "we believed that silence was peace. That if we obeyed, we would be safe."
He paused. "But silence is not always peace. It is sometimes the defect in freedom."
He went on, telling the story -- not by name, but clearly -- of what had happened. He ended with a quote:
"Speech is breath made brave."
The hall clapped. Dalton did not.
Part Eight: The Board's Decision
By the next week, Amari had been called to the office again. The board was debating whether to banish him.
Dalton stared. "Do you justify your actions?"
Amari said, "I believe in learning. I believe in questions. If that's a fault, then I'm guilty."
Dalton blinked.
"You're lucky the board is more liberal than I am."
Amari left, not sure if he'd been punished or protected.
Part Nine: The Discussion Club

Weeks passed. The rainy season brought mud to the courtyards and dampness to the dorms. But it also brought a new initiative: student-run discussion clubs, approved by the board under strict guidelines. Dalton remained skeptical, but he had lost his ability to roil the student body into submission.
Amari was invited to help organise the first club meeting. He declined at first, but Kito insisted.
"You started this. You should speak."
"Well," Amari said slowly, "maybe I'll attend."
The meeting was held in the old library, a room that had once been used for storage. Now, swept clean and lined with borrowed stools, it pulsed with energy.

Topics ranged from the circumference of social justice to the defects in colonial education systems. Some discussions were clumsy. Others, emphatic. Amari noticed students who had never raised a hand in class were suddenly leading arguments.
Even the head prefect -- a strict, conservative student named Mwanaidi -- joined the second meeting.
"I don't agree with all this liberal chatter," she said. "But I respect the courage it takes to speak."
Her presence changed everything. It gave the movement weight, credibility.
Part Ten: Legacy
One afternoon, Amari was called to Miss Kamari's office. She greeted him with tea.
"You've done something rare," she said.
"I got in trouble," Amari replied.
"You changed the tone of the school."
He smiled, then frowned. "But what happens after I leave?"
"That depends on who follows you."
Years later, Amari would return to Kambazi as a speaker. He would walk past the old dormitories, past the mango trees, and into the new auditorium named after Miss Kamari.
A portrait of her hung near the door. The caption read: She believed in brave breath.
He stepped onto the stage. The room was packed. Students from every year sat shoulder to shoulder, eyes bright, hands full of notebooks.
He took a deep breath.
"In those days," he began, "silence was safe. But safety is not the same as freedom. And freedom does not always shout. Sometimes it waits. It listens. And then it speaks."
And speak, he did.
Vocabulary Used in This Story
The following vocabulary words from Unit 7 appear in this story:
benign, reprieve, defect, disposition, systematic, cache, strenuous, conclusion, waver, ebb
replenish, exasperate, petulant, banish, conservative, emphatic, profound, counterargument
implore, ecstatic, justify, connoisseur, abate, circumference, barter, roil, habitation
liberal, unsightly, tone, initiative
Grammar Structures in This Story
As you re-read the story, look for examples of:
Past Simple vs Past Continuous (e.g., "while others played football, Amari slipped behind the hall")
Past Perfect (e.g., "whispers of independence had begun to drift across the continent")
Reported Speech (e.g., Dalton's announcements and commands)
Verbs + Prepositions (e.g., believe in, insist on, waver between)
Phrasal Verbs (e.g., get into trouble, get on with, ebb away)
Reflexive Pronouns and Time Phrases (e.g., "In those days," "By 1948")
Discourse Markers (e.g., actually, well, basically)
Year 8 -- Unit 7 Vocabulary
Historical Figures and Legacy -- Word List and Practice
Complete Vocabulary List

This document contains all 33 vocabulary words for Unit 7. Each word is listed with its part of speech, definition, and an example sentence. Study this list carefully and return to it whenever you need a reminder.
Note: The source list contained 33 entries. Words 32 and 33 are duplicates of earlier entries. You have 31 unique words to learn in this unit.
Vocabulary Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Match the Word to the Definition
Draw a line (or write the letter) to match each word with its correct definition.
Exercise 2: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence.
Word bank: disposition, systematic, waver, ebb, counterargument, implore, justify, liberal, tone, abate
Fatima has a cheerful __________ that brightens every room she enters.
The teacher used a __________ method to explain the grammar rules step by step.

As the evening wore on, the energy in the room began to __________.
In a debate, you must always prepare a strong __________ for the other side.

Answers:
disposition
systematic
waver

Exercise 3: Use the Word in a Sentence
Write an original sentence for each of the following words. Your sentence should show that you understand the meaning of the word. Do not copy the example sentences from the word list.
reprieve
petulant
conservative
circumference

roil

defect
exasperate
Exercise 4: Vocabulary in Context -- Story Sentences
Each sentence below is inspired by the story "The Rebellion of Silence." Choose the correct vocabulary word to complete each sentence.
Word bank: benign, cache, emphatic, strenuous, initiative, banish, profound, waver, systematic, unsightly
Most students saw Amari as a __________ shadow who would never cause trouble.
Kito and the others had hidden a __________ of newspapers in a biscuit tin.
Amari's tone in his essays was cautious but __________.
The week of cleaning classrooms was a __________ punishment for Amari.
Amari showed great __________ by starting the essay campaign on his own.
Dalton threatened to __________ any student who disobeyed the rule.
Something in Kito's voice kindled a __________ feeling inside Amari.
Amari tried not to __________ when the headmaster confronted him.
The student essays formed a __________ network, passed from desk to desk.

Answers:

Exercise 5: Word Families
Complete the table by filling in the missing word forms. Use a dictionary if needed.
Suggested Answers:
Exercise 6: Odd One Out
In each group of four words, one word does not belong. Circle it and explain why.
benign, liberal, petulant, ecstatic (Hint: which word has a negative meaning about personality?)
ebb, abate, replenish, waver (Hint: which word means to increase rather than decrease?)
cache, habitation, circumference, initiative (Hint: which word is about starting things, not places or shapes?)
emphatic, profound, unsightly, systematic (Hint: which word describes appearance rather than intensity or method?)
Answers:
petulant -- it describes a negative personality trait, while the others are positive or neutral.
replenish -- it means to fill up or increase, while the others mean to decrease or be uncertain.
initiative -- it is about starting things on your own, while the others relate to places, stores, or measurements.
unsightly -- it describes appearance, while the others describe intensity, depth, or organisation.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you test me on the Unit 7 vocabulary? Give me 10 sentences with blanks and I will try to fill in the correct word.
tables: Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.
A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant.
After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.
A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly.
The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.
A person's usual mood or way of thinking.
Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.
Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical.
She used a systematic approach to solve the maths problems step by step.
A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe.
The hikers found a cache of supplies hidden in the forest.
Requiring a lot of effort and energy.
Running a marathon is a strenuous activity that takes a lot of practice.
The final part of something where you summarise or decide what it means.
At the end of the story, the conclusion helped me understand the main message.
To be unsure about something or to change your mind.
She started to waver between choosing chocolate or vanilla ice cream.
To decrease or become less, especially in strength or intensity.
As the tide began to ebb, the water slowly moved away from the shore.
To fill something up again or make it full after it has been used.
After we drank all the juice, we needed to replenish the pitcher with more.
To make someone very annoyed or frustrated.
It can exasperate teachers when students do not pay attention in class.
Easily annoyed and often complaining, like a child.
After losing the game, he became petulant and started to sulk.
To send someone away or make them leave a place, often as punishment.
The king decided to banish the thief from the kingdom.
Someone who prefers traditional values and is cautious about change.
My grandfather is very conservative; he likes to keep things the way they have always been.
She gave an emphatic 'no' when asked if she wanted to go to the party.
Very deep or intense; having a strong effect or meaning.
The book had a profound impact on how I think about friendship.
An argument that opposes or disagrees with another argument.
In a debate, it is important to listen to the counterargument to understand the other side.
To ask someone very seriously and urgently to do something.
She implored her friend to help her study for the big test.
She was ecstatic when she found out she won the competition.
To give reasons or explanations so that something makes sense.
You need to justify your answer by explaining why you think it is correct.
A person who knows a lot about something and can judge its quality.
My uncle is a connoisseur of fine teas and can identify each blend by taste.
To become less strong or decrease in amount.
The storm began to abate, and the rain stopped falling.
The distance around the edge of a circle.
To find the circumference of a circle, you can multiply the diameter by 3.14.
To exchange goods or services for other goods without using money.
Instead of paying with money, they decided to barter and trade old books for new ones.
To make something messy or disturbed, especially water or a situation.
The strong wind began to roil the surface of the lake, creating big waves.
A place where people or animals live.
The forest is a natural habitation for many animals.
Open to new ideas and willing to change traditional beliefs.
My teacher has liberal views about education, believing students should have more freedom.
Not pleasant to look at; ugly or unattractive.
The unsightly rubbish on the street made the neighbourhood look dirty.
The quality or character of a sound, or the way someone speaks.
The teacher's tone was friendly, making the students feel comfortable.
The ability to start something on your own without being told.
She showed great initiative by organising the school event all by herself.
(See word 22 above -- this word is listed once.)
(See word 8 above -- this word is listed once.)
a) To fill something up again
c) Kind and gentle; not harmful
d) Expressing something strongly and clearly
e) Requiring a lot of effort and energy
f) A hidden store of things
g) To send someone away as punishment
h) Not pleasant to look at
i) The ability to start something on your own
j) Very deep; having a strong effect

paragraphs: Year 8 - Unit 8 Grammar
Present Perfect Continuous and Related Structures

This grammar reference covers all the grammar structures you need to master in Unit 8. Use it alongside your main lesson and story documents. Each section includes clear explanations, step-by-step formation guides, real examples, common mistakes to avoid, memory tips, and practice exercises with answer keys.
1. Present Perfect Continuous
What It Means and Why It Matters

The Present Perfect Continuous tense describes actions that started in the past and are still continuing now, or actions that have recently stopped but whose effects are still visible. This tense is important because it gives your listener or reader a sense of duration and ongoing effort.
Example: She has been crusading for change since last year. This means she started crusading last year, and she is still doing it now.
How to Form It
The structure is: Subject + has/have + been + verb-ing
Use have been with I, you, we, they: I have been studying.
Use has been with he, she, it: He has been playing soccer.
Negative: Add not after has/have: She has not been studying.
Question: Move has/have before the subject: Have you been eating?
Step-by-Step Guide
Identify the subject: Who is performing the action? (I, you, he, she, it, we, they)
Choose has or have: Use has with he/she/it and have with I/you/we/they.
Add been: This word is always included in the Present Perfect Continuous.
Add the verb with -ing: This is the main action. For example: write becomes writing, prepare becomes preparing.
Add time expressions if needed: Common ones include since, for, all day, all week.
Examples
They have been working on the project since Monday.
I have been learning English for three years.
We have been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes.
He has been playing video games all day.
You have been helping your sister with her homework.
She has been writing since she was ten years old.
The streets have been teeming with visitors since the festival started.
They have been preparing for the competition for three months.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Forgetting "been"
Incorrect: She has working hard.
Correct: She has been working hard.
Mistake 2: Using the wrong form of "have"
Incorrect: They has been playing.
Correct: They have been playing.
Mistake 3: Forgetting to add "-ing"
Incorrect: I have been play.
Correct: I have been playing.
Memory Tip

Think of the Present Perfect Continuous as a bridge between the past and the present. The formula is: has/have + been + action-ing. This bridge connects what started in the past to what is happening right now.
Practice Exercise
Complete the sentences using the Present Perfect Continuous tense:
She (dance) __________ for two hours.
They (watch) __________ that movie since last night.
I (read) __________ this book all week.
He (train) __________ for the marathon for months.
We (wait) __________ for you since morning.
The wind (blow) __________ strongly all afternoon.
You (practise) __________ your speech for days.
It (rain) __________ since we arrived.
✅ Answer Key
1. has been dancing
2. have been watching
3. have been reading
4. has been training
5. have been waiting
6. has been blowing
7. have been practising
8. has been raining
2. Reported Speech
What It Is and Why It Matters

Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is how you tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. This is a vital skill in storytelling because narrators frequently need to report what characters have said. It is also essential in everyday communication when you share information from conversations.
How to Form It
When converting direct speech to reported speech, you need to make three key changes:
Change the pronouns to match the new speaker. "I am happy" becomes "he was happy" (I changes to he).
Shift the verb tense back one step. Present Simple becomes Past Simple. Present Continuous becomes Past Continuous. Present Perfect becomes Past Perfect. Will becomes would. Can becomes could.
Adjust time and place expressions. Today becomes that day. Tomorrow becomes the next day. Here becomes there. This becomes that.
Using "Said" vs. "Told"
Use said when you do not mention the listener: He said he would come.
Use told when you mention who was spoken to: He told me he would come.
Reporting Questions
For yes/no questions, use if or whether and change the word order to a statement:
Direct: "Are you coming?" Reported: She asked if I was coming.
Direct: "Have you finished?" Reported: He asked whether I had finished.
For wh-questions, keep the question word but change to statement word order:
Direct: "Where are you going?" Reported: She asked where I was going.
Reporting Commands and Requests
Use to + infinitive for commands and requests:
Direct: "Please help me." Reported: He asked me to help him.
Direct: "Don't be late." Reported: She told me not to be late.
Examples
He said the antics were foolish.
She asked them to clarify their roles.
They said they would arrive by noon.
He told me to finish my homework.
Amira said she had always feared water.
Safiya told her that the crusade was not over.
Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
Mistake 1: Keeping the same verb tense
Incorrect: She said she loves reading.
Correct: She said she loved reading. (Present shifts to past.)
Mistake 2: Using direct quotes instead of reported speech
Incorrect: She said "I love reading."
Correct: She said she loved reading.
Memory Tip
Think of reported speech as telling a story about a conversation. When you tell a story, you shift the details to fit your perspective: shift the pronouns, shift the tense, and tell it your way.
Practice Exercise
Transform the following sentences into reported speech:
"I love reading," he said.
"Can you help me?" she asked.
"Please close the door," he requested.
"We have been practising all day," they said.
"I will finish my story tonight," Amira said.
"Where is the competition?" he asked.
✅ Answer Key
1. He said he loved reading.
2. She asked if I could help her.
3. He asked me to close the door.
4. They said they had been practising all day.
5. Amira said she would finish her story that night.
6. He asked where the competition was.
3. Prepositions with Nouns
What It Means

A preposition is a word that shows the relationship between a noun and other words in a sentence. Common prepositions include in, at, on, during, for, with, by, and of. When combined with nouns, they create prepositional phrases that describe where, when, or how something happens.
How to Form Prepositional Phrases
Choose a preposition: in, at, on, during, for, with, by, of.
Add a noun: commotion, scene, crusade, counter, etc.
Use in a sentence: The children were playing in a commotion.
Examples
There was loud talking in a commotion.
The police arrived at the scene quickly.
Many people joined during the crusade.
She waited at the counter for her order.
There was a scarcity of resources in the village.
Common Mistakes
Using the wrong preposition: in the scene should be at the scene.
Omitting the noun: saying "in a" without finishing the phrase. Always complete the phrase.
Memory Tip
Think of prepositions as little connectors. They connect nouns to other parts of the sentence. Ask yourself: Where is it? or When is it? This will guide you to the right preposition.
Practice Exercise
Complete the sentences with the correct preposition:
The students were excited __________ a commotion during the lunch break.
The police gathered __________ the scene of the accident.
Many people joined together __________ the crusade for peace.
She waited __________ the counter to collect her badge.
There was a scarcity __________ clean water in the region.
✅ Answer Key
1. in
2. at
3. during
4. at
5. of
4. Past Perfect vs. Past Simple
What It Means

The Past Simple describes completed actions in the past: She finished her homework. The Past Perfect shows that one action happened before another past action: She had finished her homework before dinner. Think of the Past Perfect as the past of the past. It tells you what happened first when you are already talking about the past.
How to Form Them
Past Simple: Subject + past form of the verb (regular verbs add -ed; irregular verbs have unique forms).
Past Perfect: Subject + had + past participle of the verb.
When to Use Each
Use Past Simple for actions that happened at a specific time in the past.
Use Past Perfect when you want to show that one action happened before another past action.
Often both appear in the same sentence: After she had finished her project, she went home.
Examples
Past Simple: She finished her homework.
Past Perfect: She had finished her homework before dinner.
Combined: They had wafted into the village before the decree was read.
Combined: I saw the movie after my friend had recommended it.
Combined: By the time Amira arrived, the audience had already settled.
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Confusing the tenses
Incorrect: I ate lunch before I had gone to the store.
Correct: I had eaten lunch before I went to the store.
Mistake 2: Omitting "had"
Incorrect: She finished her project before the deadline. (when you mean the earlier action)
Correct: She had finished her project before the deadline.
Memory Tip

Picture two timelines: one for the main past action and one above it for the earlier action. The earlier action uses the Past Perfect (had + past participle), and the later action uses the Past Simple.
Practice Exercise
Choose the correct form (Past Perfect or Past Simple) to complete each sentence:
After they (finish) __________ their homework, they went outside to play.
By the time I arrived, the movie (start) __________.
She (never/see) __________ a lion before her trip to the zoo.
He (eat) __________ breakfast before he (leave) __________ for school.
The concert (begin) __________ before we (arrive) __________.
✅ Answer Key
1. had finished
2. had started
3. had never seen
4. had eaten / left
5. had begun / arrived
5. Adjective + Infinitive
What It Means

The adjective + infinitive structure uses an adjective followed by to and a base verb to express feelings or opinions about actions. Example: It is feasible to support the plan. This structure is common in both spoken and written English and is particularly useful when expressing what is possible, necessary, easy, difficult, or wise to do.
How to Form It
Choose an adjective: happy, excited, easy, difficult, foolish, important, feasible, eager.
Add "to": This connects the adjective to the action.
Add the base form of a verb (infinitive): to eat, to play, to learn, to support.
Put it together: It is important to learn English. / She is eager to compete.
Examples
It is feasible to support the plan.
It was foolish to abet them.
She is excited to travel to Japan.
It is difficult to finish the homework on time.
He is eager to play soccer this weekend.
It was brave to speak the truth on stage.
Common Mistakes
Forgetting "to": Incorrect: It's easy eat. Correct: It's easy to eat.
Wrong verb form: Incorrect: It's important studying. Correct: It's important to study.
Practice Exercise
Complete the sentences with the correct adjective + infinitive structure:
It is exciting __________ (go) to the amusement park.
It was silly __________ (forget) my homework.
It is important __________ (listen) carefully in class.
She was happy __________ (win) the competition.
It would be foolhardy __________ (ignore) the warning signs.
✅ Answer Key
1. to go
2. to forget
3. to listen
4. to win
5. to ignore
6. Comparative and Superlative Structures
What It Means

Comparatives are used when comparing two things. Superlatives are used when comparing three or more things and identifying the most extreme. These structures help you express opinions and describe the world around you more precisely.
How to Form Them
Comparative Form:
One-syllable adjectives: add -er (tall becomes taller)
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -i and add -er (happy becomes happier)
Two or more syllables: use more before the adjective (beautiful becomes more beautiful)
Superlative Form:
One-syllable adjectives: add -est (tall becomes tallest)
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change -y to -i and add -est (happy becomes happiest)
Two or more syllables: use most before the adjective (beautiful becomes most beautiful)
Irregular Forms:
Good becomes better (comparative) and best (superlative)
Bad becomes worse (comparative) and worst (superlative)
Examples
My dog is taller than your dog. (Comparative)
Sarah is the smartest student in our class. (Superlative)
This puzzle is more challenging than the last one. (Comparative)
Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world. (Superlative)
Amira's story was more powerful than any speech the judges had heard.
That was the most engrossing performance of the competition.
Common Mistakes
Using "more" with one-syllable adjectives: Incorrect: She is more tall. Correct: She is taller.
Using "most" with one-syllable adjectives: Incorrect: He is the most fast runner. Correct: He is the fastest runner.
Confusing comparative and superlative: Use comparatives for two things, superlatives for three or more.
Memory Tip
Remember the rhyme: "-er is for two, -est is for a crew!" Use -er when comparing two things and -est when comparing a group.
Practice Exercise
Fill in the blanks with the correct comparative or superlative form:
My cat is __________ (soft) than my dog.
This is the __________ (exciting) game I have ever played!
Your backpack is __________ (heavy) than mine.
That was the __________ (funny) movie I have seen this year!
This story is __________ (relevant) than the last one.
✅ Answer Key
1. softer
2. most exciting
3. heavier
4. funniest
5. more relevant
7. Adverb Placement in the Present Perfect
What It Means

When using the present perfect tense, certain adverbs must be placed in specific positions to sound natural. The most common adverbs used with the present perfect are just, already, yet, ever, and never. Correct placement makes your sentences sound fluent and clear.
Where to Place Adverbs
The general rule: place adverbs between has/have and the past participle.
just: They have just recuperated.
already: She has already seen that movie.
never: I have never tried sushi.
ever: Have you ever been engrossed in a story?
yet: Used at the end of negative sentences and questions: I have not finished yet. / Have you finished yet?
Examples
They have just finished their homework.
Have you ever travelled to another country?
She has already seen that movie.
I have never tried sushi.
He has often helped his friends with projects.
Common Mistakes
Incorrect placement: "They have recuperated just." Correct: "They have just recuperated."
Forgetting the helping verb: "He finished" instead of "He has finished."
Memory Tip
Place your adverb before the action word (past participle) to add clarity. Think: adverb before the action equals clarity.
Practice Exercise
Complete the sentences using the correct adverb (just, ever, never, already):
They have __________ seen that play.
Have you __________ tried riding a bike?
I have __________ finished my book.
She has __________ visited Paris.
He has __________ arrived at the academy.
✅ Answer Key
1. already
2. ever
3. just
4. never
5. just (or already)
End of Grammar Reference - Unit 8
Year 8 - Unit 8
Global Storytelling and Creativity
Overview

Welcome to Unit 8 of your Year 8 English course. This unit is called Global Storytelling and Creativity, and it is one of the most exciting units you will explore this year. Throughout this unit, you will discover how stories shape the world around us, how creative people use language to inspire and persuade, and how you can develop your own storytelling skills. You will read a powerful original story called The Edge of the Infinite, which follows a young Somali storyteller named Amira as she competes in a national youth storytelling competition in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. Her journey will show you that great stories do not simply entertain people; they change lives, preserve memories, and give voice to communities that might otherwise be forgotten.

In this unit, you will also focus on a range of important grammar structures. Your primary grammar focus is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, which allows you to talk about actions that started in the past and continue into the present. You will also study reported speech, which helps you tell others what someone has said without using their exact words. Additionally, you will practise using prepositions with nouns, understand the difference between the past perfect and past simple, learn about adjective plus infinitive structures, explore comparative and superlative forms, and work on adverb placement in the present perfect tense.

Storytelling is deeply woven into East African culture. From the oral traditions of Somali poetry and the Swahili coast to the modern digital narratives being created by young people across the region, stories have always been a powerful way to share knowledge, preserve history, and build understanding between communities. This unit invites you to see yourself as part of that tradition. By the end of this unit, you will have the vocabulary, grammar, and confidence to tell your own stories in English with clarity, creativity, and purpose.

Because this is a self-paced course, you can work through each section at your own speed. Take time to complete every activity, revisit sections you find challenging, and make use of the AI tutor whenever you need help. Your teacher will meet with you twice a week to discuss your progress, answer questions, and help you practise speaking. Between those sessions, this lesson is your main guide.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: What is the difference between telling a story and reporting what someone said?
Or try: Can you explain the Present Perfect Continuous tense using a storytelling example?
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you should be able to:
Use the Present Perfect Continuous tense correctly in spoken and written English to describe ongoing actions.
Convert direct speech into reported speech, adjusting pronouns, verb tenses, and time expressions appropriately.
Combine prepositions with nouns to create clear and natural-sounding prepositional phrases.
Distinguish between the past perfect and past simple and use each correctly when narrating past events.
Form adjective plus infinitive structures to express feelings and opinions about actions.
Apply comparative and superlative structures when comparing people, places, and things.
