Grammar Introduction

Grammar is the framework that holds your English together. Without it, your ideas might be clear in your mind, but they will not come across clearly to your reader or listener. In this unit, you will focus on several important grammar structures. Your main grammar focus is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, but you will also practise reported speech, prepositions with nouns, past perfect versus past simple, adjective plus infinitive constructions, comparative and superlative structures, and adverb placement.
Present Perfect Continuous (Main Focus)

The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to describe actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or actions that recently stopped but have a visible result in the present. This tense is formed using the structure: subject + has/have + been + verb-ing. It creates a sense of duration and continuity that connects the past to the present moment.

For example, consider the sentence: She has been writing stories since she was ten. This tells us that the action of writing started in the past (when she was ten) and continues up to the present. The action is not finished. Compare this with the simple past tense: She wrote stories when she was ten. This tells us that the action happened in the past and is now complete. The Present Perfect Continuous emphasises that the action is ongoing.
Here are the key rules for forming this tense:
Use have been with I, you, we, and they: I have been studying / They have been practising.
Use has been with he, she, and it: He has been running / She has been reading.
To make it negative, add not after have or has: I have not been sleeping well / She has not been eating properly.
To form a question, move have or has before the subject: Have you been waiting long? / Has he been working all day?

This tense is especially useful in storytelling because it allows you to show that characters have been engaged in activities over a period of time. In the story you will read, you will see sentences like Amira had been writing since she was ten and The students had been preparing for months. These sentences create a sense of dedication and effort that makes the narrative more compelling.
Reported Speech

Reported speech is how you tell someone what another person said, without quoting their exact words. This is essential for storytelling because narrators frequently report what characters have said. When you convert direct speech to reported speech, you usually need to change the pronouns, shift the verb tense back by one step, and adjust time expressions.

For example: Direct speech: "I love this story," she said. Reported speech: She said she loved that story. Notice how I changed to she, love changed to loved, and this changed to that. These shifts happen because you are moving the speech from the speaker's perspective to the narrator's perspective, and often from the present into the past.

Use said when you are simply reporting what someone expressed. Use told when you mention who was being spoken to. For questions, change the word order and remove the question mark: "Are you coming?" becomes She asked if I was coming. For commands or requests, use the infinitive: "Please help me" becomes He asked me to help him.
Prepositions with Nouns

Prepositions are small but powerful words like in, at, on, during, and for that show relationships between nouns and other parts of a sentence. When combined with nouns, they create prepositional phrases that tell us where, when, or how something happens. For example: in a commotion, at the scene, during the crusade. Learning which prepositions go with which nouns is essential for sounding natural in English, and the best way to learn these combinations is through reading and practice.
Past Perfect vs. Past Simple

The past simple describes completed actions: She finished her homework. The past perfect shows that one action happened before another past action: She had finished her homework before dinner. Think of the past perfect as the "past of the past." When you are telling a story and need to describe an event that happened even earlier, the past perfect is the tense you need. In the story, you will see how the author uses both tenses together to create clear timelines.
Adjective + Infinitive

This structure combines an adjective with to plus a verb to express feelings or opinions about actions. Examples: It is feasible to support the plan. / It was foolish to abet them. / She is eager to compete. This pattern appears frequently in everyday English and is particularly useful when writing about characters' emotions and intentions in stories.
Comparative and Superlative Structures

Comparatives are used to compare two things: taller, more interesting, better. Superlatives compare three or more things and identify the most extreme: tallest, most interesting, best. For one-syllable adjectives, add -er or -est. For longer adjectives, use more or most before the adjective. Remember the irregular forms: good becomes better and best; bad becomes worse and worst.
Adverb Placement in the Present Perfect

When using adverbs like just, already, yet, ever, and never with the present perfect tense, place them between have/has and the past participle. For example: They have just recuperated. / Have you ever been engrossed in a story? / She has already finished her piece. Correct adverb placement makes your sentences sound natural and fluent.

Reading and Listening

The centrepiece of this unit is the story The Edge of the Infinite. This is an original contemporary narrative set in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. The story follows Amira, a young storyteller with Somali heritage, as she competes in the National Youth StoryForge competition. Through her journey, you will explore themes of perseverance, the power of storytelling, memory, climate awareness, and the courage it takes to share difficult truths with the world.
Before You Read
Before diving into the story, take a moment to think about these questions. You can write your answers in your notebook or discuss them with your AI tutor:
Have you ever told a story in front of an audience? How did it feel?
Why do you think storytelling is important in Somali and East African cultures?
What kinds of stories do you think need to be told more often in the world today?
Have you ever been so engrossed in a story that you forgot where you were?

These questions are designed to activate your thinking and connect the story's themes to your own life. Good readers always bring their own experiences to a text, and doing so will help you understand and remember what you read.
Reading the Story

Now turn to your Year 8 - Unit 8 Story document and read The Edge of the Infinite carefully. As you read, keep a pen or pencil nearby so you can underline or highlight any vocabulary words from this unit that you recognise. Also pay attention to how the author uses the grammar structures you have been learning, especially the Present Perfect Continuous tense and reported speech.
After your first reading, try to answer these comprehension questions in your notebook:
What is the National Youth StoryForge, and why is this year's competition special?
What story does Amira tell during the competition, and what is it about?
How does Safiya help Amira prepare for the final performance?
What reaction does the audience have to Amira's performance?
What does Amira do after winning the competition? How does her life change?
What is the meaning of the title The Edge of the Infinite?
Find three examples of the Present Perfect Continuous tense in the story and write them down.
Find two examples of reported speech in the story.
Listening Practice

To develop your listening skills alongside your reading, try the following activity. Read a paragraph from the story aloud, slowly and clearly. Then, without looking at the text, try to summarise what you just read in your own words. This exercise helps you practise both pronunciation and comprehension at the same time. You can also ask your AI tutor to read a passage and then quiz you on the details.

Another excellent listening exercise is to practise converting passages from the story into reported speech. For example, read aloud the line: "I have always feared water. Not because it drowns. But because it remembers." Then convert it: Amira said she had always feared water, not because it drowned, but because it remembered. Practise this with several passages from the story to strengthen both your listening and grammar skills.

Try saying: Can you quiz me on the story The Edge of the Infinite?
Or: Can you read me a passage and ask me to summarise it in reported speech?
Speaking

Speaking is one of the most important skills you will develop in this unit. Storytelling is, at its heart, an oral tradition. Long before people wrote stories down, they told them aloud. In Somali culture, oral poetry and storytelling have been central to community life for centuries. This section gives you structured opportunities to practise your spoken English in ways that connect directly to the themes of this unit.
Speaking Activity 1: Story Retelling

After reading The Edge of the Infinite, practise retelling the story in your own words. You do not need to memorise every detail. Instead, focus on the main events and try to use at least ten vocabulary words from this unit as you speak. You can practise alone by recording yourself, or you can tell the story to a family member, a friend, or your AI tutor.

When retelling the story, try to use the Present Perfect Continuous tense naturally. For example, instead of saying Amira prepared for months, you could say Amira had been preparing for months. This small change adds depth and sophistication to your spoken English.
Speaking Activity 2: Reported Speech Conversations

Work with a partner or your AI tutor. One person makes a statement, and the other person reports it. Keep going back and forth, practising the tense shifts and pronoun changes required for reported speech.
Example exchange:
Person A: I have been studying all morning.
Person B reports: She said she had been studying all morning.
Person B: I am excited about the storytelling competition.
Person A reports: He said he was excited about the storytelling competition.
Try to keep the conversation going for at least five minutes. The more you practise, the more natural reported speech will become.
Speaking Activity 3: My Storytelling Crusade

Think about a cause or topic that matters to you. It could be anything: protecting the environment, supporting education in your community, preserving cultural traditions, or standing up against injustice. Prepare a short two-minute speech in which you explain what your crusade is, why it matters, and what you have been doing to support it. Try to use the Present Perfect Continuous tense at least three times in your speech.
Here is a framework to help you organise your speech:
Introduction: Tell your audience what your crusade is and why you care about it.
Background: Explain how you first became interested in this cause. What motivated you?
Actions: Describe what you have been doing to support your cause. Use the Present Perfect Continuous tense here.
Impact: Share any results or changes you have noticed because of your efforts.
Call to action: End by asking your audience to join your crusade or support the cause.

Writing

Writing is where all your learning comes together. In this section, you will complete several writing tasks that help you practise the grammar, vocabulary, and storytelling skills from this unit. Each task builds on the previous one, so work through them in order.
Writing Task 1: Vocabulary Sentences

Write one original sentence for each of the following ten vocabulary words. Your sentences should clearly show that you understand the meaning of each word. Try to make your sentences interesting and connected to themes of storytelling, creativity, or community.
crusade
engross
stamina
feasible
anecdote
commotion
waft
ominous
malleable
jargon
Writing Task 2: Grammar Transformation

Rewrite each of the following sentences using the grammatical structure indicated in brackets. This exercise helps you practise shifting between different grammar forms, which is an essential skill for flexible and accurate English writing.
She started writing at dawn and she is still writing now. [Present Perfect Continuous]
"I will finish my story tonight," said Amira. [Reported Speech]
The audience clapped. Amira had already left the stage. [Past Perfect + Past Simple]
It is possible to finish the project on time. [Adjective + Infinitive using "feasible"]
Hasan is tall. Ahmed is taller. Yusuf is the tallest. [Write one sentence using comparative and one using superlative]
Writing Task 3: Short Story

Write your own short story of 200 to 300 words. Your story should be set in an East African location and feature a young character who faces a challenge related to storytelling, creativity, or self-expression. Your story must include at least five vocabulary words from this unit, at least two examples of the Present Perfect Continuous tense, and at least one example of reported speech.
Before you begin writing, take a few minutes to plan your story. Think about the following elements:
Setting: Where does your story take place? A school, a market, a coastal village, an academy?
Character: Who is your main character? What are they like? What do they want?
Challenge: What obstacle does your character face?
Resolution: How does your character overcome the challenge?
Theme: What message or lesson does your story convey?

Once you have a plan, write your first draft. Do not worry about making it perfect. You can always revise and improve it later. After finishing your first draft, read it aloud to yourself or to your AI tutor. Check that your grammar is correct and that your vocabulary words are used naturally in context.
Writing Task 4: Reflective Journal Entry

Write a journal entry of 100 to 150 words reflecting on what you have learned in this unit. Think about the following questions as you write: What was the most interesting thing you learned? Which grammar structure was the most challenging for you? How has your understanding of storytelling changed? What do you want to improve in the next unit? Use the Present Perfect Continuous tense at least twice in your reflection. For example, you might write: I have been practising reported speech all week, and I am starting to feel more confident.

Activities and Exercises

This section provides a variety of exercises to help you practise and reinforce the grammar and vocabulary you have been learning throughout this unit. Exercise 1: Present Perfect Continuous - Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct Present Perfect Continuous form of the verb in brackets.
She __________ (write) stories since she was a child.
They __________ (prepare) for the competition for three months.
I __________ (read) this novel all week and I am almost finished.
He __________ (practise) his speech every evening after school.
We __________ (wait) for the results since yesterday morning.
The city __________ (teem) with visitors since the festival began.
You __________ (work) on this project for weeks. Well done!
She __________ (recuperate) at home since her illness last month.
✅ Answer Key - Exercise 1
1. has been writing
2. have been preparing
3. have been reading
4. has been practising
5. have been waiting
6. has been teeming
7. have been working
8. has been recuperating
Exercise 2: Reported Speech - Transform the Sentences
Change each sentence from direct speech to reported speech.
"I love storytelling," said Amira.
"We have been practising all day," the students told the teacher.
"Can you clarify the instructions?" she asked.
"Please support our crusade," he requested.
"The competition will begin tomorrow," the organiser announced.
"I had never seen such an engrossing performance," the judge admitted.
✅ Answer Key - Exercise 2
1. Amira said she loved storytelling.
2. The students told the teacher they had been practising all day.
3. She asked if I could clarify the instructions.
4. He requested that we support their crusade. / He asked us to support their crusade.
5. The organiser announced that the competition would begin the next day.
6. The judge admitted that he/she had never seen such an engrossing performance.
Exercise 3: Past Perfect vs. Past Simple
Choose the correct tense (past perfect or past simple) to complete each sentence.
By the time the judge __________ (arrive), Amira __________ (already / finish) her performance.
She __________ (write) the story before she __________ (enter) the competition.
The audience __________ (clap) because they __________ (never / hear) anything so moving.
After he __________ (recuperate), he __________ (return) to school.
They __________ (leave) the venue after the results __________ (be announced).
✅ Answer Key - Exercise 3
1. arrived / had already finished
2. had written / entered
3. clapped / had never heard
4. had recuperated / returned
5. left / had been announced
Exercise 4: Prepositions with Nouns
Fill in each blank with the correct preposition: in, at, on, during, for, or with.
There was a commotion __________ the hallway when the results were announced.
The performers waited nervously __________ the counter to collect their badges.
The scarcity __________ clean water affected everyone __________ the village.
She joined the crusade __________ climate awareness __________ her second year.
He stood __________ the edge of the stage, looking out __________ the sea.
✅ Answer Key - Exercise 4
1. in
2. at
3. of / in
4. for / during (or in)
5. at / at (or over)
Exercise 5: Adjective + Infinitive
Complete each sentence with a suitable adjective from the box followed by the correct infinitive form. Adjectives to use: feasible, foolhardy, eager, difficult, important.
It is __________ to __________ (support) the environment whenever you can.
She was __________ to __________ (compete) in the national finals.

✅ Answer Key - Exercise 5
1. important to support
2. eager to compete
3. foolhardy to abet
4. difficult to finish
5. feasible to complete
Exercise 6: Comparative and Superlative Structures
Rewrite each sentence using the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
Amira's story was __________ (powerful) than any speech the judges had heard.

✅ Answer Key - Exercise 6

2. most engrossing
3. better

2. Have you ever tried writing poetry?
3. They have just recovered from the competition.

Self-Study Tips

Studying on your own takes discipline, but it also gives you the freedom to learn at your own pace. Here are some practical tips to help you get the most out of this unit and build good study habits that will serve you throughout your English learning journey.
Tip 1: Use Spaced Repetition for Vocabulary

Do not try to memorise all thirty-three vocabulary words in one sitting. Instead, learn five to seven words per day. Review yesterday's words before adding new ones. After a week, go back and test yourself on all the words you have learned so far. This technique is called spaced repetition, and research shows it is one of the most effective ways to move information from short-term to long-term memory.
Tip 2: Read Aloud Every Day

Reading aloud helps you practise pronunciation, build fluency, and internalise grammar patterns. Choose a paragraph from the story or one of your own sentences and read it aloud at least three times. Focus on speaking clearly and naturally. You can also record yourself and listen back to identify areas for improvement.
Tip 3: Write a Little Every Day

Even if it is just three or four sentences, writing something in English every day makes a huge difference over time. Use your vocabulary words and grammar structures in your daily writing. You could keep a simple diary, write short descriptions of your day, or create mini stories using the words from this unit.
Tip 4: Use Your AI Tutor Actively

Your AI tutor is available around the clock, so take advantage of this resource. Do not just ask for answers. Instead, ask for explanations, examples, and feedback. For instance, you might say: Can you explain why this sentence is wrong? or Can you give me a harder exercise on reported speech? The more you interact with your tutor, the more you will learn.
Tip 5: Connect English to Your Life

The best way to remember new words and grammar structures is to use them in real life. Try using your vocabulary words when speaking with family or friends, even if you mix English with Somali or Swahili. Describe things you see around you using comparative and superlative forms. Report what someone said to you using reported speech. The more you connect English to your daily life, the more natural it will become.
Tip 6: Do Not Fear Mistakes

Making mistakes is a normal and essential part of learning a language. Every mistake you make is an opportunity to learn something new. When you get an exercise wrong, do not feel discouraged. Instead, take a moment to understand why the answer was wrong and what the correct answer should be. Over time, you will find that you make fewer mistakes as your understanding deepens.

h. a quality someone is born with
j. to help someone do something wrong

paragraphs: Year 8 - Unit 8 Story
The Edge of the Infinite
Theme: Passion, perseverance, and the power of storytelling
Setting: A semi-futuristic East African media academy in Dar es Salaam
Genre: Contemporary speculative fiction with emotional and persuasive arcs
Chapter 1: The StoryForge
Every year, the coastal city of Dar es Salaam teemed with talent, ideas, and noise during the National Youth StoryForge. But this year's competition was not like any before.

It was not just about performance. This was the first time the Ministry of Public Memory had merged the contest with the National Change Crusade, a new initiative using stories to spotlight climate, history, and hidden voices across Tanzania.
At the Mambo Media Academy, students had been preparing for months. It was a crusade of ideas, a passionate campaign to bring forgotten truths to the stage.

For twelve weeks, they had trained under mentors, developing endurance and style. The stakes were high: the winners would be broadcast on the national grid, their words archived in a cultural capsule for generations.

Amira Warsame, a seventeen-year-old with an avid curiosity and endless stamina, had been writing since she was ten. She kept her stories hidden until one teacher, Mr. Ndege, discovered her notebook of anecdotes and pushed her to audition.
Now, she stood at the edge of the outdoor amphitheatre, her heart pounding. Her breath came fast as a balmy ocean breeze rolled through, lifting the scent of roasted maize and warming plastic chairs.
Chapter 2: Backstage
Inside the preparation tent, the air was thick with commotion — last-minute lines being rehearsed, costumes being adjusted, and an explosion of jargon from students critiquing one another's arcs.
"You need to clarify the second act!" someone barked.
"Your metaphor's too malleable — what does the fire actually represent?"
Amira kept to herself, sipping ginger tea. She had written a piece called The Flood That Forgot No One, a tale about a girl who learns to speak the ocean's language after her island sinks.
"You're on in three," said a voice. It was Safiya Hassan, her mentor — sharp-eyed, calm.
Amira swallowed. "What if it's not... relevant enough?"
Safiya raised an eyebrow. "You've been crusading for three months. This story is more relevant than most speeches I've heard in parliament."
That helped. A little.
Chapter 3: On Stage
Out on stage, another performer finished a vivid retelling of a market fire in Mwanza. His voice had wafted across the audience like smoke, leaving them hushed.
Now it was Amira's turn.
She stepped onto the stage. The sea was behind her. Lights framed her. A single mic waited.
She began:
"I have always feared water. Not because it drowns. But because it remembers."
The crowd stilled. She pushed forward.
"My grandmother said the sea had an infinite tongue. That it told every story you tried to bury. She told me, when I was five, about the day our island cracked."
As she spoke, Amira's voice grew stronger. She had been writing the piece for weeks, editing at dawn, rehearsing after lights out. Every word came from her bones.
"I saw bodies float past me. I saw boats become coffins. I saw truth jut from the waves like forgotten bones."
Someone in the back gasped.
Amira told of the silence after the flood. Of the scarcity of memory. Of how even officials had denied it.
"'It didn't happen,' they said. But we knew. We had dreams of drowning every night."
She ended with:
"And when you deny the past, you abet the next flood."
Silence.
Then applause — slow, then swelling.
Chapter 4: The Aftermath
Backstage, Safiya pulled her into a hug.
"You did it."
"I feel like I've just... returned from a war," Amira said.
Safiya grinned. "That's how you know it was real."
The next day, the results were posted. Amira had made it into the final ten.
She walked into the common room, where the other students were buzzing.
"Girl, that line about the boats?" said another finalist, Khadija. "That engrossed everyone. I stopped breathing."
Someone else muttered, "They'll never let her win, though. Too heavy."
Amira smiled. "Let's see."
Chapter 5: Finals Week
Finals week was even tougher. The pressure assailed everyone. One student fainted. Two others dropped out. Amira had to recuperate after a night of nausea.
Safiya kept her focused.
"You've got the stamina. Now use it wisely. Speak not just to perform — but to change."
They refined the ending. Smoothed transitions. Sharpened pauses. Amira's script now ended with a direct challenge:
"What stories will you tell when the waters rise?"
Chapter 6: The Final Broadcast
The night of the final broadcast arrived. Lights lit the open-air venue. Drones hovered above, filming.
Ministers sat in the front row. Screens flickered in cafes across the country.
Amira wore a simple white robe. No props. Just her voice.
She began again, this time slower. Louder.
She told the same tale — but fuller. She added moments of humour, antics shared with her cousins before the storm. She described her uncle's foolhardy decision to stay behind.
"He said, 'I've lived by the sea, I'll die by the sea.' And the sea replied, 'Then let me take you.'"
The audience was engrossed.
She ended again with the same line:
"What stories will you tell when the waters rise?"
Silence.
Then a standing ovation.
Chapter 7: Victory
Backstage, a minister approached. "That was... necessary," she said. "And brave."
Later, a judge read the results.
Amira had won.
A week later, at the post-competition gala, she stood beside Safiya on a rooftop.
The breeze was soft. Balmy.
"You know," said Safiya, "when I met you, you seemed unsure. Soft."
"I still feel soft."
"But your voice? That's steel."
Amira looked at the stars. "What do I do now?"
"Now?" Safiya said. "You rest. Then you keep going. The crusade isn't over. It's just changed forms."
Chapter 8: The Edge of the Infinite

Weeks passed. Amira began receiving letters from schools, climate groups, and even one from a remote village council in Zanzibar. They had heard her story. They wanted her to come speak. To listen. To write new ones.
She travelled to places where the ocean bit the land. Where houses had vanished into saltwater. Where memory had ebbed with the tide.
At each stop, she gathered new voices. She asked old women to tell their stories, to record anecdotes into her tablet. She listened to children who explained floods with drawings.
And at each place, she asked:
"What will you tell when the waters come again?"
Eventually, she returned to Dar es Salaam. But she was not the same girl who had once worried about metaphors and stage fright.
Now, she was working on a full series — a documentary mixed with narrative. She had partnered with engineers and artists, building stories from both science and soul.
Her work was called Feasible Futures. It aimed to show not just loss, but resilience. Solutions. Ways forward.
Because it was feasible to support the planet. It was foolish not to.
And Amira, who had feared her words might vanish, now knew better.
They would waft into corners she had never seen.
They already had.
Vocabulary and Grammar Coverage
Vocabulary Used

crusade, clarify, assail, infinite, motivate, stamina, uncanny, teem, relevant, malleable, avid, foolhardy, jut, ominous, balmy, decree, waft, recuperate, innate, counter, antics, commotion, subordinate, affluent, support, anecdote, scarcity, feasible, abet, engross, jargon
Grammar Structures
Present Perfect Continuous - e.g. had been preparing, had been writing
Reported Speech - e.g. She told me, when I was five, about the day our island cracked
Prepositions + Nouns - e.g. at the edge, in the back, during the crusade
Past Perfect vs. Past Simple - e.g. had trained / stood, had written / began
Adjective + Infinitive - implied in feasible to support
Comparative/Superlative - e.g. more relevant than most speeches
Adverb Placement - e.g. had already left, had just returned
Year 8 - Unit 8 Vocabulary
Global Storytelling and Creativity

This vocabulary reference contains all thirty-three words for Unit 8. Each word includes its part of speech, definition, and an example sentence. Use this document alongside your main lesson for quick reference, review, and practice.
Complete Word List
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Definitions Match
Write the correct vocabulary word next to each definition.

✅ Answer Key - Exercise 1
1. crusade  2. clarify  3. infinite  4. avid  5. jut
6. decree  7. waft  8. recuperate  9. antics  10. jargon
Exercise 2: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word from the box to complete each sentence.
Word box: engross, stamina, feasible, commotion, ominous, balmy, malleable, scarcity, affluent, teem
The city streets __________ with people during the holiday weekend.

The dark sky looked __________, warning of an approaching storm.

✅ Answer Key - Exercise 2
1. teem  2. engrossed  3. stamina  4. balmy  5. commotion
6. ominous  7. feasible  8. affluent  9. scarcity  10. malleable
Exercise 3: Sentence Writing
Write your own original sentence for each of the following words. Make sure your sentence clearly shows you understand the meaning of the word.
assail

After writing your sentences, check them with your AI tutor to make sure you have used each word correctly.
Exercise 4: Word Categories

Exercise 5: Synonyms and Antonyms

Exercise 6: Crossword Clues
Write the vocabulary word that matches each clue. You can use this as a self-test activity.

End of Vocabulary Reference - Unit 8
tables: A strong effort to achieve something important, often related to a cause or belief.
The students started a crusade to raise money for their school library.
To make something clear or easier to understand.
Can you clarify what you mean by that?
To attack someone or something violently or with strong words.
The pressure assailed everyone during finals week.
Something that has no limits or end; it goes on forever.
The universe is so big that it seems infinite.
To make someone want to do something or work harder.
The teacher tried to motivate the students by giving them fun projects.
The ability to keep going and not get tired easily.
Running every day helped her build her stamina for the marathon.
Strange or mysterious, often in a way that is unsettling.
She had an uncanny ability to guess what people were thinking.
To be full of or to have a lot of something.
The garden was teeming with colourful flowers in the spring.
Connected to the topic or situation being discussed.
The teacher said our homework was relevant to what we were learning.
Able to be shaped or bent easily without breaking.
The metal is malleable, so it can be formed into different shapes.
Very eager or enthusiastic about something.
She is an avid reader who finishes a book every week.
Taking unnecessary risks without thinking about consequences.
It was foolhardy of him to climb without safety gear.
To stick out or extend beyond something else.
The rocks jut out from the cliff, making it look dramatic.
Suggesting that something bad is going to happen.
The dark clouds looked ominous, making us worry about the storm.
Pleasantly warm and comfortable, often describing nice weather.
We enjoyed a balmy evening at the beach.
An official order or decision made by a person in authority.
The king issued a decree that everyone must celebrate the festival.
To move gently through the air, often describing smells or sounds.
The smell of fresh cookies wafted through the kitchen.
To get better after being sick or tired.
After her illness, she needed a few weeks to recuperate.
A quality or ability that a person is born with, not learned.
She has an innate talent for music.
A flat surface where people can receive services or buy things.
I went to the counter to buy a ticket for the movie.
Funny or silly actions or behaviours that people do.
The clown's antics made everyone at the party laugh.
A lot of noise and activity, usually caused by excitement or upset.
There was a commotion in the hallway when the alarm went off.
A person who has a lower position or rank than someone else.
In our project, Sarah is the leader, and I am her subordinate.
Having a lot of money and wealth.
The affluent family lived in a large house with a beautiful garden.
Help or assistance that you give to someone or something.
My friends showed their support by coming to my soccer game.
A short and interesting story about a real event or person.
The teacher shared an anecdote about her first day at school.
A situation where there is not enough of something needed.
During the drought, there was a scarcity of water for the farmers.
Possible to do easily or conveniently.
It is feasible to finish the project by next week if we work together.
To help someone do something wrong or illegal.
He was charged with trying to abet his friend in stealing.
To completely focus on something and not notice anything else.
She was so engrossed in her book that she did not hear her friend.
Special words or expressions used by a particular group.
The teacher used a lot of jargon when talking about science.

paragraphs: Grammar — Unit 9: Music as Universal Language
Year 8 English · Ehel Academy · Seven Grammar Lessons

This file contains seven grammar lessons that support your work in Unit 9. Read one lesson at a time, study the examples, and complete the practice activity at the end of each lesson. Say the example sentences out loud — grammar sticks better when you hear it! Check your answers with the answer key provided, and ask your AI tutor if you need further explanation.
Lesson 1: Present Perfect Continuous
What it means and why it matters

The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or that were happening recently and have just stopped. This tense emphasises the duration and ongoing nature of the action. It is one of the most useful tenses for describing recent experiences, long-running activities, and situations where you can see the visible result of an activity that has been in progress. For example, if your friend's hands are covered in paint, you might say, "You have been painting!" The painting may have stopped, but the evidence is still visible.
How to form it
Structure: Subject + has/have + been + verb(-ing)
Use has been with he, she, it: "She has been mustering the courage to perform."
Use have been with I, you, we, they: "They have been rehearsing since morning."
Negative: Subject + has/have + not + been + verb(-ing)
"She has not been practising enough this week."
"They have not been paying attention during rehearsals."
Question: Has/Have + subject + been + verb(-ing)?
"Has she been mustering the courage to perform?" — "Yes, she has."
"Have they been rehearsing all day?" — "Yes, they have."
When to use it
Use the Present Perfect Continuous for:
Actions still continuing: "I have been studying English for three years." (You started three years ago and you are still studying now.)
Recent actions with visible results: "She has been crying — her eyes are red." (She stopped, but you can see the evidence.)
Duration of ongoing activities: "They have been waiting for twenty minutes." (The waiting started twenty minutes ago and continues.)
Repeated actions over a period: "He has been performing in school plays since Year 6." (He started in Year 6 and still performs now.)
Key time words
The Present Perfect Continuous is often used with:
for + period: "He has been performing for five years."
since + starting point: "We have been rehearsing since Monday."
all day / all morning / all week: "She has been composing music all day."
how long: "How long have you been learning the drums?"
lately / recently: "I have been feeling more confident lately."
Present Perfect Continuous vs Present Perfect Simple

These two tenses are sometimes confused. The key difference is that the Present Perfect Continuous emphasises the process or duration, while the Present Perfect Simple emphasises the result or completion.
"I have been reading this book all week." (Focus on the ongoing process — you are still reading it.)
"I have read three chapters." (Focus on the result — three chapters are finished.)
"She has been practising the piano for two hours." (Focus on the duration.)
"She has practised the piano." (Focus on the fact that the practice happened.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Forgetting "been".
Incorrect: "She has mustering the courage."
Correct: "She has been mustering the courage."
Mistake 2: Wrong form of "have".
Incorrect: "They has been studying."
Correct: "They have been studying."
Mistake 3: Using the base verb instead of -ing form.
Incorrect: "She has been muster the courage."
Correct: "She has been mustering the courage."
Mistake 4: Using the Present Perfect Continuous with stative verbs that do not normally take -ing.
Incorrect: "I have been knowing him for years."
Correct: "I have known him for years." ("Know" is a stative verb — use Present Perfect Simple.)
Quick memory trick

Think of the Present Perfect Continuous as the "how long" tense. If you can ask "How long have you been doing this?" and the answer shows time spent on an ongoing activity, you are using this tense correctly.
Practice
Complete the sentences using the Present Perfect Continuous:
I __________ (play) the guitar for three hours.
He __________ (learn) to dance since last summer.
They __________ (wait) for the bus for twenty minutes.
She __________ (muster) the courage to perform all week.
We __________ (rehearse) this scene since Monday.
How long __________ you __________ (practise) the piano?
Answers: 1. have been playing  2. has been learning  3. have been waiting  4. has been mustering  5. have been rehearsing  6. have ... been practising
Lesson 2: Participle Clauses
What they are and why they matter

A participle clause is a special kind of phrase that helps you combine sentences in a smooth, elegant way. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can join them using a participle to add extra information about the subject. This makes your writing more concise and sophisticated. For example, instead of saying "She was rendered breathless by the performance. She collapsed backstage," you can say, "Rendered breathless, she collapsed backstage." The participle clause saves space and makes your sentence flow better.
Types of participles
Present participles end in -ing:
running, swimming, feeling, performing, mustering
Past participles usually end in -ed (or have irregular forms):
excited, exhausted, rendered, finished, inspired, hidden, torn, written
How to form participle clauses
Identify the main clause: "She collapsed backstage."
Identify the extra information: "She was rendered breathless."
Turn the extra information into a participle clause: "Rendered breathless" (remove "She was").
Combine: "Rendered breathless, she collapsed backstage."
More examples from the story and the unit theme:
"Rendered spotlight-blind, Idris moved through the blocking." (Past participle — describes Idris's state.)
"Feeling tired, he went to bed early." (Present participle — describes the reason.)
"Excited about the showcase, she packed her costume with care." (Past participle — describes the emotion.)
"Having rehearsed all day, they were exhausted by evening." (Perfect participle — shows the earlier action.)
Important rule
The subject of the participle clause must be the same as the subject of the main clause. If they are different, the sentence will sound wrong or be confusing.
Incorrect: "Feeling hungry, the pizza was eaten." (This says the pizza felt hungry!)
Correct: "Feeling hungry, he ate the pizza." (He felt hungry, and he ate.)
Participle clauses for different meanings
Participle clauses can show:
Reason: "Worried about the exam, he studied late into the night." (Why did he study? Because he was worried.)
Time: "Arriving at the theatre, she noticed the long queue." (When did she notice? When she arrived.)
Result: "The power went out, plunging the stage into darkness." (What was the result? Darkness.)
Manner: "He left the stage, bowing to the audience." (How did he leave? Bowing.)
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Mismatched subjects (dangling participle).
Incorrect: "Walking to the theatre, the rain started." (The rain was not walking!)
Correct: "Walking to the theatre, she got caught in the rain."
Mistake 2: Using the wrong participle form.
Incorrect: "She was exciting to see the performance."
Correct: "She was excited to see the performance."
Practice
Combine each pair of sentences using a participle clause:
She was excited by the news. She jumped up and down.
He was tired from running. He sat down on the grass.
She was surprised by the gift. She could not stop smiling.
He was inspired by the story. He began writing his own play.
They were determined to succeed. They rehearsed every evening.

Answers: 1. Excited by the news, she jumped up and down. 2. Tired from running, he sat down on the grass. 3. Surprised by the gift, she could not stop smiling. 4. Inspired by the story, he began writing his own play. 5. Determined to succeed, they rehearsed every evening.
Lesson 3: What Clauses for Exclamations
What they are and why they matter

Exclamatory sentences using "what" are a lively way to express strong feelings or reactions such as excitement, surprise, or admiration. In the story, characters use what clauses at moments of high emotion: "What a formidable concept!" and "What a performance!" Learning to use these structures will make your spoken and written English more expressive and engaging.
How to form what clauses
Structure: What + a/an + adjective + noun + !
Examples:
"What a formidable performance!"
"What an impulsive act!"
"What a wonderful surprise!"
"What an incredible adventure!"
With plural or uncountable nouns (no "a/an"):
"What talented musicians!" (plural)
"What beautiful music!" (uncountable)
"What deft fingers!" (plural)
What vs How
Both "what" and "how" can begin exclamations, but they follow different patterns:
What + adjective + noun: "What a beautiful song!"
How + adjective (no noun): "How beautiful!"
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using "How" with a noun instead of "What".
Incorrect: "How a beautiful day!"
Correct: "What a beautiful day!"
Mistake 2: Forgetting the adjective.
Incorrect: "What dog!"
Correct: "What a cute dog!"
Mistake 3: Using "a" before a plural or uncountable noun.
Incorrect: "What a talented musicians!"
Correct: "What talented musicians!"
Quick memory trick
Think of "What" as a magic word that opens the door to your feelings. Whenever you see or hear something amazing, just remember: "What a..." followed by an adjective and a noun!
Practice
Complete each exclamation using an appropriate adjective and noun:
What a __________ __________! (about a very impressive concert)
What an __________ __________! (about a surprising ending to a play)
What __________ __________! (about a group of very skilled dancers)
What a __________ __________! (about a mask that was very elaborate)
What __________ __________! (about music that is incredibly moving)
Suggested Answers: 1. What a spectacular concert! 2. What an unexpected ending! 3. What skilled dancers! 4. What an elaborate mask! 5. What moving music!
Lesson 4: Polite Question Structures
What they are and why they matter

Polite question structures help you communicate respectfully and considerately. When you ask questions politely, you show that you value the other person's time and feelings. This is especially important in formal situations, when talking to adults, or when meeting people for the first time. In the story, characters use polite questions in several places, such as "Do you know what 'render' really means in theatre terms?" and "Would it be too bold if we invited you to present your play?"
Common polite question structures
1. Could you...? / Can you...?
"Could you tell me where the rehearsal room is?"
"Could you please explain that scene again?"
2. Would you mind...? (+ verb-ing)
"Would you mind passing the script?"
"Would you mind repeating the instructions?"
3. Do you know...? (embedded question — statement word order)
"Do you know what 'meander' means?" (Not: "Do you know what does 'meander' mean?")
"Do you know where the theatre is?" (Not: "Do you know where is the theatre?")
4. Would you be able to...?
"Would you be able to help me with the costumes?"
"Would you be able to attend the rehearsal tomorrow?"
5. I was wondering if...? (very polite / formal)
"I was wondering if you could give me some feedback on my performance."
"I was wondering if the show has already started."
Key rule: embedded question word order
When you embed a question inside a polite phrase, the word order changes to statement order (subject before verb):
Direct question: "What time does the show start?"
Polite version: "Do you know what time the show starts?" (subject + verb, not "does the show start")
Direct question: "Where is the dressing room?"
Polite version: "Could you tell me where the dressing room is?" (not "where is the dressing room")
Adding "please" for extra politeness
You can add "please" to make any polite question even more respectful:
"Could you please help me with my lines?"
"Would you mind please lowering your voice?"
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using question word order inside a polite phrase.
Incorrect: "Do you know what does 'meander' mean?"
Correct: "Do you know what 'meander' means?"
Mistake 2: Forgetting the question mark.
Incorrect: "Could you help me with this."
Correct: "Could you help me with this?"
Practice
Rewrite each direct question as a polite question:
"What does 'sabotage' mean?" (Do you know...)
"Can you help me fix this costume?" (Would you mind...)
"Where is the nearest exit?" (Could you tell me...)
"Will you attend the rehearsal tomorrow?" (Would you be able to...)
"What time does the show start?" (Do you happen to know...)

Answers: 1. Do you know what 'sabotage' means? 2. Would you mind helping me fix this costume? 3. Could you tell me where the nearest exit is? 4. Would you be able to attend the rehearsal tomorrow? 5. Do you happen to know what time the show starts?
Lesson 5: Reported Speech
What it means and why it matters

Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is used to tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. Instead of saying "He said, 'I like music,'" you say "He said that he liked music." This is important because it allows you to share information and conversations smoothly, especially in writing. In the story, you could report what characters said: "Amina told Idris that his story mattered more than a mask."
How to change statements to reported speech
Remove the quotation marks and add that after the reporting verb.
Shift the tense back one step: present simple → past simple, present continuous → past continuous, present perfect → past perfect, past simple → past perfect, will → would, can → could.
Change the pronouns: I → he/she, we → they, my → his/her, our → their.
Change time and place words: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, now → then, here → there, this → that.
Examples:
Direct: Idris said, "The performance caused havoc."
Reported: Idris said that the performance had caused havoc.
Direct: Amina said, "I am tired of giving in."
Reported: Amina said that she was tired of giving in.
Direct: The teacher told the students, "You will perform tomorrow."
Reported: The teacher told the students that they would perform the next day.
Reporting questions
Yes/No questions use if or whether:
Direct: "Did you save the props?"
Reported: He asked if she had saved the props.
Wh- questions keep the question word:
Direct: "Where did you hide the mask?"
Reported: He asked where she had hidden the mask.
Important: In reported questions, use statement word order (subject before verb). Do not use a question mark.
Common reporting verbs
said — neutral: "He said that knowledge was a duty."
told — always followed by a person: "She told the audience that change was possible."
explained — giving reasons: "The teacher explained that the tense showed ongoing action."
announced — public statement: "The judge announced that Nuur Academy had won."
asked — for questions: "She asked if he was ready."
warned — alerting about danger: "She warned that cheating was repugnant."
Common mistakes
Incorrect: "He said that he will come." (Tense not shifted.)
Correct: "He said that he would come."
Incorrect: "She said that I was tired." (Pronoun not changed when reporting someone else's words.)
Correct: "She said that she was tired."
Practice
Change each sentence to reported speech:
Idris said, "I have been directing this play for over a year."
Amina told him, "Do not let this cause havoc in your head."
The judge said, "This is the most original performance we have seen today."
The boy asked Idris, "Will you perform here again?"

Answers: 1. Idris said that he had been directing that play for over a year. 2. Amina told him not to let that cause havoc in his head. 3. The judge said that that was the most original performance they had seen that day. 4. The boy asked Idris if/whether he would perform there again.
Lesson 6: Reflexive Pronouns
What they are and why they matter

Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same person or thing. When someone does something to themselves, we use a reflexive pronoun. This matters because it helps us be clear in our communication. For example, "She expressed herself through the performance" shows that she did the expressing for her own benefit, not someone else.
The reflexive pronouns
How to use reflexive pronouns
Place the reflexive pronoun after the verb or at the end of the sentence:
"She dressed herself in the costume."
"We enjoyed ourselves at the performance."
"He taught himself how to play the guitar."
"They prepared themselves for the big show."
Reflexive pronouns for emphasis:
"Idris himself directed the entire play." (Emphasis: he did it personally.)
"I will do it myself." (Emphasis: without anyone's help.)
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Incorrect: "She enjoyed himself at the party."
Correct: "She enjoyed herself at the party."
Mistake 2: Omitting the reflexive pronoun when needed.
Incorrect: "He taught to play the guitar."
Correct: "He taught himself to play the guitar."
Quick memory trick
Think of reflexive pronouns as a mirror. When you look in a mirror, you see yourself. In the same way, when a reflexive pronoun appears in a sentence, it reflects the action back to the subject.
Practice
Complete each sentence with the correct reflexive pronoun:
She expressed __________ through a gaudy costume.
I taught __________ how to play the oud.
They prepared __________ for the big performance.
He looked at __________ in the mirror before going on stage.
We enjoyed __________ at the drama festival.
You should believe in __________.
Answers: 1. herself  2. myself  3. themselves  4. himself  5. ourselves  6. yourself
Lesson 7: Summary Writing Techniques
What they are and why they matter

Summary writing techniques help you condense information into a shorter form while keeping the main ideas clear. This is an essential skill for academic writing, note-taking, and communication. Whether you are summarising a story, an article, or a conversation, using effective techniques makes your writing more powerful and easier to read. A good summary captures the most important points without unnecessary detail.
Step-by-step guide to writing a summary
Read or listen carefully. Make sure you understand the whole text before you begin summarising.
Identify the key ideas. What are the most important points? Underline or highlight them.
Combine related ideas. Use connecting words (and, but, because, however, therefore) to join related thoughts smoothly.
Remove unnecessary details. Cut examples, repetition, and minor points that do not add to the main message.
Use your own words. Paraphrase rather than copying sentences directly. This shows you truly understand the material.
Keep it concise. A summary should be significantly shorter than the original text.
Review and revise. Read your summary again. Does it make sense? Is it clear? Is anything important missing?
Example: Summarising a passage from the story
Original passage (long):

"After the competition, Nuur Academy was invited to perform the piece again — this time at the Kampala National Theatre. It was a chance to rebuild the show with all the original props and polish every moment. They had only ten days to prepare, and the process was gruelling. Lights had to be adjusted. The musical cues had to be timed perfectly. Even the transitions between scenes were rehearsed until their feet ached."
Summary (short):
"After winning the competition, Nuur Academy was invited to perform at the Kampala National Theatre. They had ten days of intensive preparation to polish the show."
Tips for better summaries
Focus on the who, what, when, where, and why of the text.
Use strong, precise vocabulary instead of vague or wordy phrases.
Avoid copying the writer's exact sentences — paraphrase in your own words.
A summary should usually be about one-quarter to one-third the length of the original.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Including too many details.
Fix: Ask yourself, "Is this detail essential to the main idea?" If not, remove it.
Mistake 2: Using long, complicated sentences.
Fix: Break long sentences into shorter ones for clarity.
Mistake 3: Forgetting connecting words.
Fix: Use "and", "but", "because", "however", and "therefore" to link ideas smoothly.
Practice
Read the following passage and write a summary in two to three sentences:

"At the Johannesburg showcase, Nuur Academy performed before a thousand people. The applause that night was thunderous, but what meant more to Idris was a conversation with a young boy who approached him after the show. 'That play made me cry,' the boy said. 'Because I lie sometimes too. But I do not want to anymore.'"

Suggested Answer: Nuur Academy performed to a large audience in Johannesburg. After the show, a young boy told Idris that the play had made him want to stop lying, which showed the power of honest theatre.

Unit 9: Music as Universal Language
Year 8 English · Ehel Academy · Self-Paced Lesson
🧭 How to Use This Unit
This is your main lesson file. Work through it section by section at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar, and the Story "The Curtain Never Lies".
Start with the Vocabulary file to learn the key words, then come back here for Reading, Grammar, Writing, and Speaking.
Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck or want extra practice. Bring your finished work to your teacher sessions.
Overview

Welcome to Unit 9, the final unit of Year 8. This unit is called "Music as Universal Language" because you will explore how music, performance, and the creative arts bring people together across cultures, languages, and backgrounds. Music is one of the most powerful forms of human expression. It can communicate emotions that words alone cannot capture. A song can make you feel joy, sorrow, excitement, or peace without you understanding a single word of the language in which it is sung. That is why we call music a universal language: it speaks to everyone.

In this unit, you will read a story called "The Curtain Never Lies" about a group of young East African students who face a dramatic challenge during a regional performing arts competition. Through their experience, you will explore themes of creativity, integrity, resilience, and the power of honest artistic expression. You will also study the Present Perfect Continuous tense, which allows you to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening or have only recently stopped. You will learn about participle clauses for more sophisticated sentence structures, what clauses for expressing strong feelings, and polite question structures for respectful communication.

Throughout this unit, you will develop your reading comprehension, writing, speaking, and listening skills through a range of activities connected to the theme of music and performance. By the end of this unit, you will be able to write confidently about ongoing actions and recent experiences, express strong emotions using exclamatory sentences, and communicate politely and clearly in formal and informal situations. These are skills that will serve you well in Year 9 and beyond.

In Islam, the arts and creativity hold an important place. Allah is Al-Musawwir, the Fashioner, and He has given human beings the ability to create, to express, and to communicate through beautiful forms. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said that Allah is beautiful and loves beauty. When we use our creative talents honestly and with good intention, we honour the gifts that Allah has given us. This unit will help you think about what it means to be an honest artist, a supportive teammate, and a person of integrity, whether you are on a stage or in everyday life.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what it means to call music a universal language and discuss how the performing arts connect people across cultures.
Read the story "The Curtain Never Lies" and answer comprehension and analysis questions using evidence from the text.
Use thirty key vocabulary words related to performance, creativity, emotion, and integrity in your own speaking and writing.
Form and use the Present Perfect Continuous tense correctly to describe actions that started in the past and are still continuing or have recently stopped.
Identify and create participle clauses to combine sentences and add detail in a more sophisticated way.
Use what clauses to form exclamatory sentences that express strong feelings or reactions.
Ask polite questions using appropriate structures such as "Could you...?", "Would you mind...?", and "Do you know...?"
Use reported speech to convey what someone else said without quoting their exact words.
Use reflexive pronouns correctly when the subject and object of a sentence are the same person.
Write a performance review of a play, concert, or artistic event using appropriate vocabulary and grammar.
Write a persuasive speech about the importance of the arts in education or community life.
Take part in a role-play interview with a performer, a group discussion about music and culture, and a dramatic reading of a scene.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment statements and set goals for further improvement.
Vocabulary Preview

The words below are the most important new vocabulary for this unit. You will meet them throughout the story, the grammar exercises, and the writing activities. Read each definition carefully and study the example sentence. Try to use at least five of these words in your own writing this week. Your full vocabulary companion file contains all thirty unit words with extended practice activities, so make sure you study that file as well.
📖 Did You Know?
The word 'music' comes from the ancient Greek word 'mousike', which originally
meant 'art of the Muses'. The Muses were goddesses of inspiration in Greek
mythology. In many cultures around the world, music and poetry are considered
divine gifts. The Arabic word for music, 'musiqa', shares this same root.
Grammar Introduction: Present Perfect Continuous

The main grammar focus of this unit is the Present Perfect Continuous tense. This tense is used to talk about actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or that were happening recently and have just stopped. It emphasises the duration or ongoing nature of an activity, and it is one of the most useful tenses for describing experiences and recent events.
How to Form It
Structure: Subject + has/have + been + verb(-ing)
Use has been with he, she, it: "She has been practising the piano for two hours."
Use have been with I, you, we, they: "They have been rehearsing since morning."
When to Use It
Use the Present Perfect Continuous when you want to show:
An action that started in the past and is still continuing: "I have been studying English for three years." (You started three years ago and you are still studying.)
An action that recently stopped but has a visible result: "She has been crying — her eyes are red." (She stopped crying, but you can see the result.)
Duration of an ongoing activity: "They have been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes." (The waiting started twenty minutes ago and continues now.)
Key Time Words
The Present Perfect Continuous is often used with time words that show duration:
for + period of time: "He has been performing for five years."
since + starting point: "We have been rehearsing since Monday."
all day / all morning / all week: "She has been composing music all day."
how long: "How long have you been learning to play the drums?"
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Mistake 1: Forgetting to include "been".
Incorrect: "She has practising the guitar."
Correct: "She has been practising the guitar."
Mistake 2: Using the wrong form of "have".
Incorrect: "He have been singing all morning."
Correct: "He has been singing all morning."
Mistake 3: Using the base form instead of the -ing form.
Incorrect: "They have been practice every day."
Correct: "They have been practising every day."

Your full Grammar companion file contains detailed lessons on the Present Perfect Continuous and all the other grammar points for this unit, with many more examples and practice exercises. Make sure you study that file carefully.

Activity 1: Role-Play Interview with a Performer

In this activity, one person plays the role of a journalist and the other plays the role of a young performer who has just won a competition. How to prepare:

The journalist should prepare at least five questions. Use polite question structures: "Could you tell us about...?", "Would you mind explaining...?", "Do you know what inspired...?"
The performer should prepare answers that include Present Perfect Continuous: "I have been rehearsing for months", "We have been working on this play since September."

Activity 2: Group Discussion — Music Across Cultures

After your dramatic reading, ask your audience (or your AI tutor) for feedback. What was the strongest moment? Which emotions came through most clearly? What could you improve?

Writing Workshop

This unit includes two writing projects. The first asks you to write a performance review of a play, concert, or artistic event. The second asks you to write a persuasive speech about the importance of the arts. Both projects will help you develop your ability to write clearly, use the grammar you have learned, and express your ideas with confidence.
Project A: Write a Performance Review

A performance review is a piece of writing that describes and evaluates a live performance such as a play, concert, dance, or poetry reading. Good reviews describe what happened, explain what was effective, and offer a balanced opinion. Your task is to write a review of a performance, either real or imagined. You may review a performance you have actually attended, or you may write a review of Nuur Academy's performance from the story. The review should be between 250 and 350 words.
Performance review structure:
Introduction: State what the performance was, who performed it, where and when it took place, and give your overall impression in one sentence.
Summary of the performance: Briefly describe what happened on stage. Do not retell the entire plot; focus on the key moments.
What worked well: Describe the strongest aspects of the performance. Was the acting convincing? Was the music moving? Were the costumes and set design effective? Use specific examples.
What could be improved: Offer constructive suggestions. Was anything unclear? Did the pacing feel uneven? Were there moments that lost the audience's attention?
Conclusion: Give your final opinion and say whether you would recommend the performance to others. End with a memorable final sentence.
Before you submit, check your review against this checklist:
My review has a clear introduction, body paragraphs, and a conclusion.
I have used at least five vocabulary words from this unit.
I have used the Present Perfect Continuous at least once (for example, "The cast has been rehearsing for months").
I have used at least one participle clause (for example, "Rendered speechless by the finale, the audience rose to their feet").
I have used at least one what clause for exclamation (for example, "What a formidable performance!").
My language is balanced — I describe both strengths and areas for improvement.
My review is between 250 and 350 words.
I have checked my spelling, punctuation, and grammar.
Project B: Write a Persuasive Speech

Imagine you have been asked to give a speech at your school assembly about why the performing arts (music, drama, dance, poetry) should be valued and supported in education. Your task is to write a persuasive speech of 200 to 300 words that argues for the importance of the arts. You should use facts, examples, and emotional appeal to convince your audience.
Persuasive speech structure:
Opening hook: Start with a question, a surprising fact, or a short anecdote that grabs the audience's attention.
Thesis statement: Clearly state your main argument in one sentence.
Argument 1 with evidence: Present your first reason and support it with an example or fact.
Argument 2 with evidence: Present your second reason and support it.
Argument 3 with evidence: Present your third reason and support it.
Counter-argument and rebuttal: Briefly mention what someone who disagrees might say, then explain why your position is still stronger.
Conclusion: Summarise your main points and end with a powerful call to action.
Useful persuasive phrases:
"I firmly believe that..."
"The evidence clearly shows that..."
"It is undeniable that..."
"Some may argue that..., but in reality..."
"If we neglect the arts, we risk losing..."
"I urge you all to..."
Before you submit, check your speech against this checklist:
My speech has a clear opening, body, and conclusion.
I have presented at least three arguments with supporting evidence.
I have addressed at least one counter-argument.
I have used at least three vocabulary words from this unit.
I have used at least one polite question to engage the audience.
My speech is between 200 and 300 words.
I have checked my spelling, punctuation, and grammar.

Exercise 1: Present Perfect Continuous

Self-Study Tips
Learning English effectively on your own requires good habits and the right strategies. Here are some tips to help you make the most of this unit and continue improving your English skills every day.
Tip 1: Read Aloud Every Day

Reading aloud is one of the best ways to improve your pronunciation, fluency, and confidence. Choose a paragraph from the story "The Curtain Never Lies" and read it aloud three times. The first time, focus on pronouncing every word clearly. The second time, focus on reading with expression and emotion. The third time, try to read at a natural speed, as if you are telling the story to a friend. You can also record yourself and listen back to identify areas for improvement.
Tip 2: Keep a Vocabulary Journal

Write each new vocabulary word in a dedicated journal or notebook. For every word, include the definition, an example sentence from the unit, and your own original sentence. Review your journal regularly. Try to use at least two new words in conversation each day. The more you use a word, the more naturally it will come to you when you need it.
Tip 3: Practise Grammar in Context

Instead of just memorising grammar rules, practise them by writing sentences about your own life. For example, write five sentences using the Present Perfect Continuous about things you have been doing recently: "I have been reading a new book this week." "My sister has been learning to cook." "We have been preparing for exams since April." This makes the grammar feel real and personal, which helps you remember it.
Tip 4: Use Your AI Tutor Regularly

Your AI tutor is available 24 hours a day, seven days a week. Do not be afraid to ask it questions, request extra practice, or have conversations in English. You can ask it to check your writing, explain grammar rules, give you vocabulary quizzes, or even role-play scenarios with you. The more you practise with your AI tutor, the faster you will improve.
Tip 5: Listen to Music in English

Since this unit is about music as a universal language, why not practise your English by listening to songs? Find songs with clear lyrics and try to understand the words. Write down any new vocabulary you hear and look up the meanings. Notice the grammar structures used in the lyrics. This is a fun and effective way to improve your listening skills and expand your vocabulary at the same time.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you suggest some activities I can do at
home to practise my English every day? I want to improve my speaking
and writing skills before I start Year 9.'
Suggested Daily Routine

Spreading your study across the week is more effective than trying to do everything in one session. Here is a suggested daily routine for working through this unit. Adapt it to suit your own schedule and pace.
Day 1: Vocabulary

Open your Vocabulary companion file and study Group 1 words. Read each definition and example sentence out loud. Write three original sentences using words from the group. Test yourself by covering the definitions and trying to remember them.
Day 2: Vocabulary and Reading

Study Group 2 vocabulary words using the same method as Day 1. Then begin reading "The Curtain Never Lies" in your Story companion file. Read the first half of the story slowly and carefully, noting any vocabulary words you recognise.
Day 3: Reading and Comprehension
Finish reading the story. Then return to this Lesson file and answer the first six comprehension questions. Write your answers in complete sentences with evidence from the text.
Day 4: Grammar

Open your Grammar companion file and study Lesson 1: Present Perfect Continuous. Read the explanation carefully, study the examples, and complete the practice activity. Then study Lesson 2: Participle Clauses using the same approach.
Day 5: Grammar and Exercises

Study Lesson 3: What Clauses and Lesson 4: Polite Questions in your Grammar file. Then return to this Lesson file and complete Exercises 1 through 4 in the Activities and Exercises section. Check your answers against the key.
Day 6: Writing

Choose either Project A (Performance Review) or Project B (Persuasive Speech) from the Writing Workshop section and write your first draft. Use the structure guide and checklist provided. Ask your AI tutor for feedback on your draft.
Day 7: Speaking and Review

Complete one of the speaking activities from this lesson. Then review all your vocabulary words using the Word Wall in your Vocabulary file. Finish any remaining comprehension questions or exercises. Read through the Self-Assessment section and identify any areas where you need more practice.
📅 Flexible Pacing
This daily routine is a suggestion, not a strict rule. You know yourself best.
If you need more time on vocabulary, take two days instead of one. If grammar
comes easily to you, move faster through those sections. The important thing is
to study regularly and not leave everything to the last day.
Self-Assessment

You have now worked through the whole of Unit 9. Well done! This is the final unit of Year 8, so completing it is a significant achievement. Before you finish, take a few minutes to read each statement below and think honestly about whether you can do it confidently. Place a tick next to each statement you feel sure about, and a question mark next to any that you would like to practise more. There are no wrong answers here. This is a tool to help you understand your own progress and set goals for improvement as you move into Year 9.
I can...
Explain what it means to call music a universal language and give examples of how performance connects people.
Read "The Curtain Never Lies" and answer comprehension questions using evidence from the text.
Use at least twenty vocabulary words from this unit correctly in my own sentences.
Form and use the Present Perfect Continuous tense to describe ongoing or recently completed actions.
Create participle clauses to combine sentences and add detail in a more sophisticated way.
Write exclamatory sentences using what clauses to express strong feelings.
Ask polite questions using "Could you...?", "Would you mind...?", and "Do you know...?"
Use reported speech to describe what someone else said, changing tense, pronouns, and time words correctly.
Use reflexive pronouns correctly when the subject and object are the same person.
Write a performance review with a clear structure, balanced evaluation, and appropriate vocabulary.
Write a persuasive speech with a clear argument, supporting evidence, and a strong conclusion.
Take part in a role-play interview, a group discussion, and a dramatic reading using appropriate language.
Use summary writing techniques to condense information into clear, concise sentences.
Reflect on my own learning, identify my strengths, and set goals for areas where I need more practice.

If you ticked most of these statements, you have a strong understanding of the language skills covered in this unit. Excellent work! You are well prepared for Year 9. Challenge yourself further by writing a longer piece about the role of music in your community, or by researching a famous musician or performer from East Africa and writing a biography. If you placed a question mark next to several statements, do not worry. Go back and review the relevant section of this lesson, try the exercises again, or ask your AI tutor for extra practice activities. Remember that learning is a journey, and the fact that you have completed Year 8 shows how far you have come.
